- **Complementary fringes:** Whatever is bright in reflection is dark in transmission, and vice versa.
- **Newton's rings (reflected):** Dark centre ($n = 0$), bright rings at $D_n^2 = (2n-1) \cdot 2\lambda R$ for $n = 1, 2, \dots$ (or $D_n^2 = 4n\lambda R$ for the dark rings).
- **Wedge film fringe width:** $\beta = \lambda/(2\mu\theta)$ for small wedge angle $\theta$.
- **Units to remember:** $\lambda$ in metres (or nm), $t$ in metres, $\mu$ dimensionless, $\theta$ in radians.

> **One-line memory aid:** "**In reflection, an ODD multiple of $\lambda/2$ makes it BRIGHT; an EVEN multiple makes it DARK.**" This is because of the extra $\pi$ shift that effectively "shifts" the path difference by half a wavelength.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colours in Thin Films
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Colours in Thin Films

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thin Film Interference (Colours in Thin Films):** The phenomenon of **selective reinforcement and cancellation of certain wavelengths** of light caused by the superposition of light waves reflected from the **upper and lower surfaces** of a thin transparent film (such as a soap film, oil film on water, or oxide layer on metal) is called thin film interference. The brilliant colours observed in such films are a direct consequence of **path difference** and **phase change upon reflection**.

A **thin film** is defined as a transparent medium whose thickness is comparable to the **wavelength of light** (typically of the order of $10^{-6}$ m to $10^{-9}$ m). For the interference effect to be observable, the film thickness must be on the order of a few wavelengths of the incident light, otherwise the path difference between the reflected rays becomes too small to produce observable fringes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Oil Slick on a Wet Road

Imagine pouring a thin layer of oil onto a puddle of water. As you look down at the puddle, you see swirling rainbows of colour — purples, blues, greens, oranges. **Why does this happen?**

Think of light as a wave, like a row of ocean waves hitting a seawall. When the light hits the **top of the oil layer**, some of it bounces back (Ray 1). But a small portion **enters the oil**, travels to the **bottom of the oil layer** (touching the water), and then bounces back up through the oil (Ray 2). When Ray 1 and Ray 2 meet again above the surface, they have travelled **different distances** and may have flipped in phase (because the second ray reflected off a denser medium). 

- If the two waves **arrive in step (crest-to-crest)**, the colour is **bright and reinforced**.
- If they **arrive out of step (crest-to-trough)**, the colour is **cancelled (dark)**.

Since the film is not perfectly flat — it has regions of slightly different thickness — different colours (wavelengths) are reinforced at different spots, producing the beautiful rainbow pattern.

### 1.3 Real-World Examples

| Phenomenon | Description |
|---|---|
| Soap bubbles | Colours change as film drains and thins under gravity |
| Oil film on water | Spectral colours due to varying thickness |
| Peacock feathers | Thin-film-like microstructure creates iridescent colours |
| Butterfly wings | Interference from microscopic thin layers |
| Anti-reflection coating on lenses | Destructive interference reduces unwanted reflections |
| Oxide layers on titanium/steel | Brilliant blue, gold, or purple colours |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Highlighted)

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- **Refractive index of typical soap solution:** $\mu \approx 1.33$
- **Refractive index of typical oil film:** $\mu \approx 1.40$
- **Visible wavelength range:** $\lambda \approx 400$ nm to **700 nm**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** This topic is a direct application of the principle of **superposition of waves** and is closely tied to **Newton's Rings** and **wedge-shaped films**. KTU examiners frequently test the *conditions of maxima and minima* and the *role of phase change* in thin-film problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theory of Interference in Thin Films — Mechanism

Consider a thin transparent film of uniform thickness $t$ and refractive index $\mu$, surrounded by air (refractive index $\approx 1$). A ray of monochromatic light AB is incident on the upper surface PQ of the film at an angle of incidence $i$.

The following sequence occurs at the surfaces of the film:

1. **At point B (upper surface):**
   - A part of the incident ray is **reflected** along BR (this is Ray 1).
   - The remaining part is **refracted** into the film along BC.

2. **At point C (lower surface):**
   - A part of the ray inside the film is **reflected** back into the film along CD.
   - The remaining part is transmitted out of the film (neglected in this analysis).

3. **At point D (emerging back into air):**
   - The ray CD is **refracted** along DE, becoming parallel to BR.

4. **At point E (just outside):**
   - Rays BR and DE are the two **coherent rays** derived from the same parent ray AB. They can therefore **interfere**.

### 2.2 Phase Change on Reflection — The Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fresnel's Principle of Phase Change:** Whenever light is reflected from the surface of a **denser medium (higher refractive index)**, it undergoes an **additional phase change of $\pi$ radians** (equivalent to a path difference of $\lambda/2$). No such phase change occurs when light reflects from a rarer medium.

In a typical thin film (e.g., soap film in air):
- Ray 1 (BR) reflects from a **denser medium** $\Rightarrow$ **phase change of $\pi$**
- Ray 2 (DE) reflects from a **denser medium** (inside the film, reflecting from the lower boundary which is air below — wait, this needs careful analysis)

**Careful analysis for a film of higher refractive index than both surrounding media (e.g., oil film surrounded by air and water, or soap film in air):**
- Ray 1 reflects at upper surface (denser medium below) $\Rightarrow$ phase change of $\pi$
- Ray 2 reflects at lower surface (denser medium above the lower surface — i.e., it goes from oil to water or air, which is rarer) $\Rightarrow$ **NO phase change**

In this case, there is a **net phase difference of $\pi$** between the two reflected rays due to reflection alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** If the film is sandwiched between two media both of *lower* refractive index (e.g., oil film on water where water $\mu \approx 1.33$ and oil $\mu \approx 1.40$), the ray inside the film reflects from a **rarer** medium (water) at the bottom, so **no extra phase change** occurs. This is critical in determining whether maxima or minima correspond to which condition.

### 2.3 Optical Path Difference — Derivation Concept

The **geometric path difference** between Ray 1 (BR) and Ray 2 (DE) is obtained by drawing a perpendicular from B to DE. Let this geometric path difference be denoted as $\Delta_{geo}$.

The **optical path difference** (OPD) is:
$$\Delta = \mu \cdot (\text{geometric path inside the film})$$

For a film of thickness $t$, with light incident at angle $i$ and refracted at angle $r$ (Snell's law: $\sin i = \mu \sin r$), the optical path difference between Ray 2 and Ray 1 is:

$$\Delta = 2\mu t \cos r$$

For **normal incidence** ($i = 0$, hence $r = 0$, $\cos r = 1$):
$$\Delta = 2\mu t$$

### 2.4 Effective Path Difference — Accounting for Phase Change

Since Ray 1 (reflected from top) has an extra phase change of $\pi$ (i.e., an extra path of $\lambda/2$) when the film has higher refractive index than both surrounding media, the **effective optical path difference** becomes:

$$\Delta_{eff} = 2\mu t \cos r - \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

Or, equivalently, for normal incidence:
$$\Delta_{eff} = 2\mu t - \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

### 2.5 Conditions for Maxima and Minima

Using the effective path difference, the interference conditions are:

**Case 1: Film surrounded by rarer media on both sides** (e.g., soap film in air, oil film on water with $\mu_{oil} > \mu_{water}$):

| Phenomenon | Path Condition | Equivalent Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Constructive Interference (Bright fringe / Maxima)** | $\Delta_{eff} = n\lambda$ | $2\mu t \cos r = (2n \pm 1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$ |
| **Destructive Interference (Dark fringe / Minima)** | $\Delta_{eff} = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$ | $2\mu t \cos r = n\lambda$ |

where $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ is the **order of interference**.

**Case 2: Film surrounded by denser media on one side and rarer on the other** (e.g., air film between two glass plates — as in Newton's rings): the conditions reverse.

### 2.6 Why Colours Appear

White light contains a continuous spectrum of wavelengths from approximately **400 nm (violet)** to **700 nm (red)**. For a given film thickness $t$, the condition for constructive interference:
$$2\mu t = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$$
will be satisfied for a *specific wavelength* $\lambda$ at a specific location. Since the film is not perfectly uniform in thickness, different locations satisfy this condition for different wavelengths. The colour observed at any point is the one whose wavelength is constructively reinforced at that point. Thus, the film appears **coloured**, with the colour varying from point to point based on the local thickness.

As the film thins (e.g., soap bubble draining), the colours change dynamically — this is why soap bubbles show constantly shifting rainbow patterns.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU thin-film problem:**

| S.No. | Formula | Meaning / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $2\mu t \cos r$ | Optical path difference (without phase correction) | metres (m) |
| 2 | $2\mu t \cos r - \frac{\lambda}{2}$ | Effective OPD (film denser than surrounding media) | m |
| 3 | $2\mu t = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$ | **Bright fringe (reflected light)**, film in rarer medium | m |
| 4 | $2\mu t = n\lambda$ | **Dark fringe (reflected light)**, film in rarer medium | m |
| 5 | $2\mu t = n\lambda$ | **Bright fringe (transmitted light)**, film in rarer medium | m |
| 6 | $2\mu t = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$ | **Dark fringe (transmitted light)**, film in rarer medium | m |
| 7 | $t = \frac{\lambda}{4\mu}$ | Minimum thickness for **anti-reflection coating** (Destructive in reflected light) | m |
| 8 | $t = \frac{\lambda}{2\mu}$ | Minimum thickness for **maximum reflection** (Constructive in reflected light) | m |
| 9 | $v = \frac{c}{\mu}$ | Speed of light inside the film | m/s |
| 10 | $\sin i = \mu \sin r$ | Snell's law (for oblique incidence) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The principle of thin-film interference is exploited in the manufacture of **anti-reflection coatings** (e.g., $\text{MgF}_2$ coating on camera lenses), **dichroic filters**, **high-reflectance dielectric mirrors** in lasers, and **optical instruments** used in spectroscopy and astronomy. The condition $t = \frac{\lambda}{4\mu}$ for destructive interference in reflected light is the foundational design equation for these coatings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Optical Path Difference in a Thin Film

**Setup:** A thin film of refractive index $\mu$ and uniform thickness $t$ is surrounded by air. A ray AB of monochromatic light is incident on the upper surface PQ at an angle of incidence $i$. The ray refracts into the film at angle $r$ (Snell's law applies).

**Step 1: Trace the rays**
- At point B, the incident ray AB splits:
  - **Ray 1:** Reflected along BR.
  - **Refracted ray:** Enters the film and travels to C on the lower surface.
- At C, the refracted ray splits:
  - **Ray 2:** Reflects back into the film along CD.
  - A transmitted portion (neglected).
- At D, Ray 2 refracts out of the film along DE, parallel to BR.

**Step 2: Construct the perpendicular for path difference**
Drop a perpendicular BM from B onto the ray DE. Then:
- The point M corresponds to a position such that the path from M to E is **equal in length** to the path from B to M, both being in the same medium (air).
- Therefore, the path difference between Ray 2 and Ray 1, after point M, is zero.

The **geometric path difference** between the two reflected rays (DE and BR) is:
$$\text{Path difference} = \text{Path inside the film} - \text{Path of Ray 1 above M}$$

**Step 3: Express the path inside the film**

The refracted ray travels from B to C inside the film. By Snell's law, $BC = \frac{t}{\cos r}$.

Similarly, $CD = \frac{t}{\cos r}$ (by symmetry of the geometry).

Total path inside the film = $BC + CD = \frac{2t}{\cos r}$

**Step 4: Geometric path difference**

From the geometry, with M being the foot of the perpendicular from B to DE:
$$\text{Path of Ray 1} = BM$$
$$\text{Path of Ray 2} = BC + CD$$
$$BM = BC \sin i = \frac{t}{\cos r} \sin i$$

(Using the geometry: $\angle MBC$ in the triangle has the appropriate angles.)

Therefore:
$$\text{Geometric path difference} = BC + CD - BM = \frac{2t}{\cos r} - \frac{t \sin i}{\cos r} = \frac{t}{\cos r}(2 - \sin i)$$

**Step 5: Apply Snell's Law to simplify**

Using Snell's law: $\sin i = \mu \sin r$. Substituting:

$$\text{Path difference} = \frac{t}{\cos r}(2 - \mu \sin r) = \frac{2t}{\cos r} - \frac{\mu t \sin r}{\cos r} = \frac{2t}{\cos r} - \mu t \tan r$$

**Step 6: Convert to optical path difference**

The optical path difference (OPD) is the geometric path difference multiplied by the refractive index of the medium the ray travels in:
- Path inside the film is in medium of refractive index $\mu$.
- Path above M is in air ($\mu = 1$).

$$\Delta = \mu \cdot \frac{2t}{\cos r} - 1 \cdot BM_{\text{air}}$$

A cleaner derivation (using standard text-book geometry) gives the elegant result:

$$\boxed{\Delta = 2\mu t \cos r}$$

**Step 7: Special case — normal incidence**

For $i = 0$, $r = 0$, $\cos r = 1$:

$$\Delta = 2\mu t$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Conditions for Maxima and Minima (with Phase Change)

**Given:** A film with higher refractive index than both surrounding media (e.g., soap film in air). Reflection at the top surface (denser) introduces a phase change of $\pi$ — equivalent to a path difference of $\lambda/2$. Reflection at the bottom surface is from a rarer medium (air below the film) — no extra phase change.

**Effective optical path difference:**
$$\Delta_{eff} = 2\mu t \cos r - \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Condition for Constructive Interference (Bright fringe):**

The effective path difference must be an **integer multiple of $\lambda$**:

$$2\mu t \cos r - \frac{\lambda}{2} = n\lambda, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

Rearranging:
$$2\mu t \cos r = n\lambda + \frac{\lambda}{2} = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$$

$$\boxed{2\mu t \cos r = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2} \quad \text{— Condition for Bright Fringe (Reflected Light)}}$$

**Condition for Destructive Interference (Dark fringe):**

The effective path difference must be an **odd multiple of $\lambda/2$**:

$$2\mu t \cos r - \frac{\lambda}{2} = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

Rearranging:
$$2\mu t \cos r = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2} + \frac{\lambda}{2} = (n+1)\lambda$$

Since $(n+1)$ is also an integer, we can write:

$$\boxed{2\mu t \cos r = n\lambda \quad \text{— Condition for Dark Fringe (Reflected Light)}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Solving a Standard Numerical Problem

**Problem (Model):** A soap film of refractive index $\mu = 1.33$ is illuminated normally with light of wavelength $\lambda = 589.3$ nm. Find the **minimum thickness** of the film for it to appear:
(a) **Bright** in reflected light
(b) **Dark** in reflected light

**Given:** $\mu = 1.33$, $\lambda = 589.3$ nm $= 589.3 \times 10^{-9}$ m, normal incidence ($\cos r = 1$).

**Part (a): Minimum thickness for Bright (Reflected Light)**

Using the bright condition:
$$2\mu t = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$$

For **minimum thickness**, take the smallest non-trivial value of $n$. Since $n = 0$ gives $t = \frac{\lambda}{4\mu}$, this is positive and acceptable:

$$t_{min, bright} = \frac{\lambda}{4\mu}$$

Substituting:
$$t_{min, bright} = \frac{589.3 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 1.33}$$

$$t_{min, bright} = \frac{589.3 \times 10^{-9}}{5.32}$$

$$t_{min, bright} = 110.77 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t_{min, bright} \approx 110.8 \text{ nm}}$$

**Part (b): Minimum thickness for Dark (Reflected Light)**

Using the dark condition:
$$2\mu t = n\lambda$$

For **minimum non-zero thickness**, take $n = 1$ (since $n = 0$ gives $t = 0$, which is trivial):

$$t_{min, dark} = \frac{\lambda}{2\mu}$$

Substituting:
$$t_{min, dark} = \frac{589.3 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 1.33}$$

$$t_{min, dark} = \frac{589.3 \times 10^{-9}}{2.66}$$

$$t_{min, dark} = 221.58 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{t_{min, dark} \approx 221.6 \text{ nm}}$$

### 3.4 Why the Colours Change with Time in a Soap Bubble

As a soap bubble drains, its film thickness $t$ decreases with time. For a fixed observation point, as $t$ changes:

$$\lambda_{reinforced} = \frac{4\mu t}{2n+1}$$

Each colour $\lambda$ gets reinforced for a specific $t$. As $t$ decreases, the reinforced wavelength shifts:

- For **larger $t$** (early in life of bubble): **longer wavelengths (red, orange)** are reinforced.
- For **smaller $t$** (later, when bubble is about to burst): **shorter wavelengths (violet, blue)** are reinforced.
- Just before the film breaks ($t \to 0$): no wavelength is reinforced — the film appears **black** (or completely dark in reflected light, since $2\mu t \to 0$, which is a dark condition).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Why does a soap film appear black just before it bursts?" — The answer is that the film becomes so thin that $2\mu t \ll \lambda/2$ for all visible wavelengths, so the dark-fringe condition is satisfied for all wavelengths simultaneously, and the reflected light vanishes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Thin-Film Interference

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Incident White Light Ray] --> nodeB[Strikes Upper Surface of Thin Film]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Splitting into Two Coherent Components]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Ray 1 - Reflected from Top Surface]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Ray 2 - Refracted and Reflected from Bottom]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Phase Change of PI - Denser Medium]
    nodeE --> nodeG[No Phase Change - Rarer Medium Below]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Superposition in Air Above Film]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI{Path Difference Delta}
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Delta = 2 mu t cos r minus lambda over 2]
    nodeI --> nodeK{Evaluate Interference Condition}
    nodeK --> nodeL[Delta = n lambda - DESTRUCTIVE - DARK]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Delta equals 2n+1 times lambda over 2 - CONSTRUCTIVE - BRIGHT]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Specific Wavelength Cancelled - Complementary Colour Seen]
    nodeM --> nodeO[Specific Wavelength Reinforced - Pure Colour Seen]
    nodeN --> nodeP[Film Appears Coloured - White Minus Cancelled Wavelength]
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Applications: Soap Bubbles, Oil Films, Coatings]
```

### 4.2 Ray Diagram — Path of Light in a Thin Film

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR[Incident Ray AB] --> nodeS[Point B on Top Surface]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Reflected Ray BR - Ray 1]
    nodeS --> nodeU[Refracted Ray BC inside film]
    nodeU --> nodeV[Point C on Bottom Surface]
    nodeV --> nodeW[Reflected Ray CD inside film]
    nodeV --> nodeX[Transmitted Ray - Lost to Analysis]
    nodeW --> nodeY[Point D - Refraction Out]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[Emerging Ray DE - Ray 2 - Parallel to BR]
    nodeT --> nodeAA[Superposition Region in Air]
    nodeZ --> nodeAA
    nodeAA --> nodeBB[Observer Sees Interference Pattern]
    nodeBB --> nodeCC{Constructive or Destructive}
    nodeCC --> nodeDD[BRIGHT - Coloured - If Delta equals 2n+1 times lambda over 2]
    nodeCC --> nodeEE[DARK - If Delta equals n lambda]
```

### 4.3 Logic Flow for Solving Thin-Film Problems

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start: Given Wavelength mu and t] --> check[Check Film Type and Surrounding Media]
    check --> case1[Film Higher mu than Surroundings]
    check --> case2[Film Lower mu than Surroundings]
    case1 --> path1[Path Difference Delta equals 2 mu t cos r]
    case2 --> path2[Path Difference Delta equals 2 mu t cos r]
    path1 --> cond1{Is Phase Change PI Applicable?}
    path2 --> cond2{Is Phase Change PI Applicable?}
    cond1 --> yes1[Yes - Add lambda over 2 Correction]
    cond1 --> no1[No - No Correction]
    cond2 --> yes2[Yes - Add lambda over 2 Correction]
    cond2 --> no2[No - No Correction]
    yes1 --> bright1[Bright Condition: 2 mu t cos r equals 2n+1 times lambda over 2]
    no1 --> bright3[Bright Condition: 2 mu t cos r equals n lambda]
    yes2 --> bright2[Bright Condition: 2 mu t cos r equals n lambda]
    no2 --> bright4[Bright Condition: 2 mu t cos r equals 2n+1 times lambda over 2]
    bright1 --> solve1[Solve for Required t or lambda]
    bright2 --> solve1
    bright3 --> solve1
    bright4 --> solve1
    solve1 --> result[Compute Numerical Answer with Units]
    result --> sanity[Sanity Check: Is t Comparable to Wavelength]
    sanity --> done[Final Answer with Units in nm or micrometres]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of an Anti-Reflection Coating (Engineering Application)

```mermaid
graph TD
    inc[Incident Light from Air mu equals 1] --> coat[Layer of MgF2 mu equals 1.38]
    coat --> subs[Substrate Glass mu equals 1.5]
    coat --> ref1[Reflection 1 at Air-MgF2 Interface - Phase Change PI]
    subs --> ref2[Reflection 2 at MgF2-Glass Interface - Phase Change PI]
    ref1 --> sup[Both Rays Have Phase Change PI - They Cancel Out]
    ref2 --> sup
    sup --> thick[Design: t equals lambda over 4 mu to Ensure OPD equals lambda over 2]
    thick --> result[Destructive Interference in Reflected Light]
    result --> app[Lens Surface Appears Non-Reflective - More Light Enters the System]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangle (node) represents a physical step or decision. Arrows indicate the direction of propagation of the light or the logical flow of analysis. Use these as a **mental checklist** when solving KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style, Dec 2024]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** *What is thin film interference? Why do soap bubbles and oil films on water show beautiful colours?*

**Model Answer:**

Thin film interference is the optical phenomenon in which light waves reflected from the upper and lower surfaces of a thin transparent film (with thickness comparable to the wavelength of light) superpose to produce regions of constructive and destructive interference. When white light falls on such a film, the path difference $2\mu t \cos r$ differs from point to point (because $t$ varies across the film) and from wavelength to wavelength. At each point, only those wavelengths satisfying the constructive condition $2\mu t \cos r = (2n+1)\lambda/2$ are strongly reflected, while other wavelengths are cancelled. Since white light contains all colours, the film reflects a **mixture of surviving colours** at every point, and this mixture changes with position, producing the brilliant rainbow colours seen in soap bubbles, oil films on water, oxide-coated metals, and similar phenomena.

---

**Q2. [3 Marks]** *State the conditions for constructive and destructive interference of light reflected from a thin film of refractive index $\mu$ surrounded by air, when illuminated normally.*

**Model Answer:**

For a thin film of refractive index $\mu$ (higher than the surrounding air), illuminated normally ($\cos r = 1$):

- **Condition for Constructive Interference (Bright fringe in reflected light):**
  $$2\mu t = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

- **Condition for Destructive Interference (Dark fringe in reflected light):**
  $$2\mu t = n\lambda, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

The extra $\lambda/2$ term in the constructive condition arises because the ray reflected from the upper surface (denser medium) undergoes an additional phase change of $\pi$, equivalent to a path difference of $\lambda/2$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module Internal Choice Format, Est. Style May 2025]**

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2025 (Expected Style)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Derive the conditions for constructive and destructive interference in reflected light from a thin film of uniform thickness $t$ and refractive index $\mu$, when monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident normally on it. Clearly explain the role of phase change on reflection.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A thin film of oil of refractive index $1.40$ floats on a pool of water (refractive index $1.33$). It is illuminated normally by light of wavelength $589.3$ nm. Calculate the minimum thickness of the oil film for it to appear (i) bright and (ii) dark in reflected light.*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Setup and ray tracing.**
Consider a thin film of uniform thickness $t$ and refractive index $\mu$, surrounded by air (refractive index $\approx 1$). A ray of monochromatic light is incident normally on the upper surface. Two reflected rays are obtained:
- Ray 1: Reflected from the top surface.
- Ray 2: Refracted in, reflected from the bottom surface, refracted out.

**Step 2: Identify the phase change.**
- Ray 1 reflects from a **denser medium** (the film below) at the top surface $\Rightarrow$ **extra phase change of $\pi$ radians** (path $\lambda/2$).
- Ray 2 reflects from a **rarer medium** (air below the film) at the bottom surface $\Rightarrow$ **no extra phase change**.

**Step 3: Optical path difference (without phase change).**
For normal incidence, the geometric path inside the film is $2t$, traversed in a medium of refractive index $\mu$:
$$\Delta = 2\mu t$$

**Step 4: Effective path difference (with phase correction).**
Adding the extra $\lambda/2$ from the phase change of Ray 1:
$$\Delta_{eff} = 2\mu t - \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Step 5: Constructive condition (bright fringe).**
For maximum intensity, $\Delta_{eff} = n\lambda$:
$$2\mu t - \frac{\lambda}{2} = n\lambda$$
$$2\mu t = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Step 6: Destructive condition (dark fringe).**
For minimum intensity, $\Delta_{eff} = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$:
$$2\mu t - \frac{\lambda}{2} = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$$
$$2\mu t = (n+1)\lambda = m\lambda$$
Renaming $m$ as $n$:
$$2\mu t = n\lambda$$

**Step 7: Conclusion.**
The two conditions are:
- **Bright:** $2\mu t = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$, $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
- **Dark:** $2\mu t = n\lambda$, $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

These conditions show that for a given $\lambda$ and $\mu$, only specific thicknesses give bright/dark fringes. The reverse conditions apply for the transmitted light.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $\mu_{oil} = 1.40$, $\mu_{water} = 1.33$, $\lambda = 589.3$ nm $= 589.3 \times 10^{-9}$ m.

**Case (i): Minimum thickness for BRIGHT reflection.**

Using $2\mu t = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$. For minimum thickness, $n = 0$:
$$2\mu t = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$
$$t = \frac{\lambda}{4\mu}$$
$$t = \frac{589.3 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 1.40}$$
$$t = \frac{589.3 \times 10^{-9}}{5.60}$$
$$\boxed{t_{bright} \approx 105.2 \text{ nm}}$$

**Case (ii): Minimum thickness for DARK reflection.**

Using $2\mu t = n\lambda$. For minimum non-zero thickness, $n = 1$:
$$t = \frac{\lambda}{2\mu}$$
$$t = \frac{589.3 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 1.40}$$
$$t = \frac{589.3 \times 10^{-9}}{2.80}$$
$$\boxed{t_{dark} \approx 210.5 \text{ nm}}$$

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Expected Style)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the phenomenon of formation of colours in thin films with a suitable diagram. Why do we see different colours at different points? Why does a soap film appear black just before it breaks?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A glass lens is to be coated with a thin film of magnesium fluoride ($\mu = 1.38$) to make it non-reflective for light of wavelength $550$ nm (green light, near the peak sensitivity of the human eye). Calculate the minimum thickness of the coating required. The lens is made of glass with $\mu = 1.50$.*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Explanation of the phenomenon:**

When a thin film of transparent material (such as soap, oil, or a metallic oxide) is illuminated by white light, the light is partly reflected from the top surface and partly refracted into the film, then reflected from the bottom surface, and finally emerges out. These two reflected waves are coherent (they originate from the same source) and superpose to produce interference.

The optical path difference between them is:
$$\Delta = 2\mu t \cos r - \frac{\lambda}{2}$$
(the $\lambda/2$ being due to the phase change on reflection at the top surface).

**Why different colours at different points:**

The condition for constructive interference in reflected light is:
$$2\mu t \cos r = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$$

Since the thickness $t$ varies from point to point (the film is never perfectly flat), this condition is satisfied for **different wavelengths at different points**. At any point, only the wavelength for which the constructive condition is satisfied is strongly reflected. The colour seen at that point is therefore the colour of the dominant reflected wavelength. As we move across the film, the colour changes because $t$ changes.

**Why a soap film appears black just before breaking:**

As the soap film drains due to gravity, its thickness $t$ becomes very small — approaching zero. For all visible wavelengths (400–700 nm), the optical path difference $2\mu t \cos r$ becomes much smaller than $\lambda/2$. The condition for destructive interference $2\mu t = n\lambda$ is satisfied with $n = 0$ (since $t \to 0$). Therefore, all wavelengths suffer destructive interference in reflected light, and **no light is reflected** — only the dark background (or transmitted darkness) is seen, making the film appear **black**. The film is actually so thin that it is comparable to a few molecular layers, and the standard bulk interference analysis breaks down.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $\mu_{MgF_2} = 1.38$, $\mu_{glass} = 1.50$, $\lambda = 550$ nm $= 550 \times 10^{-9}$ m.

**Objective:** Make the lens non-reflective at $\lambda = 550$ nm. This means we need **destructive interference in reflected light**.

**Analysis of phase changes:**
- Light travels from air ($\mu = 1$) into MgF$_2$ ($\mu = 1.38$): reflection at top surface is from a **denser medium** $\Rightarrow$ **phase change of $\pi$**.
- Light travels inside MgF$_2$ ($\mu = 1.38$) and reflects at the MgF$_2$-glass interface: from $\mu = 1.38$ to $\mu = 1.50$ (denser medium) $\Rightarrow$ **phase change of $\pi$**.

**Both reflected rays undergo a phase change of $\pi$.** Therefore, the **net phase difference due to reflection alone is ZERO** (the two $\pi$ phase changes cancel out).

**Effective path difference = Geometric path difference = $2\mu t$** (for normal incidence).

**Condition for destructive interference (destructive OPD = odd multiple of $\lambda/2$):**
$$2\mu t = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$$

For **minimum thickness**, $n = 0$:
$$2\mu t = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$
$$t = \frac{\lambda}{4\mu}$$
$$t = \frac{550 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 1.38}$$
$$t = \frac{550 \times 10^{-9}}{5.52}$$
$$\boxed{t \approx 99.6 \text{ nm}}$$

This is the standard $\lambda/4$ anti-reflection coating condition.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\lambda/2$ phase correction:** Many students write $2\mu t = n\lambda$ for bright fringes in reflected light. This is **WRONG** when the film has a higher refractive index than its surrounding media. The bright condition is $2\mu t = (2n+1)\lambda/2$.
> 2. **Confusing reflected and transmitted light conditions:** The conditions for maxima and minima **swap** when going from reflected to transmitted light. If reflected has a bright fringe, transmitted has a dark fringe, and vice versa.
> 3. **Using $n = 0$ for minimum thickness in dark conditions:** For $2\mu t = n\lambda$, setting $n = 0$ gives $t = 0$, which is not a physically meaningful minimum thickness. Use $n = 1$ for the minimum non-zero dark film.
> 4. **Mixing up cosine of incidence and refraction:** The path difference is $2\mu t \cos r$, NOT $2\mu t \cos i$. You must use the angle of **refraction** $r$, not the angle of incidence $i$.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units:** $\lambda$ is often given in nm, but thickness $t$ must be computed in metres and then converted. Final answers should be expressed in **nm or $\mu$m**.
> 6. **In anti-reflection coating problems:** Carefully check whether the lower medium is denser or rarer than the coating. The phase change pattern can be different from the standard soap-film case.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Colours in Thin Films**

- **Definition:** Interference due to reflection from upper and lower surfaces of a transparent film whose thickness is of the order of the wavelength of light.
- **Phase change of $\pi$ (path $\lambda/2$):** Occurs when light reflects from a **denser medium**. This is the most critical concept.
- **Optical path difference (with phase correction):** $\Delta_{eff} = 2\mu t \cos r - \lambda/2$ (for film denser than surroundings).
- **Conditions for reflected light (film denser than surroundings):**
  - **Bright fringe:** $2\mu t \cos r = (2n+1)\lambda/2$, $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
  - **Dark fringe:** $2\mu t \cos r = n\lambda$, $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$
- **For normal incidence:** $\cos r = 1$, so conditions reduce to $2\mu t = (2n+1)\lambda/2$ (bright) and $2\mu t = n\lambda$ (dark).
- **Transmitted light conditions are exactly reversed** of the reflected light conditions.
- **Origin of colours:** Different wavelengths satisfy the bright condition at different points (varying $t$). White light minus the cancelled colour appears as the reflected colour.
- **Soap bubble appears black before bursting** because the film becomes so thin ($t \to 0$) that the dark condition $2\mu t = 0$ is satisfied for all visible wavelengths simultaneously — no reflection.
- **Anti-reflection coating (e.g., $\text{MgF}_2$ on glass):** Minimum thickness $t = \lambda / (4\mu)$. Both reflections have phase change $\pi$, so they cancel, and the effective path difference is just $2\mu t$.
- **Maximum reflection (highly reflective coating):** $t = \lambda / (2\mu)$ (or odd multiples of $\lambda/4$ depending on geometry).
- **Snell's law:** $\sin i = \mu \sin r$ — required for oblique incidence problems.
- **Units:** Always keep $\lambda$, $\mu$, and $t$ in consistent SI units (m), or convert $\lambda$ to nm and $t$ to nm in the final answer for readability.
- **Engineering applications:** Anti-reflection coatings on camera lenses, eyeglasses, solar cells; high-reflectance dielectric mirrors in lasers; optical filters; iridescent colour production in cosmetics and paints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked "explain the formation of colours in thin films", always structure your answer in three parts: (1) describe the interference mechanism, (2) state the conditions for maxima/minima with the phase-change correction, and (3) explain why colours appear (wavelength selectivity due to varying $t$) and what happens at zero thickness. This guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Newton's Rings - Determination of Refractive Index of Transparent Liquids and Wavelength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GZPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Newton's Rings - Determination of Refractive Index of Transparent Liquids and Wavelength # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Newton's Rings** is a classical phenomenon of **thin-film interference** observed when a monochromatic beam of light is reflected from a thin film of air (or another transparent medium) trapped between the curved surface of a **plano-convex lens** of large radius of curvature *R* and a flat, optically polished **glass plate** placed in contact with it. The film thickness *t* increases radially outward from the central point of contact, and the resulting interference pattern consists of a series of **alternating dark and bright concentric rings** centred on the point of contact. The rings are circular because the locus of points equidistant from the centre (where thickness is constant) forms a circle. The contact point itself is always **dark** (in reflected light) because of the additional $\lambda/2$ phase reversal that occurs upon reflection at the lower surface of the air film. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine dropping a smooth, dome-shaped pebble (the lens) onto a still pond (the glass plate). The water trapped in the thin gap between them forms a film whose *thickness increases* as you move radially outward from the contact point. Now imagine the pond surface illuminated by a single pure colour (say, pure yellow from a sodium lamp). The reflections from the upper and lower surfaces of this water film will alternately **cancel** (dark rings) and **reinforce** (bright rings) each other, painting a beautiful bullseye pattern. The pebble is our **lens**, the pond is the **glass plate**, and the trapped water is the **air film**. If we replace the water with honey (a liquid of higher refractive index), the bullseye pattern **shrinks** inwards — and the *amount* of shrinkage tells us the refractive index of the honey! ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$ - **Wavelength of sodium D-lines:** $\lambda_D \approx 589.3 \, \text{nm}$ (mean of $D_1 = 589.6 \, \text{nm}$ and $D_2 = 589.0 \, \text{nm}$) - **Radius of curvature** of the plano-convex lens: typically $R \approx 100 \, \text{cm}$ in standard laboratory experiments - **Order of interference** $n$: a positive integer, $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ - **Refractive index of air:** $\mu_{air} \approx 1.0003 \approx 1$ for practical KTU calculations # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Formation Geometry — The "Why" Behind the Rings The plano-convex lens touches the glass plate at a single point. Let the **thickness of the air film at a radial distance $r$** from the contact point be $t$. Applying the Pythagoras theorem to the geometry (and using $t \ll R$): $$ \begin{aligned} R^2 &= (R - t)^2 + r^2 \\ R^2 &= R^2 - 2Rt + t^2 + r^2 \\ r^2 &= 2Rt - t^2 \\ r^2 &\approx 2Rt \quad \text{(since } t \ll R \text{)} \\ \therefore \quad t &= \frac{r^2}{2R} \end{aligned} $$ This tells us: the thickness $t$ of the air film is **proportional to the square of the radius $r$**, which is the fundamental geometric reason for the rings being circular and progressively closer together as $n$ increases. ## 2.2 Path Difference Analysis — The "How" Behind Bright/Dark When monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$ falls normally on the air film: - **Ray 1:** Reflects from the **bottom surface of the lens** (top of the air film). Light travels from glass ($\mu_g \approx 1.5$) into air ($\mu \approx 1$) — this is a reflection from a *denser-to-rarer* medium, so **NO phase change** occurs. - **Ray 2:** Enters the air film, reflects from the **top surface of the glass plate** (bottom of the air film). Light travels from air ($\mu \approx 1$) into glass ($\mu_g \approx 1.5$) — this is a reflection from a *rarer-to-denser* medium, so a phase change of $\pi$ (equivalent to an **extra path of $\lambda/2$**) occurs. Therefore, the **effective optical path difference** between the two reflected rays is: $$ \Delta = 2\mu t \cos\theta + \frac{\lambda}{2} $$ For **normal incidence**, $\theta = 0$, hence $\cos\theta = 1$: $$ \Delta = 2\mu t + \frac{\lambda}{2} $$ ## 2.3 Conditions for Constructive and Destructive Interference | Phenomenon | Condition | Resulting Equation (in air, $\mu = 1$) | |------------|-----------|---------------------------------------| | **Constructive** (Bright rings in reflection) | $\Delta = n\lambda$ | $2t = (2n - 1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$ | | **Destructive** (Dark rings in reflection) | $\Delta = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$ | $2t = n\lambda$ | | **Constructive** (Bright rings in transmission) | $\Delta = n\lambda$ (no $\lambda/2$ from either surface) | $2t = n\lambda$ | | **Destructive** (Dark rings in transmission) | $\Delta = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$ | $2t = (2n-1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU:** Notice the **complementary swap** — what is *dark in reflection* is *bright in transmission*, and vice versa. This follows the principle of conservation of energy: light energy not reflected is transmitted, so the interference patterns must be inverted. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Used For | |---|---------|----------------|----------| | 1 | $t = \dfrac{r^2}{2R}$ | Film thickness at radius $r$ | Geometry of the air gap | | 2 | $r_n^2 = n\lambda R$ | **Dark rings in air** | Refractive index of air, basic theory | | 3 | $r_n^2 = (2n-1)\dfrac{\lambda R}{2}$ | **Bright rings in air** | Bright-ring variant problems | | 4 | $r_n^2 = \dfrac{n\lambda R}{\mu}$ | **Dark rings in liquid** | Refractive index of liquid | | 5 | $r_n^2 = (2n-1)\dfrac{\lambda R}{2\mu}$ | **Bright rings in liquid** | Bright-ring liquid variant | | 6 | $\mu = \dfrac{D_n^2(\text{air})}{D_n^2(\text{liquid})}$ | Refractive index (using diameters $D$) | **Liquid refractive index** determination | | 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2}{4pR}$ | Wavelength using two dark rings | **Wavelength** determination | | 8 | $R = \dfrac{D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2}{4p\lambda}$ | Radius of curvature of lens | Lens radius determination | | 9 | $\mu = \dfrac{D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2}{4p\lambda R}(\text{air}) \bigg/ \dfrac{D_{n+p}^{\prime 2} - D_n^{\prime 2}}{4p\lambda R}(\text{liq.})$ | Generalised form | Avoids needing $R$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Convention:** Diameter $D = 2r$ is **always preferred over radius** in experiments because it is far easier to read two opposite ring positions on a travelling microscope and take the mean, thereby cancelling out any error due to the centre of the ring pattern not coinciding with the cross-wire. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Optical Metrology:** Determining the radius of curvature of lens surfaces to micron-level precision. - **Quality Control of Optical Components:** Inspecting smoothness and sphericity of high-precision lenses, prisms, and mirrors. - **Liquid Refractometry:** Measuring refractive indices of transparent liquids (oils, biological fluids, chemicals) with extreme accuracy (down to $\pm 0.0001$). - **Thin-Film Coatings Industry:** Characterising anti-reflective coatings, lens blooming, and optical filters. - **Biomedical Engineering:** Studying properties of biological fluids, contact-lens materials, and ophthalmic liquids. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Radius of the $n^{\text{th}}$ Dark Ring (in Air) > [!NOTE] > **Starting Point:** The air-film thickness at radial distance $r_n$ is $t_n = \dfrac{r_n^2}{2R}$. The condition for a **dark ring** in reflection is $2t = n\lambda$ (since the $\lambda/2$ extra path from the lower surface reflection is what produces darkness in the first place — i.e., the central contact point, $n=0$, is dark because $t = 0$ there). **Step 1:** Write the destructive interference condition: $$ 2t_n = n\lambda $$ **Step 2:** Substitute $t_n = \dfrac{r_n^2}{2R}$: $$ 2 \cdot \frac{r_n^2}{2R} = n\lambda $$ **Step 3:** Simplify the left-hand side: $$ \frac{r_n^2}{R} = n\lambda $$ **Step 4:** Rearrange to obtain the standard KTU result: $$ \boxed{r_n^2 = n\lambda R} $$ **Step 5:** Express in terms of diameter $D_n = 2r_n$, so $r_n^2 = D_n^2/4$: $$ D_n^2 = 4n\lambda R $$ This is the **fundamental equation** from which all other KTU derivations follow. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Refractive Index of a Transparent Liquid > [!IMPORTANT] > **Setup:** A drop of transparent liquid (refractive index $\mu$) is introduced into the air gap. The liquid fills the wedge between the lens and the plate, replacing the air film. The lens is no longer in direct contact with the glass plate. **Step 1:** The optical path difference now involves the liquid, so the destructive condition becomes: $$ 2\mu t_n' = n\lambda $$ **Step 2:** The geometry remains the same (same lens, same $R$), so: $$ t_n' = \frac{r_n'^2}{2R} $$ **Step 3:** Substituting into the destructive condition: $$ 2\mu \cdot \frac{r_n'^2}{2R} = n\lambda $$ **Step 4:** Simplify: $$ r_n'^2 = \frac{n\lambda R}{\mu} $$ **Step 5:** Compare with the air case $r_n^2 = n\lambda R$. Dividing the two: $$ \frac{r_n^2}{r_n'^2} = \mu $$ **Step 6:** Express in terms of measurable diameters: $$ \boxed{\mu = \frac{D_n^2 \text{ (in air)}}{D_n^{\prime \, 2} \text{ (in liquid)}}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write $\mu = D_n(\text{air})/D_n'(\text{liq.})$ — **WRONG!** The ratio must be of the **squares** of the diameters, not the diameters themselves, because the interference condition relates to $r^2$, not $r$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wavelength of Monochromatic Light **Step 1:** Write the dark-ring diameter relation for the $n^{\text{th}}$ and $(n+p)^{\text{th}}$ rings: $$ D_n^2 = 4n\lambda R \quad \text{and} \quad D_{n+p}^2 = 4(n+p)\lambda R $$ **Step 2:** Subtract the two equations (this eliminates the unknown order $n$): $$ D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2 = 4(n+p)\lambda R - 4n\lambda R $$ **Step 3:** Expand the right-hand side: $$ D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2 = 4n\lambda R + 4p\lambda R - 4n\lambda R $$ **Step 4:** Cancel the $4n\lambda R$ terms: $$ D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2 = 4p\lambda R $$ **Step 5:** Solve for $\lambda$: $$ \boxed{\lambda = \frac{D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2}{4pR}} $$ **Step 6:** For a liquid, the wavelength relation becomes: $$ \lambda = \frac{\mu (D_{n+p}^{\prime \, 2} - D_n^{\prime \, 2})}{4pR} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this is clever:** The subtraction trick eliminates the unknown order $n$ entirely. You never need to know whether you're looking at the 5th ring or the 50th ring — you just need the diameters of *any two rings separated by $p$ orders*. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sample Calculation Strategy | Given Quantity | Find | Approach | |----------------|------|----------| | $D_n$, $D_{n+p}$, $R$ | $\lambda$ | Use $\lambda = \dfrac{D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2}{4pR}$ | | $D_n$ (air), $D_n'$ (liquid) | $\mu$ | Use $\mu = \dfrac{D_n^2}{D_n'^2}$ | | $D_n$ (air), $D_n'$ (liquid), $\lambda$ | $R$ | From $R = \dfrac{D_n^2}{4n\lambda}$ in air, then verify with liquid data | | $D_n$ at $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ | Dispersive properties | Compare $D_n^2$ for the two wavelengths | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup of Newton's Rings ```mermaid flowchart TB LightSource["Monochromatic Light Source
Sodium Lamp"] Filter["Filter for Monochromatic Light
589.3 nm D Line"] BeamSplitter["Glass Plate at 45 degrees
Acts as Beam Splitter"] LensSetup["Plano-Convex Lens on Flat Glass Plate
Curved Surface in Contact"] AirFilm["Thin Air Film or Liquid Film
Variable Thickness t of r"] TravellingMicroscope["Travelling Microscope
Measures Ring Diameters D sub n"] ObserverEye["Viewing System
Concentric Dark and Bright Rings"] LightSource --> Filter Filter --> BeamSplitter BeamSplitter -- "Reflected Beam Normal Incidence" --> LensSetup LensSetup -- "Thin Film Interference" --> AirFilm AirFilm -- "Reflected Concentric Rings" --> TravellingMicroscope TravellingMicroscope --> ObserverEye BeamSplitter -. "Transmitted Component" .-> TravellingMicroscope ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Interference Process ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Incident["Incident Monochromatic Light"] RayIn["Parallel Beam of Wavelength lambda"] end subgraph FilmAssembly["Air or Liquid Film Assembly"] TopSurface["Top of Film
Bottom of Lens
NO Phase Change"] BottomSurface["Bottom of Film
Top of Glass Plate
Phase Change Pi"] end subgraph PathDiff["Path Difference Computation"] TwoMuT["Path 1: 2 times mu times t"] LambdaHalf["Additional Path: lambda by 2"] TotalDelta["Total Delta equals 2 mu t plus lambda by 2"] end subgraph Interference["Interference Outcome"] Bright["Bright Ring when Delta equals n lambda"] Dark["Dark Ring when Delta equals 2n plus 1 times lambda by 2"] end RayIn --> TopSurface TopSurface --> TwoMuT TwoMuT --> TotalDelta RayIn --> BottomSurface BottomSurface --> LambdaHalf LambdaHalf --> TotalDelta TotalDelta --> Bright TotalDelta --> Dark ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Description — Ray Diagram of Formation The physical setup consists of a **plano-convex lens** of large radius of curvature $R$ placed with its convex side resting on a flat, polished **glass plate**. The contact point $O$ is the geometric centre. At a radial distance $r$ from $O$, the air gap has thickness $t = r^2/(2R)$. - A ray of monochromatic light falls **normally** on the lens surface. - Ray AB partially reflects from the **bottom surface of the lens** at point B (ray BD — no phase change). - Ray AB refracts into the air gap, travels to the top of the glass plate at point C, and reflects back (ray CE — phase change of $\pi$). - Rays BD and CE then interfere at the microscope objective. - The **central spot at O is dark** because $t = 0$ gives an effective path difference of exactly $\lambda/2$ (destructive interference). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** > **Why is the central spot in Newton's rings experiment dark in the reflected light system?** **Model Answer:** The central spot corresponds to the point of contact between the plano-convex lens and the glass plate, where the air-film thickness is $t = 0$. At this point, the two reflected rays (one from the bottom of the lens, one from the top of the glass plate) traverse no geometric path difference. However, the ray reflecting from the top of the glass plate (going from a rarer medium *air* to a denser medium *glass*) undergoes a **phase change of $\pi$**, equivalent to an additional path of $\lambda/2$. The resulting effective path difference is therefore $\Delta = \lambda/2$, which is the condition for **destructive interference**. Hence, the central spot appears **dark** in reflected light. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question A2 > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > **What happens to the Newton's ring pattern if the air film is replaced by a liquid of refractive index $\mu > 1$? Justify your answer.** **Model Answer:** When the air film is replaced by a transparent liquid of refractive index $\mu$, the condition for dark rings changes from $r_n^2 = n\lambda R$ to $r_n^2 = n\lambda R / \mu$. Since $\mu > 1$, the radii of all the rings **decrease** (the rings contract towards the centre). The ring pattern **becomes denser and moves closer to the centre**, while the central spot remains dark. This contraction occurs because the optical path difference $2\mu t$ now increases more rapidly with $t$, so the same path difference $n\lambda$ is achieved at a smaller geometric thickness $t$ — and hence at a smaller radius $r$. **(3 marks)** ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Theory + Refractive Index Problem > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]** > > **(a)** Derive the expression for the radius of the $n^{\text{th}}$ dark ring in Newton's rings experiment. Explain why the rings become closer as we move away from the centre. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** In a Newton's rings experiment, the diameter of the 5th dark ring was found to be $2.50 \times 10^{-3}$ m in air. When a liquid is introduced between the lens and the plate, the diameter of the 5th dark ring becomes $2.00 \times 10^{-3}$ m. Calculate the refractive index of the liquid. If the radius of curvature of the lens is $1.50$ m, determine the wavelength of light used. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1 — Geometry:** From the figure, by Pythagoras theorem: $$ R^2 = (R - t)^2 + r_n^2 \implies r_n^2 = 2Rt - t^2 \approx 2Rt $$ **Step 2 — Condition for dark ring in reflection:** The two reflected rays have an effective path difference of $\Delta = 2t + \lambda/2$ (the $\lambda/2$ coming from phase change $\pi$ at the lower surface). For destructive interference: $$ 2t + \frac{\lambda}{2} = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2} \implies 2t = n\lambda $$ **Step 3 — Combining:** $$ 2 \cdot \frac{r_n^2}{2R} = n\lambda \implies \boxed{r_n^2 = n\lambda R} $$ **Step 4 — Why rings get closer:** Since $r_n^2 \propto n$, we have $r_n \propto \sqrt{n}$. The gap between consecutive rings $\Delta r_n = r_{n+1} - r_n = \sqrt{\lambda R}(\sqrt{n+1} - \sqrt{n})$. As $n$ increases, the rate of change of $\sqrt{n}$ decreases, so $\Delta r_n$ shrinks. **Therefore, rings become progressively closer as we move outward from the centre.** **(7 marks — full marks distribution: 2 for geometry, 2 for interference condition, 2 for final derivation, 1 for ring spacing explanation)** --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Refractive index formula:** The dark ring diameter in air: $D_5^2 = 4 \cdot 5 \cdot \lambda R$ and in liquid: $D_5'^2 = 4 \cdot 5 \cdot \lambda R / \mu$. Taking the ratio: $$ \mu = \frac{D_5^2}{D_5'^2} $$ **Step 2 — Plug in values:** $$ \mu = \frac{(2.50 \times 10^{-3})^2}{(2.00 \times 10^{-3})^2} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{4.00 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.5625 $$ $$ \boxed{\mu \approx 1.563} $$ **Step 3 — Wavelength:** From the air relation $D_n^2 = 4n\lambda R$: $$ \lambda = \frac{D_n^2}{4nR} = \frac{(2.50 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \cdot 5 \cdot 1.50} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{30.0} $$ $$ \boxed{\lambda = 2.083 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} \approx 208.3 \, \text{nm}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Reality check:** 208 nm is in the UV range, which is unusual for a Newton's rings experiment (typically visible light is used). This suggests the problem data is purely numerical and may not be physically realistic, but the **mathematical procedure is correct**. **(7 marks — 2 for formula, 2 for $\mu$ value, 3 for $\lambda$ with units and verification)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Wavelength Determination > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > > **(a)** Describe the experimental setup to obtain Newton's rings and explain how the wavelength of a given monochromatic light source is determined using this setup. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** In a Newton's rings experiment, the diameter of the $n^{\text{th}}$ dark ring is $4.20 \times 10^{-3}$ m and that of the $(n+8)^{\text{th}}$ dark ring is $6.40 \times 10^{-3}$ m. If the radius of curvature of the lens is $1.80$ m, calculate the wavelength of light used and the order of the first ring. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Experimental Setup:** A plano-convex lens of large radius of curvature $R$ is placed with its convex surface resting on a flat, optically polished glass plate. Monochromatic light from a sodium lamp is made parallel using a converging lens, then reflected downward by a glass plate held at $45^\circ$. The light falls normally on the lens-glass plate assembly. The reflected light passes through the $45^\circ$ glass plate and enters a travelling microscope focused on the air film. **Observation:** A pattern of concentric, alternating dark and bright rings is observed, with a dark central spot. **Wavelength Determination:** - Measure the diameters $D_n$ and $D_{n+p}$ of two dark rings separated by $p$ orders. - The relations are: $D_n^2 = 4n\lambda R$ and $D_{n+p}^2 = 4(n+p)\lambda R$. - Subtracting: $D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2 = 4p\lambda R$. - Solving: $$ \boxed{\lambda = \frac{D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2}{4pR}} $$ The value of $R$ is either supplied or measured independently using a spherometer, and the diameters are read using the travelling microscope's vernier scale. **(7 marks — 2 for setup description, 2 for observation, 3 for derivation and formula)** --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Apply the wavelength formula:** $$ \lambda = \frac{D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2}{4pR} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values ($p = 8$, $R = 1.80$ m):** $$ \lambda = \frac{(6.40 \times 10^{-3})^2 - (4.20 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \cdot 8 \cdot 1.80} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the squares:** $$ (6.40 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 40.96 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2 $$ $$ (4.20 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 17.64 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the difference:** $$ 40.96 \times 10^{-6} - 17.64 \times 10^{-6} = 23.32 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Compute the denominator:** $$ 4 \cdot 8 \cdot 1.80 = 57.6 \, \text{m} $$ **Step 6 — Final division:** $$ \lambda = \frac{23.32 \times 10^{-6}}{57.6} = 4.048 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m} \approx 404.8 \, \text{nm} $$ $$ \boxed{\lambda \approx 405 \, \text{nm}} $$ This corresponds to **violet light** (in the visible spectrum, ~400–420 nm corresponds to violet). **Step 7 — Order of the first ring:** Use $D_n^2 = 4n\lambda R$: $$ n = \frac{D_n^2}{4\lambda R} = \frac{17.64 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \cdot 4.048 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 1.80} $$ $$ n = \frac{17.64 \times 10^{-6}}{2.9146 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6.05 $$ Since $n$ must be an integer, the value is $n \approx 6$. Therefore, the diameters quoted correspond to the **6th and 14th dark rings**. $$ \boxed{n = 6} $$ **(7 marks — 3 for $\lambda$ calculation with all steps, 2 for integer verification, 2 for the order derivation)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > > 1. **Forgetting the SQUARE in $\mu$ formula** — students write $\mu = D_n/D_n'$ instead of $\mu = D_n^2/D_n'^2$. Loss: **1–2 marks**. > 2. **Mixing up the factor of 4 in $D_n^2$ equation** — $D_n^2 = 4n\lambda R$, not $D_n^2 = n\lambda R$ (which is for $r_n^2$). Loss: **1 mark**. > 3. **Forgetting units in the final $\lambda$ answer** — always quote in metres (SI) or nanometres with explicit unit. Loss: **0.5 mark**. > 4. **Using $D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2$ vs $D_{n+p} - D_n$** — must always be the **difference of squares** of diameters. Loss: **2 marks**. > 5. **Not verifying that $n$ is an integer** — examiners will check this. If you get $n = 6.05$, you must round to 6 and acknowledge the small experimental error. Loss: **0.5–1 mark**. > 6. **Skipping the derivation step in (a)** — you cannot directly write the formula. Loss: **2–3 marks** out of the 7 for that sub-part. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Setup:** Plano-convex lens on flat glass plate → thin air film → monochromatic light at normal incidence. - **Central spot is ALWAYS dark** (in reflection) due to the $\lambda/2$ phase change at the lower reflection surface. - **Dark ring radius:** $r_n^2 = n\lambda R$ (air) or $r_n^2 = n\lambda R / \mu$ (liquid). - **Bright ring radius:** $r_n^2 = (2n-1)\lambda R / 2$ (air) or $r_n^2 = (2n-1)\lambda R / (2\mu)$ (liquid). - **Refractive index of liquid:** $\mu = D_n^2(\text{air}) / D_n^2(\text{liquid})$ — ratio of **squares of diameters** of the *same-order* ring. - **Wavelength formula:** $\lambda = (D_{n+p}^2 - D_n^2) / (4pR)$ — uses **difference of squares** of diameters of rings separated by $p$ orders. - **Rings get closer** as we move outward because $r_n \propto \sqrt{n}$, and the rate of change of $\sqrt{n}$ decreases. - **Larger $\mu$** → rings **contract** inwards; **larger $\lambda$** → rings **expand** outwards. - **Always prefer diameters over radii** in experiments to eliminate cross-wire centring errors. - **The order $n$ must always be a positive integer** — if your calculated $n$ is not an integer, you've made a numerical or data error. - **Reflections in Newton's rings:** Glass-to-air (no phase change) at lens bottom; air-to-glass (phase change of $\pi$) at plate top — this is why $\lambda/2$ appears in the path difference. - **Complementary patterns:** Bright rings in reflection = dark rings in transmission (and vice versa). - **Engineering applications:** Lens radius measurement, optical surface quality control, liquid refractometry, thin-film characterisation, biomedical fluid analysis.

topics.title: Air Wedge - Measurement of Thickness of Thin Sheets; Diffraction - Types of Diffraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air Wedge - Measurement of Thickness of Thin Sheets; Diffraction - Types of Diffraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Air Wedge — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> An **air wedge** is a thin film of air of varying thickness formed between two optically flat glass surfaces inclined at a very small angle $\theta$ to each other. When monochromatic light is incident normally on this wedge, interference takes place due to the superposition of light reflected from the upper and lower glass-air interfaces, producing a system of equally spaced, straight, parallel dark and bright fringes.

These fringes are called **fringes of equal thickness** (also known as **Haidinger's fringes** in this specific geometry), because each fringe corresponds to a locus of points where the air-film thickness $t$ is constant.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Staircase Between Two Glass Plates"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine placing two flat glass plates on a table such that they touch at one edge and are separated by a thin wire (or a thin sheet whose thickness we wish to measure) at the other edge. The thin slice of air trapped between them is not uniform — it is **zero at one edge and maximum at the other**, forming the shape of a **wedge** (like a slice of cake, but extremely thin).
>
> When you shine a sodium street lamp vertically down onto this wedge, you see a beautiful pattern of **alternating dark and bright parallel bands** running perpendicular to the edge of contact. This pattern is the *signature* of the air wedge, and counting the bands lets you measure the thickness of the wire/sheet to within a fraction of a micrometer — a precision that no ruler could ever achieve.

---

## 1.3 Diffraction — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> **Diffraction** is the phenomenon of bending of light waves around the edges of an obstacle or aperture, accompanied by interference between the resulting secondary wavelets, leading to a redistribution of light intensity in the region of the geometrical shadow. It is a direct consequence of the **Huygens–Fresnel principle**.

---

## 1.4 Two Types of Diffraction

Diffraction phenomena are classified into two broad categories based on the relative geometry of the source, the diffracting obstacle/aperture, and the observation screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Type 1 — Fresnel Diffraction (Near-Field Diffraction):**
> Either the source or the screen (or both) are at a **finite distance** from the diffracting aperture. The incident wavefront is **spherical or cylindrical**. No lenses are required. Example: Diffraction at a straight edge illuminated by a point source placed close to it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Type 2 — Fraunhofer Diffraction (Far-Field Diffraction):**
> Both the source and the screen are effectively at an **infinite distance** from the aperture, so the incident and diffracted wavefronts are **plane (collimated)**. Usually achieved experimentally by placing the source at the focal point of a converging lens before the aperture and observing at the focal plane of a second lens after the aperture. Example: Single-slit diffraction pattern with parallel light.

---

## 1.5 Why Air Wedge and Diffraction Are Studied Together

Both topics are manifestations of the **wave nature of light** and are governed by the **superposition principle**. Air wedge uses **division of amplitude** (two reflections from a film), while diffraction uses the **Huygens–Fresnel** construction (division of wavefront). Together, they form the backbone of the optics module in the KTU 2024 physics syllabus.

| Property | Air Wedge | Diffraction |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Mechanism** | Interference (by amplitude division) | Interference of secondary wavelets |
| **Fringe Shape** | Straight, parallel, equally spaced | Complex (bands, rings, spots) |
| **Engineered Use** | Thickness measurement of thin films/sheets | Spectrometer calibration, grating analysis |
| **Typical Application** | Quality control of foils, wire gauges | Wavelength measurement, resolution of optical instruments |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Air Wedge — Theory of Formation

### 2.1.1 Construction of the Wedge

Two rectangular optically flat glass plates $P_1$ and $P_2$ are placed one on top of the other. A thin spacer (wire, foil, or the sheet whose thickness is to be measured) of thickness $d$ is introduced at one end $B$. The two plates thus touch at the opposite end $A$, forming a thin film of air whose thickness varies linearly from **$0$ at $A$** to **$d$ at $B$**.

The angle of the wedge is given by:

$$\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{d}{L}\right) \approx \frac{d}{L} \quad (\text{since } \theta \text{ is very small})$$

where $L$ is the length of the plates from the contact edge.

### 2.1.2 Path Difference Analysis

A ray of monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$ falls normally (i.e., from above, perpendicular to the top plate) onto the wedge. It undergoes two partial reflections:

1. **Ray 1:** Reflects from the **lower surface of the upper plate** (glass → air boundary — reflection from a *rarer* medium).
2. **Ray 2:** Refracts into the air film, reflects from the **upper surface of the lower plate** (air → glass boundary — reflection from a *denser* medium), and emerges back up.

At a point where the local air-film thickness is $t$, the **optical path difference** between Ray 2 and Ray 1 is:

$$\Delta = 2\mu t \cos r + \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

For air, $\mu = 1$, and for normal incidence, $r = 0$, $\cos r = 1$, so:

$$\Delta = 2t + \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

The **$\frac{\lambda}{2}$ term** arises because Ray 2 alone undergoes a phase change of $\pi$ (equivalent to an extra path of $\lambda/2$) on reflection at the denser medium — a critical detail frequently missed by students.

### 2.1.3 Conditions for Maxima and Minima

- **Constructive Interference (Bright Fringe):**

$$\Delta = n\lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2t + \frac{\lambda}{2} = n\lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2t = (2n-1)\frac{\lambda}{2}$$

- **Destructive Interference (Dark Fringe):**

$$\Delta = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2t + \frac{\lambda}{2} = (2n+1)\frac{\lambda}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2t = n\lambda$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:**
> At the contact edge, $t = 0$. Substituting in the dark-fringe condition: $2(0) = 0\lambda$, which is satisfied for $n=0$. Hence, the **contact edge is a DARK fringe**. This is the unmistakable signature of a wedge interference pattern.

### 2.1.4 Fringe Width (Spacing)

The $n^{\text{th}}$ dark fringe occurs at a distance $x_n$ from the contact edge where:

$$2t_n = n\lambda \quad \text{and} \quad t_n = x_n \tan\theta \approx x_n \theta$$

Combining:

$$2 x_n \theta = n\lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_n = \frac{n\lambda}{2\theta}$$

Similarly, the $(n+1)^{\text{th}}$ dark fringe is at:

$$x_{n+1} = \frac{(n+1)\lambda}{2\theta}$$

The **fringe width** $\beta$ is the separation between two successive dark (or bright) fringes:

$$\beta = x_{n+1} - x_n = \frac{(n+1)\lambda}{2\theta} - \frac{n\lambda}{2\theta} = \frac{\lambda}{2\theta}$$

This shows that **fringes are equally spaced**, with spacing dependent only on $\lambda$ and $\theta$ (and independent of $n$).

### 2.1.5 Measurement of Thickness of a Thin Sheet

Let the spacer (sheet/wire) of unknown thickness $d$ be placed at a distance $L$ from the contact edge.

- Total distance from contact edge to the position of the sheet: $x_N = L$, where the $N^{\text{th}}$ dark fringe is observed.
- The corresponding film thickness is $t_N = L \tan\theta = d$ (the sheet thickness itself).

Using $2d = N\lambda$:

$$d = \frac{N\lambda}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility — Industrial Application:**
> This is the **standard non-destructive method** for measuring the thickness of thin metallic foils, polymer films, and even the diameter of fine wires in semiconductor and packaging industries. A typical measurement accuracy is $\pm \lambda/4 \approx 0.1\,\mu m$ for green light — vastly superior to mechanical micrometers for sub-micron films.

---

## 2.2 Diffraction — Theoretical Analysis of the Two Types

### 2.2.1 General Principle (Huygens–Fresnel)

Every unobstructed point on a wavefront acts as a source of secondary wavelets, and the resultant field at any later point is the **vector sum** of the contributions of all these wavelets, with proper phase and amplitude.

$$\Psi(P) = -\frac{i}{\lambda}\iint_\Sigma \Psi(Q)\, \frac{e^{ikr}}{r} \cdot \frac{\cos\theta}{2}\, dS$$

The difference between Fresnel and Fraunhofer regimes lies in the mathematical simplification of the above integral.

### 2.2.2 Fresnel Diffraction — Characteristics

- **Geometry:** Source $S$ and screen $C$ are at **finite** distances $a$ and $b$ from the aperture.
- **Wavefront:** Spherical (or cylindrical) wavefront illuminates the aperture.
- **Mathematical Treatment:** Integral is evaluated using **Fresnel integrals** $C(v)$ and $S(v)$ or via **half-period zones (zone plate analysis)**.
- **Pattern:** Sharp-edged shadows of the obstacle, with light and dark diffraction bands within and outside the geometric shadow boundary.
- **Example:** Light from a point source passing the edge of a razor blade casts a pattern of non-uniform bright and dark bands near the shadow edge.

### 2.2.3 Fraunhofer Diffraction — Characteristics

- **Geometry:** Source at infinity (parallel beam) and screen at infinity (or at the focal plane of a converging lens).
- **Wavefront:** Plane wavefronts both incident and diffracted.
- **Mathematical Treatment:** The integral simplifies to a **Fourier transform** of the aperture transmission function. For a single slit of width $a$:
$$I(\theta) = I_0 \left(\frac{\sin\beta}{\beta}\right)^2, \quad \beta = \frac{\pi a \sin\theta}{\lambda}$$
- **Pattern:** Central maximum with symmetrically placed minima and secondary maxima of rapidly decreasing intensity.
- **Example:** Grating spectrometer, where the grating is placed between two collimating lenses.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the table below — these are the formulas that recur in every KTU University Exam paper from this module.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Remarks |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wedge angle | $\theta = d / L$ | $d$ = sheet thickness, $L$ = plate length | Small-angle approximation valid |
| 2 | Optical path difference | $\Delta = 2\mu t \cos r + \lambda/2$ | $t$ = local film thickness, $r$ = ref. angle | $\lambda/2$ due to denser reflection |
| 3 | Bright fringe condition | $2t = (2n-1)\lambda/2$ | $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ | $n=0$ excluded (gives $t < 0$) |
| 4 | Dark fringe condition | $2t = n\lambda$ | $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ | $n=0$ → contact edge is DARK |
| 5 | Position of $n^{\text{th}}$ dark fringe | $x_n = n\lambda / (2\theta)$ | Measured from contact edge | Linear in $n$ |
| 6 | Fringe width | $\beta = \lambda / (2\theta)$ | Independent of $n$ | Equidistant fringes |
| 7 | Thickness of thin sheet | $d = N\lambda / (2)$ | $N$ = fringe count over length $L$ | Direct, non-contact method |
| 8 | Fresnel diffraction | Finite source + finite screen | Spherical wavefronts | Zone construction used |
| 9 | Fraunhofer diffraction | Source & screen at $\infty$ | Plane wavefronts | Fourier-transform math |
| 10 | Single-slit intensity (Fraunhofer) | $I = I_0(\sin\beta/\beta)^2$ | $\beta = \pi a \sin\theta / \lambda$ | Central max + side lobes |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where you'll use this in your engineering career:**

- **Air Wedge in Industry:** Thickness gauging of aluminium foils, plastic wraps, paint coatings on metal substrates, and paper.
- **Air Wedge in Optics:** Testing the flatness of glass surfaces (any deviation from straight, equally spaced fringes indicates a surface defect).
- **Fresnel Diffraction in Industry:** Design of **Fresnel lenses** (lighthouse lenses, solar concentrators, VR headsets) and **zone plates** (X-ray imaging).
- **Fraunhofer Diffraction in Industry:** **Diffraction gratings** in spectrometers for chemical analysis, **CD/DVD pickup heads**, **laser beam profilers**, and **resolution testing** of camera lenses (using USAF 1951 resolution chart is essentially a Fraunhofer setup).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fringe Width in an Air Wedge

We will derive the expression for the distance between two consecutive dark fringes in a wedge-shaped air film.

**Step 1 — Geometric relation between $t$ and $x$:**

Consider a wedge with vertex at the contact edge $A$ and semi-angle $\theta$ (between the two plates). Let $x$ be the horizontal distance from the contact edge to a point $P$ on the lower plate, and $t$ the corresponding vertical thickness of the air film at $P$.

By the geometry of a right-angled triangle:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{t}{x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = x \tan\theta$$

**Step 2 — Condition for the $n^{\text{th}}$ dark fringe:**

For a dark fringe produced by reflected light from the air film, the condition is:

$$2t = n\lambda, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two relations:**

$$2x_n \tan\theta = n\lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_n = \frac{n\lambda}{2\tan\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Find the position of the $(n+1)^{\text{th}}$ dark fringe:**

$$x_{n+1} = \frac{(n+1)\lambda}{2\tan\theta}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract to obtain the fringe width $\beta$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\beta &= x_{n+1} - x_n \\
&= \frac{(n+1)\lambda}{2\tan\theta} - \frac{n\lambda}{2\tan\theta} \\
&= \frac{\lambda}{2\tan\theta}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the small-angle approximation $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ (since $\theta$ is of the order of a few minutes of an arc):**

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{\lambda}{2\theta}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU result** for air-wedge fringe width.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thickness of a Thin Sheet from Fringe Counting

**Step 1 — Let the thin sheet (of unknown thickness $d$) be placed at a distance $L$ from the contact edge of the wedge.**

**Step 2 — Suppose $N$ dark fringes are observed between the contact edge and the position of the sheet.**

The position of the $N^{\text{th}}$ dark fringe is at:

$$x_N = L = \frac{N\lambda}{2\tan\theta}$$

**Step 3 — The thickness of the air film at the location of the sheet equals the sheet thickness itself:**

$$t_N = L \tan\theta = d$$

**Step 4 — From the dark-fringe condition:**

$$2t_N = N\lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2d = N\lambda$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $d$:**

$$\boxed{d = \frac{N\lambda}{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice the elegance:** The thickness of the sheet depends *only* on $N$ and $\lambda$ — not on $L$, not on $\theta$, not on any geometric quantity that is hard to measure. The only measurement required is *counting the number of fringes* $N$. This makes it one of the most accurate thickness-measurement methods in physics.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Path Difference in the Air Wedge (Detailed)

**Step 1 — Incident ray strikes the upper surface of the top plate normally. Part of it is reflected (Ray 1), part is transmitted into the air film.**

**Step 2 — The transmitted ray travels through the air film of thickness $t$ to the lower plate, undergoes reflection at the denser (glass) medium, and travels back up through the air film.**

**Step 3 — Geometric path traversed by Ray 2 inside the air film:**

$$\text{Geometric path} = 2t \quad (\text{since } r = 0)$$

**Step 4 — Optical path of Ray 2 inside the air film (multiply by refractive index of air $\mu$):**

$$\text{Optical path} = 2\mu t \cos r = 2(1)(t)(1) = 2t$$

**Step 5 — Additional path contribution due to reflection phase change:**

Ray 2 alone reflects from a **denser** medium (air → glass), so it suffers a **phase change of $\pi$**, equivalent to an **extra optical path of $\lambda/2$**.

Ray 1 reflects from a **rarer** medium (glass → air), so it suffers **no phase change**.

**Step 6 — Total effective optical path difference between Ray 2 and Ray 1:**

$$\Delta = 2\mu t \cos r + \frac{\lambda}{2} = 2t + \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fraunhofer Diffraction at a Single Slit (Conceptual Derivation)

This is a high-yield derivation for the KTU module. We will sketch the key steps.

**Step 1 — Consider a slit of width $a$ illuminated by a plane wavefront of wavelength $\lambda$ from a source at infinity.**

**Step 2 — Divide the slit into a large number of equal, infinitesimally thin strips, each of width $dy$, parallel to the slit length.**

**Step 3 — Each strip acts as a source of secondary wavelets (Huygens–Fresnel principle), all in phase initially.**

**Step 4 — At an observation point $P$ on the screen at angle $\theta$ to the slit normal, the path difference between wavelets from the extreme edges of the slit is:**

$$\delta = a \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 — The corresponding phase difference is:**

$$\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} a \sin\theta \equiv 2\beta, \quad \text{where } \beta = \frac{\pi a \sin\theta}{\lambda}$$

**Step 6 — Summing (integrating) the contributions of all the strips, treating each as a phasor of equal amplitude $dA$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A(\theta) &= A_0 \int_{-a/2}^{+a/2} e^{iky\sin\theta} \, dy \\
&= A_0 \cdot \frac{\sin\beta}{\beta}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — The intensity is the modulus squared of the amplitude:**

$$\boxed{I(\theta) = I_0 \left(\frac{\sin\beta}{\beta}\right)^2, \quad \beta = \frac{\pi a \sin\theta}{\lambda}}$$

**Step 8 — Positions of minima:** $\sin\beta = 0$ and $\beta \neq 0$, so $a \sin\theta_n = n\lambda$, $n = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$

**Step 9 — Position of the central maximum:** $\beta = 0$, i.e., $\theta = 0$, $I = I_0$.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem — Model Valuation:**
>
> **Q:** An air wedge is formed by placing a wire of diameter $0.10\,\text{mm}$ at one end of two glass plates of length $10\,\text{cm}$. If sodium light of wavelength $5893\,\text{Å}$ is used, calculate (a) the wedge angle, (b) the fringe width, and (c) the number of dark fringes observed across the plate.

**Solution:**

**Given:** $d = 0.10\,\text{mm} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$, $L = 10\,\text{cm} = 0.1\,\text{m}$, $\lambda = 5893\,\text{Å} = 5.893 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}$.

**(a) Wedge angle:**

$$\theta \approx \frac{d}{L} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.1} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{rad}$$

**(b) Fringe width:**
