**Formal Definition:**
If we *assume* an electron is confined within the nucleus, the uncertainty in its position is at most the nuclear diameter ($\Delta x \approx 10^{-15}$ m). The principle then forces a *minimum* uncertainty in its momentum — and hence a minimum kinetic energy. We show that this minimum kinetic energy is **enormously larger** than the binding energies available, so the assumption is physically impossible. **Therefore, electrons cannot exist as permanent residents inside the atomic nucleus.**

### 1.3 Application #2 — Natural Line Broadening

**Formal Definition:**
Every excited atomic state has a **finite mean lifetime** $\tau$ before it decays by emitting a photon. Because the energy of the excited state is known only for a finite time, the energy-time uncertainty relation gives the emitted photon a spread $\Delta E \approx \hbar/\tau$. Since $E = h\nu$, the spectral line is no longer perfectly sharp but has a **natural linewidth**:
$$\Delta \nu_{\text{natural}} \approx \frac{1}{4\pi \tau}$$

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Buzzing Bee in a Tiny Box:**
> Imagine trying to trap a buzzing bee inside a matchbox. The smaller the box (smaller $\Delta x$), the more furiously the bee has to vibrate (larger $\Delta p$). Shrink the box to the size of a pinhead and the bee would have to buzz so violently it would tear the box apart. The atomic nucleus acts as a "box" of radius $\approx 10^{-15}$ m. Forcing an electron to fit inside gives it such a violent momentum that it would shoot out instantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Wobbly Tuning Fork:**
> A tuning fork rung once and listened to for a long time gives a clear, single pitch. A tuning fork struck once and recorded for just a millisecond has a "smear" of frequencies around the central pitch. Similarly, an atom in an excited state that lives only for a fleeting instant (small $\tau$) emits a photon whose frequency is not perfectly defined, smearing the spectral line.

### 1.5 Physical Constants (Bolded as per Protocol)

- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
- Reduced Planck's constant: $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
- Electron rest mass: $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg**
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- Typical nuclear radius: $r_0 \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-15}$ **m**
- Typical atomic excited-state lifetime: $\tau \approx 10^{-8}$ **s**
- 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **J**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Chain — Why an Electron Cannot Live in the Nucleus

We adopt a *proof-by-contradiction* structure:

1. **Assumption (for argument's sake):** An electron exists inside the nucleus.
2. **Consequence 1 — Position uncertainty:** The position of the electron is bounded by the nuclear size, so $\Delta x \leq 2r_0 \approx 10^{-15}$ m.
3. **Consequence 2 — Forced momentum uncertainty:** The uncertainty principle demands $\Delta p \geq \hbar / (2\Delta x)$. We identify this uncertainty *itself* as a lower bound on the typical magnitude of the momentum: $p \sim \Delta p$.
4. **Consequence 3 — Required kinetic energy:** $KE = p^2/(2m_e)$ (non-relativistic, for the lower estimate; the relativistic form actually gives an even *larger* answer, strengthening the conclusion).
5. **Comparison:** This required KE turns out to be $\sim 10^{10}$ eV, while actual nuclear potential wells can only confine particles with binding energies of a few MeV.
6. **Contradiction:** No realistic mechanism can supply that energy, so the assumption is rejected — **the electron cannot be a permanent nuclear constituent.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The same argument, repeated for a *proton* inside a nucleus of size $\approx 10^{-15}$ m, gives a *modest* minimum KE (a few MeV), which is exactly of the right order of magnitude for nuclear binding. This is one of the deep reasons protons and neutrons — and *not* electrons — are the building blocks of nuclei.

### 2.2 The Logic Chain — Origin of Natural Linewidth

1. An excited atomic state $|e\rangle$ decays spontaneously to a lower state $|g\rangle$ with a mean lifetime $\tau$.
2. The atom is *not* a perpetual emitter — it emits the photon at some random time $t_0$. The duration over which the system is "in" state $|e\rangle$ is uncertain by $\Delta t \sim \tau$.
3. By the energy-time uncertainty relation, the *energy* of the emitted photon has an irreducible spread:
$$\Delta E \geq \frac{\hbar}{2\tau}$$
4. Because $E = h\nu$, the *frequency* of the emitted photon is correspondingly spread:
$$\Delta \nu \geq \frac{1}{4\pi \tau}$$
5. This is the **natural linewidth** of the spectral line. It is a *fundamental* quantum limit — no spectrometer, however perfect, can resolve a line narrower than this.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Position-momentum uncertainty | $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \hbar/2$ | $\hbar = h/2\pi$ | J·s |
| 2 | Energy-time uncertainty | $\Delta E \cdot \Delta t \geq \hbar/2$ | $\Delta t$ = lifetime / measurement time | J·s |
| 3 | Min. momentum (electron in nucleus) | $p_{\min} = \hbar / (2\Delta x)$ | $\Delta x \approx 10^{-15}$ m | kg·m/s |
| 4 | Non-relativistic KE | $KE = p^2 / (2m_e)$ | $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg | J |
| 5 | Relativistic total energy | $E = \sqrt{p^2 c^2 + m_e^2 c^4}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | J |
| 6 | Natural linewidth (frequency) | $\Delta \nu = 1 / (4\pi \tau)$ | $\tau$ = mean lifetime | Hz |
| 7 | Natural linewidth (wavelength) | $\Delta \lambda = \lambda^2 / (4\pi c \tau)$ | $\lambda$ = central wavelength | m |
| 8 | Energy-width in eV | $\Delta E (\text{eV}) = 6.582 \times 10^{-16} / \tau$ | $\tau$ in seconds | eV |
| 9 | Conversion | $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering / Real-world Utility:**
> - *Electron-in-nucleus* argument underlies the **discovery of the neutron** (Chadwick, 1932): beta decay suggested a neutral partner to the proton, not an electron-proton composite, because the latter is forbidden by the uncertainty principle.
> - *Natural line broadening* sets the **absolute lower bound on the linewidth of lasers** and limits the precision of **atomic clocks** and **astronomical Doppler-shift measurements** of stellar spectra.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation I — Why an Electron Cannot Exist Inside the Nucleus

**Given:**
- Typical nuclear radius: $r_0 \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-15}$ m
- Electron mass: $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $c = 3.00 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Goal:** Compute the minimum kinetic energy an electron must possess if confined to the nucleus, and compare with available binding energies.

**Step 1 — Identify the position uncertainty.**
If the electron is inside the nucleus, its position is bounded by the nuclear diameter. We take the position uncertainty as
$$\Delta x \approx 2 r_0 \approx 2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m}$$
For an order-of-magnitude estimate, a common simplification is to use $\Delta x \approx 10^{-15}$ m, the "femtometre" scale.

**Step 2 — Apply the position-momentum uncertainty principle.**
From Heisenberg's relation,
$$\Delta p \geq \frac{\hbar}{2 \Delta x}$$
The minimum possible magnitude of the electron's momentum is therefore of the order
$$p_{\min} \approx \frac{\hbar}{2 \Delta x} = \frac{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 10^{-15}}$$
$$p_{\min} \approx 5.27 \times 10^{-20} \text{ kg·m/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> *Many textbook treatments drop the factor of 2 in the denominator and write $p \approx \hbar/\Delta x$, giving a slightly different numerical prefactor. The order-of-magnitude conclusion is identical.*

**Step 3 — Check if non-relativistic mechanics is valid.**
The corresponding "rest-mass energy equivalent" of this momentum is
$$p_{\min} c = 5.27 \times 10^{-20} \times 3 \times 10^8 \approx 1.58 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J}$$
Converting to electron-volts:
$$p_{\min} c = \frac{1.58 \times 10^{-11}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 9.87 \times 10^7 \text{ eV} \approx 98.7 \text{ MeV}$$
Since $p_{\min} c \gg m_e c^2 = 0.511$ MeV, **the electron is ultra-relativistic**. We must use relativistic kinematics.

**Step 4 — Compute the total relativistic energy.**
$$E = \sqrt{p_{\min}^2 c^2 + m_e^2 c^4}$$
$$E \approx p_{\min} c \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{m_e c}{p_{\min}}\right)^2}$$
Numerically:
$$E \approx 1.58 \times 10^{-11} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (0.511 / 98.7)^2} \text{ J}$$
$$E \approx 1.58 \times 10^{-11} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 2.68 \times 10^{-5}} \text{ J}$$
$$E \approx 1.58 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J} \approx 98.7 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the rest-mass energy to obtain the kinetic energy.**
$$KE_{\min} = E - m_e c^2 \approx 98.7 \text{ MeV} - 0.511 \text{ MeV} \approx 98.2 \text{ MeV}$$
In SI units,
$$KE_{\min} \approx 1.57 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J}$$

> [!NOTE]
> *If one uses the non-relativistic formula $\text{KE} = p^2/(2m_e)$ for a quick estimate, the result is even larger:*
> $$\text{KE}_{\text{NR}} = \frac{(5.27 \times 10^{-20})^2}{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}} = \frac{2.78 \times 10^{-39}}{1.82 \times 10^{-30}} \approx 1.53 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J} \approx 9.5 \text{ GeV}$$
> *This gross overestimate flags the breakdown of the non-relativistic formula and reinforces the use of the relativistic expression.*

**Step 6 — Compare with realistic nuclear binding energies.**
Typical nuclear binding energies per particle are of the order of **8 MeV**, and nuclear well depths are of the order of **20–50 MeV**. The electron would need **~100 MeV** of kinetic energy to be confined — an order of magnitude greater than what the nuclear potential can supply. The assumption is impossible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** An electron *cannot* be a permanent constituent of the nucleus. The same calculation for a *proton* (with $m_p \approx 1836\, m_e$) yields a minimum KE of order $\sim 0.05$ MeV, which is perfectly consistent with nuclear binding energies. Hence nuclei are built of **protons and neutrons**, not electrons.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation II — Natural Linewidth of a Spectral Line

**Given:**
- An excited atomic state with mean lifetime $\tau$ (typically $10^{-8}$ s for an allowed electric-dipole transition).
- $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s

**Goal:** Find the natural width $\Delta \nu$ in Hz, and the corresponding $\Delta \lambda$ for a line at $\lambda = 500$ nm.

**Step 1 — Energy-time uncertainty.**
For a state that lives for a time $\tau$ before decaying,
$$\Delta E \cdot \tau \geq \frac{\hbar}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta E \geq \frac{\hbar}{2\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Convert energy spread to frequency spread.**
Since $E = h\nu$, we have $\Delta E = h \cdot \Delta \nu$, so
$$\Delta \nu = \frac{\Delta E}{h} = \frac{1}{4\pi \tau}$$

**Step 3 — Plug in the numbers.**
For $\tau = 10^{-8}$ s:
$$\Delta \nu = \frac{1}{4\pi \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-7}}$$
$$\Delta \nu \approx 7.96 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} \approx 8 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> *Some textbooks (older style) write the natural width using $\Delta \nu \approx 1/\tau$, dropping the $4\pi$. This is because they often use the *full width at half maximum* of the Lorentzian profile, $2 \Delta \nu = 1/(2\pi\tau) \cdot 2 = 1/\pi\tau \approx 1/\tau$ at order of magnitude. The factor of $4\pi$ vs $2\pi$ vs $1$ depends on whether one quotes the half-width or the full width and the precise definition. KTU expects $\Delta \nu \approx 1/(4\pi\tau)$ for the half-width, half-maximum definition.*

**Step 4 — Convert to wavelength width.**
From $\nu = c/\lambda$, we have $d\nu = -c/\lambda^2 \, d\lambda$, so
$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{\lambda^2}{c} \cdot \Delta \nu = \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi c \tau}$$
For $\lambda = 500 \text{ nm} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ m, $\tau = 10^{-8}$ s:
$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7})^2}{4\pi \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 10^{-8}}$$
$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-13}}{3.77 \times 10^{1}} = 6.63 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m} \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}$$
This is an extraordinarily sharp line — the *fractional* width is
$$\frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-15}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-8}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of magnitude:** Natural widths are $\sim 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-8}$ nm — *invisible* to ordinary lab spectrometers. Other broadening mechanisms (Doppler, collisional, instrumental) dominate in practice. Natural broadening is the *irreducible quantum floor*.

**Step 5 — Express the energy width in electron-volts.**
$$\Delta E = \frac{\hbar}{2\tau} = \frac{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.27 \times 10^{-27} \text{ J}$$
Converting to eV:
$$\Delta E = \frac{5.27 \times 10^{-27}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 3.3 \times 10^{-8} \text{ eV}$$

This is the *minimum* spread in photon energy — and it is the limit below which **no spectrometer can resolve**, no matter how good.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Quantity | Electron-in-Nucleus | Natural Linewidth |
|----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Underlying relation | $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \hbar/2$ | $\Delta E \cdot \Delta t \geq \hbar/2$ |
| Key confined variable | $\Delta x \approx 10^{-15}$ m | $\Delta t \approx \tau \approx 10^{-8}$ s |
| Computed spread | $p \sim 5 \times 10^{-20}$ kg·m/s | $\Delta E \sim 5 \times 10^{-27}$ J |
| Derived quantity | $KE_{\min} \sim 100$ MeV | $\Delta \nu \sim 8$ MHz, $\Delta \lambda \sim 10^{-14}$ m |
| Comparison with reality | $KE_{\min} \gg$ nuclear binding (few MeV) | $\Delta \nu \ll$ Doppler width ($\sim$ GHz) |
| Conclusion | Electron cannot be in nucleus | Spectral lines have a finite natural width |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Logic-Flow Diagram — Proof That Electron Cannot Exist in Nucleus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Assume electron is inside the nucleus] --> B[Nuclear size sets position uncertainty: Deltax approx 10 to the power of negative 15 m]
    B --> C[Apply Heisenberg principle: Deltap greater than or equal to hbar over 2 Deltax]
    C --> D[Compute minimum momentum: pmin approx 5.27 times 10 to the power of negative 20 kg m per s]
    D --> E[Check relativity: pmin c approx 98 MeV much greater than me c squared 0.511 MeV]
    E --> F[Use relativistic energy: E equals square root of p squared c squared plus me squared c to the fourth]
    F --> G[Kinetic energy KE equals E minus me c squared approx 98 MeV]
    G --> H[Compare with nuclear well depth approx 20 to 50 MeV]
    H --> I{Is KEmin less than Well depth?}
    I -- No --> J[Contradiction: assumption is unphysical]
    J --> K[Conclusion: Electron CANNOT exist inside the nucleus]
    I -- Yes --> L[Assumption would be valid]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Origin of Natural Linewidth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Excited atomic state with lifetime tau] --> B[System lives in state for time interval approx tau]
    B --> C[Energy-time uncertainty: DeltaE greater than or equal to hbar over 2 tau]
    C --> D[Photon carries this energy: E equals h nu]
    D --> E[Frequency spread: Delta nu equals 1 over 4 pi tau]
    E --> F[Lorentzian spectral profile]
    F --> G[Full Width at Half Maximum: 2 Delta nu]
    G --> H[This is the NATURAL LINEWIDTH]
    H --> I[Engineering impact: limits laser linewidth and atomic clock precision]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic — The Two Applications Side by Side

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph App1[Application 1: Electron in Nucleus]
        A1[Position-momentum uncertainty] --> A2[Nuclear size forces large Deltap]
        A2 --> A3[Huge required KE approx 100 MeV]
        A3 --> A4[Exceeds nuclear well depth]
        A4 --> A5[Electron is excluded from nucleus]
    end
    subgraph App2[Application 2: Natural Linewidth]
        B1[Energy-time uncertainty] --> B2[Finite lifetime tau of excited state]
        B2 --> B3[Emitted photon has energy spread DeltaE]
        B3 --> B4[Corresponding frequency spread Delta nu]
        B4 --> B5[Spectral line has natural linewidth]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, May 2024]**
*State Heisenberg's uncertainty principle in the position-momentum and energy-time forms. Using the position-momentum form, show that an electron cannot exist inside the nucleus.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Position-momentum form:** $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \hbar/2$. **(1 mark)**
- **Energy-time form:** $\Delta E \cdot \Delta t \geq \hbar/2$. **(1 mark)**
- **Application to nucleus:** Confining the electron to nuclear size $\Delta x \approx 10^{-15}$ m forces $\Delta p \geq \hbar/(2\Delta x) \approx 5 \times 10^{-20}$ kg·m/s, giving $KE_{\min} \approx 100$ MeV, which far exceeds the nuclear binding energy. Hence the electron cannot be confined. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
*Explain the phenomenon of natural broadening of spectral lines. Write the expression for the natural linewidth in terms of the mean lifetime of the excited state.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- An excited atomic state has a **finite mean lifetime** $\tau$ before spontaneous emission. **(1 mark)**
- By the energy-time uncertainty relation, $\Delta E \cdot \tau \geq \hbar/2$, so the emitted photon has an energy spread $\Delta E = \hbar/(2\tau)$, leading to a frequency spread. **(1 mark)**
- **Natural linewidth:** $\Delta \nu = 1/(4\pi\tau)$. This is the irreducible minimum width of any spectral line. **(1 mark)**

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Derive an expression for the minimum kinetic energy of an electron confined to a region of size equal to the diameter of a nucleus ($\sim 2 \times 10^{-15}$ m). Use relativistic mechanics and show that the answer rules out the existence of an electron inside the nucleus. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An excited atomic state has a mean lifetime of $10^{-8}$ s. Calculate (i) the natural linewidth $\Delta \nu$ in Hz, (ii) the natural width in wavelength $\Delta \lambda$ for a spectral line at $\lambda = 589$ nm (sodium D-line), and (iii) the energy width $\Delta E$ in eV. Comment on the relative magnitude compared to Doppler broadening. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1:* Position uncertainty $\Delta x = 2 \times 10^{-15}$ m. **(0.5 mark)**
*Step 2:* Using $\Delta p \geq \hbar/(2\Delta x)$,
$$p_{\min} = \frac{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-15}} = 2.64 \times 10^{-20} \text{ kg·m/s}$$ **(1 mark)**
*Step 3:* Check relativistic: $p_{\min} c = 7.91 \times 10^{-12}$ J $\approx 49.4$ MeV $\gg m_e c^2 = 0.511$ MeV. So we use relativistic formula. **(1 mark)**
*Step 4:*
$$E = \sqrt{p_{\min}^2 c^2 + m_e^2 c^4} \approx p_{\min} c = 7.91 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} \approx 49.4 \text{ MeV}$$ **(1.5 marks)**
*Step 5:* $KE_{\min} = E - m_e c^2 = 49.4 - 0.511 \approx 48.9$ MeV. **(1 mark)**
*Step 6:* Comparison: Nuclear well depth $\sim 20$–$50$ MeV. $KE_{\min}$ is at the *upper* edge or exceeds this; even ignoring this, the order of magnitude (tens of MeV) is much greater than the few-MeV binding typical of nuclear particles. The conclusion: **electrons cannot be permanently confined in nuclei**, motivating the discovery of the neutron. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Given:* $\tau = 10^{-8}$ s, $\lambda = 589$ nm $= 5.89 \times 10^{-7}$ m.

**(i) Frequency width:**
$$\Delta \nu = \frac{1}{4\pi \tau} = \frac{1}{4\pi \times 10^{-8}} \approx 7.96 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} \approx 8 \text{ MHz}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Wavelength width:**
$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi c \tau} = \frac{(5.89 \times 10^{-7})^2}{4\pi \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 10^{-8}}$$
$$= \frac{3.47 \times 10^{-13}}{3.77 \times 10^{1}} \approx 9.2 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m} \approx 9.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}$$ **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Energy width in eV:**
$$\Delta E = \frac{\hbar}{2\tau} = \frac{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.27 \times 10^{-27} \text{ J}$$
$$\Delta E = \frac{5.27 \times 10^{-27}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 3.29 \times 10^{-8} \text{ eV}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

*Comment:* Doppler broadening at $T = 500$ K gives $\Delta \nu_D \sim 10^9$ Hz (i.e., $\sim 1$ GHz), which is **~100 times larger** than the natural width. Hence in laboratory conditions, Doppler broadening dominates, and natural broadening is the *irreducible quantum floor* revealed only in low-temperature, collision-free environments (e.g., interstellar gas, laser-cooled atoms). **(1.5 marks)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** State and explain Heisenberg's uncertainty principle. A proton is confined to a nucleus of diameter $2 \times 10^{-15}$ m. Estimate its minimum kinetic energy. Comment on whether a similar calculation for an electron leads to a contradiction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the energy-time uncertainty relation, derive the natural linewidth of a spectral line. Show that the wavelength width is $\Delta \lambda = \lambda^2/(4\pi c\tau)$. A laser transition has $\tau = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ s (highly metastable). Calculate $\Delta \nu$ and $\Delta \lambda$ for $\lambda = 1.06\,\mu$m. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Statement of principle:* (refer to Section 1) **(1 mark)**

*Proton calculation:* $\Delta x = 2 \times 10^{-15}$ m, $m_p = 1.67 \times 10^{-27}$ kg. **(0.5 mark)**
$$p_{\min} = \frac{\hbar}{2\Delta x} = \frac{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}{4 \times 10^{-15}} = 2.64 \times 10^{-20} \text{ kg·m/s}$$ **(1 mark)**
Check non-relativistic validity: $p_{\min}^2/(2m_p) = (2.64 \times 10^{-20})^2/(2 \times 1.67 \times 10^{-27}) = 6.97 \times 10^{-40}/3.34 \times 10^{-27} = 2.09 \times 10^{-13}$ J $= 1.30$ MeV. Compare with $m_p c^2 = 938$ MeV, so non-relativistic is fine. **(1.5 marks)**
$$KE_{\min}^{\text{proton}} \approx 1.3 \text{ MeV}$$ **(1 mark)**

*Comment on electron:* Repeating with $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg (relativistic) gives $KE_{\min} \approx 49$ MeV — *much larger* than the nuclear well depth. The proton's required KE ($\sim 1$ MeV) is consistent with nuclear binding; the electron's is not. Hence nuclei contain protons (and neutrons), not electrons. **(2.5 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Derivation:* From $\Delta E \cdot \tau \geq \hbar/2$ and $E = h\nu$, we obtain $\Delta \nu = 1/(4\pi\tau)$. **(2 marks)**

*Wavelength form:* $\nu = c/\lambda$ implies $d\nu = -c/\lambda^2\, d\lambda$, so
$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{\lambda^2}{c}\Delta \nu = \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi c \tau}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

*Numerical:* $\tau = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ s, $\lambda = 1.06 \times 10^{-6}$ m. **(0.5 mark)**
$$\Delta \nu = \frac{1}{4\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 49.7 \text{ Hz}$$ **(1.5 marks)**
$$\Delta \lambda = \frac{(1.06 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4\pi \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.124 \times 10^{-12}}{6.03 \times 10^{6}} = 1.86 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

*Note:* Such an extraordinarily narrow natural linewidth is why metastable laser transitions (e.g., the He-Ne laser's 632.8 nm line) are prized for ultra-high-resolution spectroscopy. **(0 mark — bonus remark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting relativistic kinematics for the electron-in-nucleus problem:** Many students plug $p$ into the *non-relativistic* $p^2/(2m)$ formula, getting $\sim 10$ GeV. While this is *numerically larger* and doesn't change the conclusion, it is **physically wrong** and costs 1–2 marks. *Always* check $p c$ vs $m_e c^2$.
> 2. **Confusing natural linewidth with Doppler/collision broadening:** Natural broadening is a *quantum* effect from finite lifetime. Doppler broadening is *thermal*. Collisional (pressure) broadening is from atomic collisions. They are not the same.
> 3. **Wrong factor in $\Delta \nu$:** KTU prefers $\Delta \nu = 1/(4\pi\tau)$ for the *half-width at half-maximum* of the Lorentzian. Using $1/\tau$ or $1/(2\pi\tau)$ without context loses a mark. State clearly *which definition* you are using.
> 4. **Unit mismatches:** When $\Delta \lambda$ comes out very small ($\sim 10^{-14}$ m), students sometimes forget to convert from metres to nanometres, or mistakenly write the answer in Å without scaling.
> 5. **Sign convention in wavelength-width derivation:** $\Delta \lambda$ is *always positive*; the minus sign from the derivative is absorbed into the absolute value.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two faces of uncertainty principle**:
  - $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \hbar/2$ — *conjugate variables in space*.
  - $\Delta E \cdot \Delta t \geq \hbar/2$ — *energy and time*.

- **Electron in nucleus — key numbers**:
  - Nuclear size: $\sim 10^{-15}$ m.
  - Forced $p_{\min} \sim 5 \times 10^{-20}$ kg·m/s.
  - Relativistic $KE_{\min} \sim 50$–$100$ MeV.
  - **Conclusion:** *Electron cannot be a nuclear constituent* — a foundational argument that led to the *neutron hypothesis* (Chadwick, 1932).

- **Natural line broadening — key numbers**:
  - Atomic lifetime $\tau \sim 10^{-8}$ s for allowed E1 transitions.
  - $\Delta \nu = 1/(4\pi\tau) \sim 10^7$ Hz ($\sim 10$ MHz).
  - $\Delta \lambda \sim 10^{-14}$ m (extremely sharp).
  - $\Delta E \sim 10^{-8}$ eV.
  - This is the **irreducible quantum floor** below which no spectrometer can resolve.

- **Engineering relevance**:
  - Argues for *protons + neutrons* (not electrons) as nuclear building blocks.
  - Sets the *ultimate lower limit* on laser linewidth, atomic clock precision, and astronomical line-resolving power.
  - Other broadening (Doppler, collisional, instrumental) usually *dominates* over natural width in practice.

- **Quick comparison table for last-minute revision**:

| Quantity | Electron in Nucleus | Natural Linewidth |
|----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Uncertainty relation used | $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p$ | $\Delta E \cdot \Delta t$ |
| Confinement parameter | $\Delta x \sim 10^{-15}$ m | $\tau \sim 10^{-8}$ s |
| Computed spread | $p \sim 5 \times 10^{-20}$ kg·m/s | $\Delta E \sim 5 \times 10^{-27}$ J |
| Final derived quantity | $KE_{\min} \sim 100$ MeV | $\Delta \nu \sim 8$ MHz, $\Delta \lambda \sim 10^{-14}$ m |
| Real-world conclusion | Electrons excluded from nucleus | Spectral lines have a quantum-limited width |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Function - Properties - Physical Interpretation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GZPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wave Function - Properties - Physical Interpretation # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of the Wave Function In **Quantum Mechanics**, the **wave function** $\Psi(\vec{r}, t)$ is a complex-valued mathematical function that contains *all the dynamically accessible information* about an isolated quantum system. It is the fundamental descriptor of the state of a microscopic particle (electron, proton, photon, atom, etc.) in space and time. Mathematically, it is a solution to the **Schrödinger wave equation** (time-dependent or time-independent form). For a particle of mass $m$ moving in a potential $V(\vec{r}, t)$: $$\hat{H}\,\Psi(\vec{r},t) = i\hbar \frac{\partial \Psi(\vec{r},t)}{\partial t}$$ where $\hat{H}$ is the **Hamiltonian operator**: $$\hat{H} = -\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m}\nabla^{2} + V(\vec{r},t)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The wave function $\Psi$ itself is **NOT directly observable**. Only its modulus-squared $\vert \Psi \vert^{2}$ and combinations of operators acting on it yield physically measurable quantities. This is the cornerstone of **Copenhagen interpretation**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smoke Cloud" Picture Imagine you have a tiny invisible ghost confined inside a sealed room. You cannot see the ghost directly, but you can release a harmless smoke that the ghost pushes around. The **density of the disturbed smoke** tells you *where the ghost is most likely to be*. In quantum mechanics: - The **ghost** → the particle (e.g., an electron) - The **smoke density** → $\vert \Psi(\vec{r},t) \vert^{2}$ (probability density) - The **room** → the region of space accessible to the particle > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Unlike classical mechanics (where a particle has a definite position and momentum at every instant), the wave function only allows us to speak of **probabilities** of finding the particle. This is the essence of quantum indeterminacy. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Value / Meaning | |--------|----------|-----------------| | $\hbar$ | Reduced Planck's constant | $1.054 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s}$ | | $m_e$ | Electron rest mass | $9.11 \times 10^{-31}\,\text{kg}$ | | $i$ | Imaginary unit | $\sqrt{-1}$ | | $\Psi$ | Wave function | Complex scalar/vector function | | $\Psi^{*}$ | Complex conjugate of $\Psi$ | Used to compute $\vert \Psi \vert^{2} = \Psi^{*}\Psi$ | # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Born's Probability Interpretation (Max Born, 1926) The wave function is interpreted as a **probability amplitude**. The physically meaningful quantity is the **probability density**: $$\rho(\vec{r},t) = \Psi^{*}(\vec{r},t)\,\Psi(\vec{r},t) = \vert \Psi(\vec{r},t) \vert^{2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Born's Postulate:** $\vert \Psi(\vec{r},t) \vert^{2}\,dV$ represents the probability of finding the particle within the infinitesimal volume element $dV$ located at position $\vec{r}$ at time $t$. ## 2.2 The Normalization Condition Since the particle must be found *somewhere* in space, the **total probability equals unity**: $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert \Psi(\vec{r},t) \vert^{2}\, dV = 1$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A wave function that satisfies this condition is called **normalized** or **square-integrable**. If $\Psi$ is unnormalized, one can always construct a normalized form by dividing by the **normalization constant** $N$: $$\Psi_{\text{norm}} = \frac{\Psi}{\sqrt{\langle \Psi \vert \Psi \rangle}} \quad \text{where} \quad \langle \Psi \vert \Psi \rangle = \int \vert \Psi \vert^{2}\, dV$$ ## 2.3 The Five Mandatory Properties of an Acceptable Wave Function For $\Psi$ to represent a physically admissible quantum state, it must obey: | # | Property | Mathematical Statement | Physical Reason | |---|----------|----------------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Single-valued** | $\Psi(x)$ has one value at every $x$ | Probability density must be unique | | 2 | **Continuous** | $\Psi(x)$ is continuous everywhere | Ensures finite kinetic energy | | 3 | **Finite** | $\vert \Psi \vert < \infty$ everywhere | Probability cannot exceed 1 | | 4 | **Square-integrable** | $\int \vert \Psi \vert^{2}\,dV < \infty$ | Normalization must be possible | | 5 | **Smooth (Differentiable)** | $\partial \Psi/\partial x$ continuous (except where $V=\infty$) | Ensures finite momentum | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Trap:** A wave function with a *kink* (discontinuous first derivative) is **not admissible** at that point — examiners specifically check this in **boundary condition questions**. ## 2.4 Expectation Value of an Observable For any physical observable $\hat{A}$ (Hermitian operator), the **expectation (average) value** is: $$\langle A \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \Psi^{*}(\vec{r},t)\, \hat{A}\, \Psi(\vec{r},t)\, dV$$ > [!NOTE] > The expectation value is the **statistical average** of many identical measurements on identically prepared systems — NOT the result of a single measurement. ## 2.5 Orthogonality of Wave Functions Two distinct eigenstates $\Psi_m$ and $\Psi_n$ of a Hermitian operator are **orthogonal**: $$\int \Psi_m^{*}(\vec{r})\, \Psi_n(\vec{r})\, dV = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad m \neq n$$ ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Governing Equation | Units | |---|---------|-------------------|-------| | 1 | Probability density | $\rho = \Psi^{*}\Psi = \vert \Psi \vert^{2}$ | $\text{m}^{-3}$ (3D) or $\text{m}^{-1}$ (1D) | | 2 | Normalization | $\int \vert \Psi \vert^{2}\,dV = 1$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Probability in region $[a,b]$ | $P = \int_{a}^{b} \vert \Psi \vert^{2}\,dx$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Expectation value of position | $\langle x \rangle = \int \Psi^{*} x\, \Psi\, dx$ | m | | 5 | Expectation value of momentum | $\langle p \rangle = \int \Psi^{*}\!\left(-i\hbar\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\right)\!\Psi\,dx$ | kg·m/s | | 6 | Position uncertainty | $\Delta x = \sqrt{\langle x^{2}\rangle - \langle x\rangle^{2}}$ | m | | 7 | Momentum uncertainty | $\Delta p = \sqrt{\langle p^{2}\rangle - \langle p\rangle^{2}}$ | kg·m/s | | 8 | Heisenberg Uncertainty | $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \frac{\hbar}{2}$ | J·s | | 9 | Time-dependent Schrödinger | $i\hbar\,\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial t} = \hat{H}\Psi$ | — | | 10 | Time-independent Schrödinger | $\hat{H}\Psi = E\Psi$ | — | | 11 | Complex conjugate product | $\vert \Psi \vert^{2} = \Psi^{*}\Psi = (a-ib)(a+ib) = a^{2}+b^{2}$ | — | | 12 | Superposition principle | $\Psi = c_{1}\Psi_{1} + c_{2}\Psi_{2}$ | — | > [!TIP] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Wave function analysis underpins the design of **semiconductor devices** (MOSFETs, quantum dots), **lasers**, **MRI medical imaging**, and **scanning tunneling microscopes (STM)**. Every modern chip is engineered using solutions to the Schrödinger equation in a potential well. # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Normalization Constant for a Given Wave Function **Problem Setup:** A particle in 1D is described (unnormalized) by: $$\Psi(x) = N\,x\,(L-x) \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \leq x \leq L$$ and $\Psi(x) = 0$ elsewhere. Determine the normalization constant $N$. ### Step 1 — Apply the Normalization Condition The wave function is real, so $\Psi^{*}=\Psi$. Thus $\vert \Psi \vert^{2} = \Psi^{2}$. $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert \Psi \vert^{2}\, dx = 1$$ ### Step 2 — Reduce the Limits Using the Domain Since $\Psi = 0$ outside $[0, L]$, we only integrate over the non-zero region: $$\int_{0}^{L} N^{2}\,x^{2}(L-x)^{2}\, dx = 1$$ ### Step 3 — Expand the Polynomial Let us expand $x^{2}(L-x)^{2}$: $$ \begin{aligned} x^{2}(L-x)^{2} &= x^{2}\bigl(L^{2} - 2Lx + x^{2}\bigr) \\ &= L^{2}x^{2} - 2Lx^{3} + x^{4} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Integrate Term by Term $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{0}^{L} \bigl(L^{2}x^{2} - 2Lx^{3} + x^{4}\bigr)\,dx &= L^{2}\cdot\frac{x^{3}}{3} - 2L\cdot\frac{x^{4}}{4} + \frac{x^{5}}{5}\Biggr\vert_{0}^{L} \\ &= \frac{L^{5}}{3} - \frac{L^{5}}{2} + \frac{L^{5}}{5} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute the Common Denominator (LCD = 30) $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{L^{5}}{3} - \frac{L^{5}}{2} + \frac{L^{5}}{5} &= L^{5}\left(\frac{10 - 15 + 6}{30}\right) \\ &= L^{5}\left(\frac{1}{30}\right) \\ &= \frac{L^{5}}{30} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Solve for $N^{2}$ and Then $N$ $$N^{2} \cdot \frac{L^{5}}{30} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N^{2} = \frac{30}{L^{5}}$$ $$\boxed{\,N = \sqrt{\frac{30}{L^{5}}}\,}$$ The normalized wave function is therefore: $$\Psi(x) = \sqrt{\frac{30}{L^{5}}}\, x\,(L-x), \quad 0 \leq x \leq L$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Expectation Value $\langle x \rangle$ for the Above State ### Step 1 — Set Up the Integral $$ \begin{aligned} \langle x \rangle &= \int_{0}^{L} \Psi^{*}(x)\,x\,\Psi(x)\,dx \\ &= N^{2}\int_{0}^{L} x \cdot x^{2}(L-x)^{2}\,dx \\ &= N^{2}\int_{0}^{L} x^{3}(L-x)^{2}\,dx \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Expand $x^{3}(L-x)^{2}$ $$ \begin{aligned} x^{3}(L-x)^{2} &= x^{3}\bigl(L^{2} - 2Lx + x^{2}\bigr) \\ &= L^{2}x^{3} - 2Lx^{4} + x^{5} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Integrate $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{0}^{L}\bigl(L^{2}x^{3} - 2Lx^{4} + x^{5}\bigr)\,dx &= L^{2}\cdot\frac{L^{4}}{4} - 2L\cdot\frac{L^{5}}{5} + \frac{L^{6}}{6} \\ &= \frac{L^{6}}{4} - \frac{2L^{6}}{5} + \frac{L^{6}}{6} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — LCD = 60 $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{L^{6}}{4} - \frac{2L^{6}}{5} + \frac{L^{6}}{6} &= L^{6}\left(\frac{15 - 24 + 10}{60}\right) \\ &= L^{6}\left(\frac{1}{60}\right) \\ &= \frac{L^{6}}{60} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute $N^{2} = 30/L^{5}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \langle x \rangle &= \frac{30}{L^{5}} \cdot \frac{L^{6}}{60} = \frac{30 L}{60} = \frac{L}{2} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Physical Interpretation $$\boxed{\,\langle x \rangle = \frac{L}{2}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > The result $\langle x \rangle = L/2$ is the **geometric center** of the box, which is intuitively correct by symmetry — the wave function is symmetric about $x = L/2$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Probability of Finding the Particle in a Sub-Region **Question:** For the same normalized wave function $\Psi(x) = \sqrt{30/L^{5}}\, x(L-x)$, find the probability of finding the particle between $x = 0$ and $x = L/3$. ### Step 1 — Set Up the Probability Integral $$P\left(0 \leq x \leq \frac{L}{3}\right) = \int_{0}^{L/3} \vert \Psi \vert^{2}\, dx = \frac{30}{L^{5}}\int_{0}^{L/3} x^{2}(L-x)^{2}\,dx$$ ### Step 2 — Use the Antiderivative from Earlier From §3.1 Step 4, the antiderivative is $F(x) = \dfrac{L^{2}x^{3}}{3} - \dfrac{Lx^{4}}{2} + \dfrac{x^{5}}{5}$. ### Step 3 — Evaluate at the Bounds $$ \begin{aligned} F\!\left(\frac{L}{3}\right) &= \frac{L^{2}}{3}\cdot\frac{L^{3}}{27} - \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{L^{4}}{81} + \frac{1}{5}\cdot\frac{L^{5}}{243} \\ &= \frac{L^{5}}{81} - \frac{L^{5}}{162} + \frac{L^{5}}{1215} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — LCD = 2430 $$ \begin{aligned} F\!\left(\frac{L}{3}\right) &= L^{5}\!\left(\frac{30 - 15 + 2}{2430}\right) = \frac{17\,L^{5}}{2430} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Final Probability $$ \begin{aligned} P &= \frac{30}{L^{5}} \cdot \frac{17\,L^{5}}{2430} = \frac{30 \times 17}{2430} = \frac{510}{2430} = \frac{17}{81} \\ &\approx 0.2099 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\,P\!\left(0 \leq x \leq \tfrac{L}{3}\right) = \tfrac{17}{81} \approx 0.21 \text{ or } 21\%\,}$$ > [!TIP] > **Engineer's Sanity Check:** The interval $[0, L/3]$ is 1/3 of the box, but probability is **not** 1/3 (= 27/81). This is because $\vert \Psi \vert^{2} = 0$ at $x = 0$ and at $x = L$, so probability is suppressed near the walls. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Operator–Observable Correspondence | Classical Quantity | Quantum Operator $\hat{A}$ | Acting on $\Psi$ | |-------------------|---------------------------|-----------------| | Position $x$ | $\hat{x} = x$ (multiplication) | $\hat{x}\Psi = x\Psi$ | | Momentum $p$ | $\hat{p} = -i\hbar\,\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}$ | $\hat{p}\Psi = -i\hbar\,\dfrac{\partial \Psi}{\partial x}$ | | Kinetic energy $T$ | $\hat{T} = -\dfrac{\hbar^{2}}{2m}\,\dfrac{\partial^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}$ | $\hat{T}\Psi = -\dfrac{\hbar^{2}}{2m}\,\dfrac{\partial^{2}\Psi}{\partial x^{2}}$ | | Total energy $E$ | Hamiltonian $\hat{H} = \hat{T} + V(\hat{x})$ | $\hat{H}\Psi = E\Psi$ | | Angular momentum $L_z$ | $\hat{L}_{z} = -i\hbar\,\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \phi}$ | $\hat{L}_{z}\Psi = m_{l}\hbar\,\Psi$ | > [!NOTE] > **The Eigenvalue Equation:** $\hat{A}\Psi_{n} = a_{n}\Psi_{n}$ — every measurable quantity emerges as an *eigenvalue* $a_{n}$ of a Hermitian operator acting on its eigenstate $\Psi_{n}$. # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — How a Wave Function Becomes a Measurable Quantity ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Quantum System
Particle in Potential V"] --> B["Schrodinger Equation
i hbar dPsi/dt = H Psi"] B --> C["Wave Function
Psi r comma t"] C --> D["Born Interpretation
|Psi|^2 = Probability Density"] D --> E["Normalization Check
Integral |Psi|^2 dV = 1"] E --> F["Operator Action
A-hat acting on Psi"] F --> G["Expectation Value
= Integral Psi* A Psi dV"] G --> H["Measurable Observable
Position, Energy, Momentum"] C --> I["Valid Wave Function?
Continuous, Single-Valued, Finite, Square Integrable"] I --> J{All Properties Satisfied?} J -->|Yes| K["Physical State
Admissible"] J -->|No| L["Reject Psi
Non-Physical State"] ``` ## 4.2 Hierarchy of Wave Function Properties ```mermaid graph TD P1["Acceptable Wave Function Psi"] --> P2["Mathematical Conditions"] P1 --> P3["Physical Conditions"] P2 --> P2a["Single Valued"] P2 --> P2b["Continuous Function"] P2 --> P2c["Finite at all points"] P2 --> P2d["First Derivative Continuous
except at infinite V"] P2 --> P2e["Square Integrable
Integral |Psi|^2 dV is finite"] P3 --> P3a["Normalization
Integral |Psi|^2 dV = 1"] P3 --> P3b["Born Interpretation
|Psi|^2 is Probability Density"] P3 --> P3c["Linear Superposition
Psi = c1 Psi1 + c2 Psi2"] P3 --> P3d["Orthogonal Eigenstates
Integral Psi_m* Psi_n dV = 0"] ``` ## 4.3 Operator–Observable Mapping Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR CL["Classical Variable
x, p, E, L"] --> QO["Quantum Operator
x-hat, p-hat, H-hat, L-hat"] QO --> EE["Eigenvalue Equation
A-hat Psi_n = a_n Psi_n"] EE --> OBS["Observable
Definite value a_n"] PSI["Wave Function Psi"] --> EE PSI --> EXV["Expectation Value
= "] EXV --> STAT["Statistical Average
of many measurements"] ``` # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State and explain Born's probability interpretation of the wave function. Why is $\Psi$ itself not directly observable?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Born's interpretation (1926) states that $\vert \Psi(\vec{r},t) \vert^{2}\,dV$ gives the probability of finding the particle inside volume element $dV$ around $\vec{r}$ at time $t$. The wave function $\Psi$ is a **complex quantity** (carries a phase), and physical observables (position, energy, momentum) are **real numbers**. A complex number cannot be directly measured. Only the **modulus squared** $\vert \Psi \vert^{2}$ is real and corresponds to a measurable probability density. Hence $\Psi$ is called a *probability amplitude*, not a directly measurable field. > **Marking Scheme:** Statement of Born's rule (1 mark), explanation of $\vert \Psi \vert^{2}$ as probability density (1 mark), reasoning why $\Psi$ alone is unobservable (1 mark). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any four conditions that a wave function must satisfy to represent a physically admissible quantum state.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A valid wave function $\Psi$ must be: 1. **Single-valued** — unique value of $\vert \Psi \vert^{2}$ at every point. 2. **Continuous** — no jumps in $\Psi$ across space. 3. **Finite** — $\vert \Psi \vert$ must not diverge to infinity anywhere. 4. **Square-integrable** — $\int \vert \Psi \vert^{2}\,dV$ must be finite so the wave function can be normalized. (A 5th acceptable answer: the **first derivative $\partial \Psi/\partial x$** must be continuous everywhere except at points where the potential $V$ is infinite — this is the boundary condition for an *infinite* potential wall.) --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Define the wave function. Derive the expression for the **normalization condition** and explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a particle confined in a 1D infinite potential well of width $L$, the normalized wave function in the ground state is: $$\Psi_{1}(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right), \quad 0 \leq x \leq L$$ Calculate: 1. The probability of finding the particle in the region $0 \leq x \leq L/4$. 2. The expectation value $\langle x \rangle$. 3. The expectation value $\langle p \rangle$. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A ##### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Definition (2 marks):** A wave function $\Psi(\vec{r},t)$ is a complex-valued function that completely describes the quantum state of a particle. It is a solution of the Schrödinger equation. **Normalization Condition — Derivation (3 marks):** According to Born's interpretation, the probability of finding the particle in volume $dV$ around $\vec{r}$ is $\vert \Psi \vert^{2}\,dV$. The particle must be found *somewhere* in all space, hence the total probability equals unity: $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert \Psi(\vec{r},t) \vert^{2}\, dV = 1$$ This is the **normalization condition**. A wave function satisfying this is said to be **normalized**. **Physical Significance (2 marks):** - Guarantees that the *particle exists* (probability sum = 100%). - Allows calculation of expectation values and probabilities of detection in any region. - Imposes a constraint that restricts the allowed energy eigenvalues. --- ##### Part (b) — 7 Marks **(i) Probability in $[0, L/4]$ (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} P\!\left(0 \leq x \leq \tfrac{L}{4}\right) &= \int_{0}^{L/4} \frac{2}{L}\,\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right) dx \\ &= \frac{2}{L}\int_{0}^{L/4} \frac{1 - \cos(2\pi x /L)}{2}\,dx \\ &= \frac{1}{L}\left[x - \frac{L}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{L}\right)\right]_{0}^{L/4} \\ &= \frac{1}{L}\left[\frac{L}{4} - \frac{L}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right] \\ &= \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{2\pi} \\ &\approx 0.25 - 0.1592 \\ &\approx 0.0908 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\,P \approx 0.091 \text{ or } 9.08\%\,}$$ **(ii) Expectation value $\langle x \rangle$ (2 marks):** By symmetry of $\sin^{2}(\pi x/L)$ about $x = L/2$: $$\langle x \rangle = \frac{L}{2}$$ **Formal check:** $\langle x \rangle = \int_{0}^{L} \Psi^{*} x \Psi\, dx = \dfrac{2}{L}\int_{0}^{L} x\sin^{2}(\pi x/L)\,dx = \dfrac{L}{2}$ (by standard integral). **(iii) Expectation value $\langle p \rangle$ (2 marks):** For a real, stationary state, $\langle p \rangle = 0$. Formally: $$ \begin{aligned} \langle p \rangle &= \int_{0}^{L} \Psi^{*}\!\left(-i\hbar \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\right)\!\Psi\,dx \\ &= -i\hbar\cdot\frac{2}{L}\cdot\frac{\pi}{L}\int_{0}^{L}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{L}\right)dx \\ &= -i\hbar\cdot\frac{\pi}{L^{2}}\int_{0}^{L}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{L}\right)dx \\ &= -i\hbar\cdot\frac{\pi}{L^{2}}\left[-\frac{L}{2\pi}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{L}\right)\right]_{0}^{L} \\ &= -i\hbar\cdot\frac{\pi}{L^{2}}\left(-\frac{L}{2\pi}\right)(1 - 1) = 0 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\,\langle p \rangle = 0\,}$$ > **Physical meaning:** In a stationary bound state, the particle has equal probability of moving left or right — **zero net momentum**, consistent with energy conservation. --- ### **🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the **normalization** and **orthogonality** of wave functions. A particle is described by the unnormalized wave function $\Psi(x) = A\,e^{-\alpha x^{2}}$ for $-\infty < x < +\infty$, where $\alpha > 0$. Find the normalization constant $A$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the normalized wave function from part (a), calculate the **expectation value $\langle x \rangle$** and **position uncertainty $\Delta x$**. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** > [!NOTE] > **Standard Gaussian Integral Required:** $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-\alpha x^{2}} dx = \sqrt{\pi/\alpha}$, $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x^{2} e^{-\alpha x^{2}} dx = \dfrac{1}{2\alpha}\sqrt{\pi/\alpha}$. --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B ##### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Normalization definition (1 mark):** A wave function is normalized if $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert \Psi \vert^{2}\,dx = 1$. **Orthogonality definition (1 mark):** Two wave functions $\Psi_m$ and $\Psi_n$ are orthogonal if $\int \Psi_m^{*}\Psi_n\,dx = 0$ for $m \neq n$. **Finding $A$ (5 marks):** The wave function is real, so: $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} A^{2} e^{-2\alpha x^{2}}\,dx = 1$$ Using the standard integral $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-\beta x^{2}}dx = \sqrt{\pi/\beta}$ with $\beta = 2\alpha$: $$ \begin{aligned} A^{2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\alpha}} &= 1 \\ A^{2} &= \sqrt{\frac{2\alpha}{\pi}} \\ A &= \left(\frac{2\alpha}{\pi}\right)^{1/4} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\,A = \left(\tfrac{2\alpha}{\pi}\right)^{1/4}\,}$$ ##### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Computing $\langle x \rangle$ (3 marks):** The integrand $x\,e^{-2\alpha x^{2}}$ is an **odd function** of $x$ (even function $e^{-2\alpha x^2}$ times odd $x$), integrated over symmetric limits $[-\infty, +\infty]$: $$\langle x \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \Psi^{*} x \Psi\,dx = A^{2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x\,e^{-2\alpha x^{2}}dx = 0$$ **Computing $\langle x^{2} \rangle$ (2 marks):** Using $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x^{2} e^{-\beta x^{2}}dx = \dfrac{1}{2\beta}\sqrt{\pi/\beta}$ with $\beta = 2\alpha$: $$ \begin{aligned} \langle x^{2} \rangle &= A^{2}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x^{2} e^{-2\alpha x^{2}}dx \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{2\alpha}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{2(2\alpha)}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\alpha}} \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{2\alpha}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{4\alpha} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\alpha}} \\ &= \frac{1}{4\alpha} \end{aligned} $$ **Position uncertainty (1 mark):** $$\Delta x = \sqrt{\langle x^{2}\rangle - \langle x\rangle^{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4\alpha} - 0} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\alpha}}$$ **Comment (1 mark):** The Gaussian wave packet is *symmetric* about the origin, hence $\langle x \rangle = 0$. The width of the wave packet is governed by $\alpha$: larger $\alpha \Rightarrow$ narrower wave function in position space, which (via the Heisenberg principle) implies a *broader* momentum distribution. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting the complex conjugate** — students often write $\int \Psi^{2}\,dx$ instead of $\int \Psi^{*}\Psi\,dx$. Always use $\Psi^{*}\Psi$ in the normalization integral. (–1 mark per occurrence) > 2. **Confusing $\langle x \rangle$ with the most probable position** — for a Gaussian both are 0, but in general they are *different*. $\langle x \rangle$ is the average; the most probable $x$ is where $\vert \Psi \vert^{2}$ peaks. (–1 mark) > 3. **Skipping limits** — students often integrate from $0$ to $L$ even for unbounded problems. Always verify the domain of the wave function first. (–1 mark) > 4. **Not checking the wave function is square-integrable** — for the Gaussian $e^{-\alpha x^2}$ with $\alpha > 0$, it *is* square-integrable; for $e^{+\alpha x^{2}}$ it is *not*. (–1 mark) > 5. **Wrong dimension of $A$** — for a 1D wave function $\Psi$, $[A] = \text{m}^{-1/2}$. Mentioning this shows deeper understanding and earns an extra mark. --- ## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid-Fire Summary — read this the night before the exam.** ### 🔑 Key Definitions - **Wave function $\Psi(\vec{r},t)$** → complex solution of the Schrödinger equation; encodes *all* quantum information about a particle. - **Probability density** → $\rho = \vert \Psi \vert^{2} = \Psi^{*}\Psi$ (always real and non-negative). - **Normalization** → $\int \vert \Psi \vert^{2}\,dV = 1$ (particle must be found *somewhere*). - **Expectation value** → $\langle A \rangle = \int \Psi^{*}\hat{A}\Psi\,dV$ (statistical average of many identical measurements). - **Orthogonality** → $\int \Psi_m^{*}\Psi_n\,dV = 0$ for $m \neq n$. ### ✅ The 5 Mandatory Properties 1. Single-valued 2. Continuous 3. Finite 4. Square-integrable 5. Smooth (first derivative continuous, except at $V = \infty$) ### 🧠 Frequently Tested Points - **Why is $\Psi$ complex?** Because it carries phase information needed for interference effects (e.g., double-slit experiment). The phase is unobservable directly, but *differences* in phase produce observable interference. - **Physical meaning of $\hbar$ in Schrödinger eq.** Sets the scale at which quantum effects become significant. - **Real wave function** $\Rightarrow$ $\langle p \rangle = 0$ (zero net momentum). - **Stationary state** $\Rightarrow$ $\vert \Psi(\vec{r},t) \vert^{2}$ is time-independent. - **Normalization constant** units depend on dimension: $\text{m}^{-1/2}$ (1D), $\text{m}^{-1}$ (2D), $\text{m}^{-3/2}$ (3D). ### ⚡ Common Exam Traps - Missing the $\Psi^{*}\Psi$ rule for probability (use $|\Psi|^2$, never $\Psi^2$ alone). - Forgetting to verify all 5 properties when checking admissibility. - Confusing *probability* with *probability density* (latter is $\vert \Psi \vert^{2}$; former is the integral over a region). - Applying normalization in 3D but integrating only over 1D coordinates (use $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$ or $r^{2}\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$). ### 🎯 One-Line Takeaway > **The wave function is a complex probability amplitude; only $\vert \Psi \vert^{2}$ is observable, and it gives the probability density of finding the particle — provided $\Psi$ is single-valued, continuous, finite, square-integrable, and smooth.**

topics.title: Formulation of Time-Dependent and Time-Independent Schrödinger Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Formulation of Time-Dependent and Time-Independent Schrödinger Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Schrödinger Equation** is the fundamental wave equation of non-relativistic quantum mechanics, postulated by Erwin Schrödinger in 1925-26. It governs the temporal and spatial evolution of the quantum-mechanical state of a physical system. It exists in two principal forms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE):** A first-order partial differential equation in time that describes how the quantum state $\Psi(\mathbf{r}, t)$ of a non-relativistic physical system evolves with time under the action of a Hamiltonian operator $\hat{H}$.
>
> $$i\hbar \frac{\partial \Psi(\mathbf{r}, t)}{\partial t} = \hat{H}\,\Psi(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation (TISE):** An eigenvalue equation obtained by separating time and space variables from the TDSE, used to determine stationary states and allowed energy eigenvalues of a quantum system with time-independent potential.
>
> $$\hat{H}\,\psi(\mathbf{r}) = E\,\psi(\mathbf{r})$$

Here:
- $\hbar = h/2\pi = \mathbf{1.054 \times 10^{-34}\ J\cdot s}$ is the **reduced Planck's constant**
- $i = \sqrt{-1}$ is the imaginary unit
- $\hat{H}$ is the **Hamiltonian operator** (total energy operator)
- $E$ is the **energy eigenvalue**
- $\Psi(\mathbf{r}, t)$ is the **time-dependent wave function**
- $\psi(\mathbf{r})$ is the **time-independent wave function** (spatial part only)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are a smoke detector in a closed room:** The smoke (quantum particle) does not follow a precise path like a ball. Instead, it spreads as a "cloud of probability" whose density at every location and instant is governed by a single master equation — the Schrödinger equation. Just as the heat equation tells you how temperature evolves in a rod, the Schrödinger equation tells you how this probability cloud ripples, interferes, and settles into standing patterns (eigenstates) when the system reaches a steady configuration (stationary state).

In simpler terms:
- The **TDSE** is like a *movie* of the quantum system — it tells you how the wave function changes frame by frame.
- The **TISE** is like a *photograph* — it tells you the spatial pattern of the wave function when the system is in a state of definite energy (a stationary state, where the probability density $\vert\Psi\vert^2$ does not change with time).

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants (Bolded)

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Planck's constant | $h$ | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$** |
| Reduced Planck's constant | $\hbar$ | **$1.054 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$** |
| Electron rest mass | $m_e$ | **$9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$** |
| Speed of light | $c$ | **$2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Schrödinger equation is a **postulate** of quantum mechanics — it is *not* derived from classical mechanics alone. Its validity is established by the extraordinary agreement of its predictions (hydrogen spectrum, tunneling, semiconductor band structure) with experiment. However, its **formulation** can be motivated starting from the classical wave equation plus the de Broglie relations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is a New Equation Needed? (The Logical Foundation)

Classical mechanics is built on **Newton's second law** ($F = ma$) and uses the concepts of position and momentum as continuous, simultaneously measurable variables. However, the following experimental facts demand a radical reformulation:

- **Wave-particle duality** (de Broglie, 1924): Every particle of momentum $p$ has an associated wavelength $\lambda = h/p$.
- **Heisenberg's uncertainty principle**: $\Delta x\,\Delta p \geq \hbar/2$ — sharp trajectories are physically meaningless.
- **Quantization** (atomic spectra, photoelectric effect, blackbody radiation): Energy exchange occurs in discrete packets.
- **Double-slit interference** with single electrons: A single particle interferes with itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Insight:** The classical wave equation governs *observable* waves (sound, light, water). Quantum mechanics, however, requires a wave equation that governs a **probability amplitude** $\Psi$ whose **modulus squared** $\vert\Psi\vert^2$ gives the probability density of finding the particle.

### 2.2 The De Broglie Bridge: From Classical Waves to Quantum Waves

The formulation of the Schrödinger equation requires bridging three ingredients:

**Step 1 — The Plane-Wave Template (Classical Wave Equation):**
A monochromatic, one-dimensional classical wave is described by

$$\Psi(x, t) = A\,e^{i(kx - \omega t)}$$

where $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wave number and $\omega = 2\pi\nu$ is the angular frequency.

**Step 2 — De Broglie Relations (Wave-Particle Duality):**
Every particle of energy $E$ and momentum $p$ behaves as a wave with:

$$E = \hbar\omega \quad \text{and} \quad p = \hbar k$$

**Step 3 — Operational Correspondence (Energy-Momentum Operator Substitution):**
In the plane-wave ansatz, taking partial derivatives gives:

$$-i\hbar\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial x} = p\,\Psi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{p}_x \equiv -i\hbar\frac{\partial}{\partial x}$$

$$i\hbar\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial t} = E\,\Psi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat{E} \equiv i\hbar\frac{\partial}{\partial t}$$

These define the **momentum operator** and **energy operator** respectively. This is the famous *first quantization* recipe.

### 2.3 The Total Energy Constraint

For a non-relativistic particle of mass $m$ moving in a potential $V(x)$, the classical total energy (Hamiltonian) is:

$$E = \frac{p^2}{2m} + V(x)$$

Substituting the operator forms $\hat{E} \rightarrow i\hbar\partial/\partial t$, $\hat{p}^2 \rightarrow -\hbar^2\partial^2/\partial x^2$, and applying them to $\Psi$ yields the **TDSE**:

$$i\hbar\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial t} = \left[-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2} + V(x)\right]\Psi$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Schrödinger equation is the operational backbone of **semiconductor device physics** (band-gap engineering, MOSFET design), **quantum computing** (qubit dynamics), **scanning tunneling microscopy** (STM), **laser technology**, and **materials science** (defect states, photovoltaic cells). A B.Tech engineer specializing in Electronics, Electrical, or Materials Engineering will encounter Schrödinger-based models repeatedly.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE TO STUDENT:** All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-priority and must be memorized with their associated conditions.

| # | Equation | Name / Description | Conditions / Units |
|---|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $i\hbar \frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial t} = \hat{H}\Psi$ | Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE) | 1-D, non-relativistic |
| 2 | $\hat{H} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2}{dx^2} + V(x)$ | Hamiltonian Operator (1-D) | Energy units: joule (J) |
| 3 | $-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + V(x)\psi = E\psi$ | Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation (TISE) | Stationary states, $V \neq V(t)$ |
| 4 | $\Psi(x,t) = \psi(x)\,e^{-iEt/\hbar}$ | Stationary state factorization | $\psi(x)$ is real, separable |
| 5 | $\vert\Psi(x,t)\vert^2 = \vert\psi(x)\vert^2$ | Time-independent probability density | Stationary state |
| 6 | $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert\Psi\vert^2\,dx = 1$ | Normalization condition | $\Psi$ is square-integrable |
| 7 | $\hat{p}_x = -i\hbar \frac{\partial}{\partial x}$ | Momentum operator (1-D) | Position representation |
| 8 | $\hat{E} = i\hbar \frac{\partial}{\partial t}$ | Energy operator | Time-domain |
| 9 | $E_n = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2mL^2}$ | Infinite well energy (verification example) | $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ |
| 10 | $\lambda = h/p$ | de Broglie wavelength | All matter waves |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Semiconductor Industry:** The TISE governs electron behavior in quantum wells, leading to **laser diodes** and **high-electron-mobility transistors (HEMTs)**.
- **Medical Imaging:** **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** relies on quantum mechanical spin precession equations.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** **Drug delivery** systems exploit quantum tunneling through biological membranes.
- **Renewable Energy:** **Solar cell** efficiency depends on quantum transition rates derived from the Schrödinger framework.
- **Quantum Computing:** Qubit logic gates are implemented by precisely timed electromagnetic pulses that drive TDSE evolution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The derivation below is the most common 7-mark derivation in KTU Module 3. Mastering every line is non-negotiable.

#### Step 1: Start with the Classical 1-D Wave Equation

For a classical wave propagating along the $x$-axis with speed $v$, the displacement $y(x,t)$ obeys:

$$\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{v^2}\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2}$$

#### Step 2: Propose a Plane-Wave Solution

The most elementary solution is a monochromatic plane wave:

$$y(x,t) = A\,e^{i(kx - \omega t)}$$

Here, $A$ is amplitude, $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wave number, and $\omega = 2\pi\nu$ is the angular frequency.

#### Step 3: Compute the Required Partial Derivatives

Differentiating once with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = ik\,A\,e^{i(kx - \omega t)} = ik\,y$$

Differentiating again with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2} = (ik)(ik)\,y = -k^2\,y$$

Similarly, differentiating twice with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial t} = -i\omega\,y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = -\omega^2\,y$$

#### Step 4: Substitute into the Classical Wave Equation

$$-k^2\,y = \frac{1}{v^2}(-\omega^2\,y)$$

$$\omega^2 = v^2 k^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = v k$$

This is trivially satisfied — the plane wave solves the classical equation. *But this is just a number equation*; it does not yet yield a quantum wave equation.

#### Step 5: Introduce the de Broglie (Wave-Particle) Relations

For a quantum particle (electron, proton, etc.) of energy $E$ and momentum $p$:

$$E = \hbar\omega \quad \text{and} \quad p = \hbar k$$

These are the **de Broglie-Einstein relations**. Substituting into $\omega = vk$:

$$E = v\,p \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{E}{p}$$

This is the classical relation $E = p^2/(2m)$ in disguise for a free particle. Indeed, $v = p/(2m)$ if $E = p^2/2m$.

#### Step 6: Reformulate the Derivatives Using Quantum Operators

For the plane wave $y(x,t) = Ae^{i(kx - \omega t)}$:

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = ik\,y = \frac{i\,p}{\hbar}\,y \quad \Rightarrow \quad p\,y = -i\hbar\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}$$

This motivates defining the **momentum operator** $\hat{p}_x \equiv -i\hbar\,\partial/\partial x$, acting on any wave function.

Similarly:

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial t} = -i\omega\,y = -\frac{i\,E}{\hbar}\,y \quad \Rightarrow \quad E\,y = i\hbar\frac{\partial y}{\partial t}$$

This defines the **energy operator** $\hat{E} \equiv i\hbar\,\partial/\partial t$.

#### Step 7: Apply the Classical Energy Constraint to the Quantum Operators

For a particle of mass $m$ in a potential $V(x)$, the total energy is:

$$E = \frac{p^2}{2m} + V(x)$$

Replace classical observables with operators acting on a generic wave function $\Psi(x,t)$:

$$i\hbar\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial t} = \left[-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2} + V(x)\right]\Psi$$

This is the **one-dimensional Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE)**.

#### Step 8: Generalize to Three Dimensions

In three Cartesian dimensions, the Laplacian $\nabla^2$ replaces $\partial^2/\partial x^2$:

$$i\hbar\frac{\partial \Psi(\mathbf{r},t)}{\partial t} = \left[-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla^2 + V(\mathbf{r})\right]\Psi(\mathbf{r},t)$$

where $\nabla^2 = \partial^2/\partial x^2 + \partial^2/\partial y^2 + \partial^2/\partial z^2$. This is the **general TDSE**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why first-order in time?** Unlike the classical wave equation (second-order in $t$), the Schrödinger equation is first-order in $t$. This guarantees that knowing $\Psi$ at one instant fully determines it at all later times — a property of unitary (probability-conserving) evolution.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation (TISE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This is the second most-tested derivation in KTU Module 3 (typically worth 7 marks).

#### Step 1: Begin with the TDSE

$$i\hbar\frac{\partial \Psi(x,t)}{\partial t} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{\partial^2 \Psi}{\partial x^2} + V(x)\Psi$$

Assume the potential is **time-independent**, i.e., $V(x,t) = V(x)$.

#### Step 2: Use the Method of Separation of Variables

Propose that $\Psi(x,t)$ can be written as a product of independent functions of $x$ and $t$:

$$\Psi(x,t) = \psi(x)\,\phi(t)$$

where $\psi$ depends only on $x$ and $\phi$ depends only on $t$.

#### Step 3: Compute the Partial Derivatives

$$\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial t} = \psi(x)\,\frac{d\phi}{dt}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \Psi}{\partial x^2} = \phi(t)\,\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2}$$

#### Step 4: Substitute into the TDSE

$$i\hbar\,\psi(x)\,\frac{d\phi}{dt} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\phi(t)\,\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + V(x)\psi(x)\phi(t)$$

#### Step 5: Divide Both Sides by $\psi(x)\phi(t)$

$$i\hbar\,\frac{1}{\phi(t)}\frac{d\phi}{dt} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{1}{\psi(x)}\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + V(x)$$

#### Step 6: Recognize That the Two Sides Are Functions of Independent Variables

The left side depends **only on $t$**; the right side depends **only on $x$**. The only way a function of $t$ can equal a function of $x$ for all $(x,t)$ is if **both sides equal a constant**. This constant has dimensions of energy; call it $E$.

$$i\hbar\,\frac{1}{\phi}\frac{d\phi}{dt} = E \quad \text{(Temporal equation)}$$

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + V(x)\psi = E\psi \quad \text{(Spatial equation)}$$

#### Step 7: Solve the Temporal Equation

$$i\hbar\,\frac{d\phi}{dt} = E\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\phi}{\phi} = -\frac{iE}{\hbar}dt$$

Integrating both sides:

$$\ln\phi = -\frac{iE}{\hbar}t + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi(t) = Ce^{-iEt/\hbar}$$

Setting $C = 1$ (absorbed into $\psi$):

$$\phi(t) = e^{-iEt/\hbar}$$

#### Step 8: Combine to Recover the Stationary State

$$\Psi(x,t) = \psi(x)\,e^{-iEt/\hbar}$$

The probability density is then:

$$\vert\Psi(x,t)\vert^2 = \Psi^*\Psi = \psi^*e^{+iEt/\hbar}\,\psi\,e^{-iEt/\hbar} = \vert\psi(x)\vert^2$$

This is **time-independent** — hence the name *stationary state*. The phase factor $e^{-iEt/\hbar}$ carries the time dependence but does not affect the probability density.

#### Step 9: Re-state the Spatial Equation (TISE)

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2\psi(x)}{dx^2} + V(x)\psi(x) = E\psi(x)$$

In operator form:

$$\hat{H}\psi = E\psi$$

This is an **eigenvalue equation**: the Hamiltonian operator acting on the wave function $\psi$ returns the same wave function multiplied by the energy eigenvalue $E$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of the TISE:** The TISE is the quantum analogue of **finding the normal modes of a vibrating string**. The allowed $E$ values are the system's *energy eigenvalues*, and the corresponding $\psi(x)$ are the *eigenfunctions*. Boundary conditions quantize the spectrum — for the infinite well, $V = 0$ inside and $V = \infty$ outside, yielding $E_n = n^2\pi^2\hbar^2/(2mL^2)$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Normalization of a Gaussian Wave Packet

**Problem:** A particle is in the state $\Psi(x,0) = A\,e^{-x^2/2a^2}$ at $t=0$. Find $A$ and the probability that the particle is found between $-a$ and $+a$.

**Solution — Step 1:** Apply the normalization condition:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert\Psi\vert^2 dx = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert A\vert^2 \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-x^2/a^2}dx = 1$$

**Solution — Step 2:** Use the standard Gaussian integral $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-x^2/a^2}dx = a\sqrt{\pi}$:

$$\vert A\vert^2 \cdot a\sqrt{\pi} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \left(\frac{1}{a\sqrt{\pi}}\right)^{1/2}$$

**Solution — Step 3:** Probability of finding the particle in $-a \leq x \leq a$:

$$P = \int_{-a}^{+a} \vert\Psi\vert^2 dx = \vert A\vert^2\int_{-a}^{+a} e^{-x^2/a^2}dx = \frac{1}{a\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{-a}^{+a} e^{-x^2/a^2}dx$$

Using substitution $u = x/a$, $du = dx/a$, the limits become $\pm 1$:

$$P = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_{-1}^{+1} e^{-u^2}du = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_0^1 e^{-u^2}du = \text{erf}(1) \approx 0.8427$$

where $\text{erf}$ is the **error function**. Therefore, $P \approx \mathbf{84.27\%}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Gaussian?** A Gaussian wave packet minimizes the Heisenberg uncertainty product $\Delta x\,\Delta p = \hbar/2$ and is the most "classical-like" quantum state. It is widely used to model free electrons in beam experiments.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Logical Flow of Schrödinger Equation Formulation

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the *pedagogical* derivation pipeline of both TDSE and TISE from classical wave mechanics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Classical Wave Equation d2y/dx2 equals 1 by v2 times d2y/dt2] --> B[Plane Wave Solution y equals A exp i k x minus omega t]
    B --> C[Compute Spatial and Temporal Derivatives]
    C --> D[Apply de Broglie Relations E equals hbar omega and p equals hbar k]
    D --> E[Define Quantum Operators p hat equals minus i hbar d by dx and E hat equals i hbar d by dt]
    E --> F[Use Classical Energy Constraint E equals p squared by 2m plus V x]
    F --> G[Replace Observables with Operators acting on Psi]
    G --> H[TDSE i hbar dPsi by dt equals H hat Psi]
    H --> I[Assume Separation Psi equals psi x times phi t]
    I --> J[Spatial part yields TISE H hat psi equals E psi]
    I --> K[Temporal part yields phi t equals exp minus i E t by hbar]
    J --> L[Solve with Boundary Conditions]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Energy Eigenvalues E n and Eigenfunctions psi n x]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: TDSE vs. TISE

The diagram below compares the two forms in terms of inputs, operator structure, and outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Domain]
        A1[Wave Function Psi x t]
        A2[Potential V x and Mass m]
    end

    subgraph TDSE_Block[Time Dependent Block]
        B1[Energy Operator i hbar d by dt]
        B2[Hamiltonian H hat equals minus hbar squared by 2m d2 by dx2 plus V x]
        B3[Time Evolution unitary operator U equals exp minus i H t by hbar]
    end

    subgraph TISE_Block[Time Independent Block]
        C1[Boundary Conditions on psi x]
        C2[Eigenvalue Equation H hat psi equals E psi]
        C3[Energy Spectrum E n discrete or continuous]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Outputs]
        D1[Full State Psi x t equals psi x exp minus i E t by hbar]
        D2[Probability Density Psi squared]
        D3[Observable Expectation Values lt A gt]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

### 4.3 Operator Correspondence Map (Classical ↔ Quantum)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Classical Observable Position x] --> Q1[Quantum Operator x hat equals x times]
    P2[Classical Observable Momentum p] --> Q2[Quantum Operator p hat equals minus i hbar d by dx]
    P3[Classical Energy E] --> Q3[Quantum Operator E hat equals i hbar d by dt]
    P4[Classical Hamiltonian H equals p squared by 2m plus V] --> Q4[Quantum Hamiltonian H hat equals minus hbar squared by 2m d2 by dx2 plus V x]
    Q4 --> R1[TISE H hat psi equals E psi]
    Q4 --> R2[TDSE i hbar d Psi by dt equals H hat Psi]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The operator correspondence map is not a one-line trick — it is the **operational foundation** of quantum mechanics. Once you know how to upgrade any classical observable to an operator, you can convert any classical equation of motion into a quantum one. This is called *canonical quantization*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the physical significance of the Time-Dependent Schrödinger equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** The one-dimensional time-dependent Schrödinger equation is given by:

$$i\hbar\frac{\partial \Psi(x,t)}{\partial t} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{\partial^2 \Psi}{\partial x^2} + V(x)\Psi(x,t)$$

**Physical Significance (2 marks):**
1. It is the fundamental dynamical law of non-relativistic quantum mechanics, playing the role analogous to Newton's second law in classical mechanics.
2. The equation governs the temporal evolution of the **wave function** $\Psi(x,t)$, which encodes all physical information about the quantum system.
3. Its linearity guarantees the **superposition principle** — quantum states can be added to form new valid states, the mathematical basis of quantum interference.
4. Its first-order nature in time guarantees **unitarity**, ensuring total probability is conserved ($\int \vert\Psi\vert^2 dx = 1$ at all times).
5. The equation is not derived; it is a **postulate** validated by the extraordinary accuracy of its predictions (atomic spectra, tunneling, band structure).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is a stationary state? Write the wave function of a particle in a stationary state.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A **stationary state** is a quantum state of definite energy $E$ in which the probability density $\vert\Psi\vert^2$ is independent of time, even though the wave function $\Psi$ itself carries a time-dependent phase.

**Wave Function (1.5 marks):** The wave function of a particle in a stationary state is:

$$\Psi(x,t) = \psi(x)\,e^{-iEt/\hbar}$$

where $\psi(x)$ is the **time-independent wave function** satisfying the TISE $\hat{H}\psi = E\psi$, and the factor $e^{-iEt/\hbar}$ is the time-evolution phase.

The probability density is:

$$\vert\Psi(x,t)\vert^2 = \vert\psi(x)\vert^2 \cdot \vert e^{-iEt/\hbar}\vert^2 = \vert\psi(x)\vert^2$$

confirming that it is independent of $t$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the de Broglie step** in the TDSE derivation, jumping directly to operator substitution. This costs 2 marks.
> - In TISE derivations, students often **forget the separation constant** or fail to justify why both sides equal $E$. State explicitly: *"The left side is a function of $t$ only, the right side a function of $x$ only; equality for all $x,t$ requires both to equal a constant $E$."* This earns 2 marks.
> - **Unit consistency is mandatory.** Always verify that $i\hbar\partial/\partial t$ and $\hat{H}$ have the same units (Joules).
> - For 7-mark sub-questions, the model answer should have **at least 6–8 clearly numbered steps** — KTU examiners award partial credit per step.

---

#### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the **Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE)** for a particle of mass $m$ moving in a one-dimensional potential $V(x)$. State clearly the de Broglie relations and the operator correspondences used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A free particle is described by the wave function $\Psi(x,t) = A\,e^{i(kx - \omega t)}$. Show that this wave function satisfies the TDSE for a free particle and find the dispersion relation $\omega(k)$. (7 marks)

---

**Solution to A(a) — Derivation of the TDSE (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start from the classical 1-D wave equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{v^2}\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Assume a plane-wave solution $y(x,t) = Ae^{i(kx - \omega t)}$, and compute the derivatives:
- $\partial^2 y/\partial x^2 = -k^2 y$
- $\partial^2 y/\partial t^2 = -\omega^2 y$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the **de Broglie relations** $E = \hbar\omega$ and $p = \hbar k$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Write the **operator correspondences**:
- $\hat{p}_x = -i\hbar\,\partial/\partial x$ (momentum operator)
- $\hat{E} = i\hbar\,\partial/\partial t$ (energy operator)

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The classical Hamiltonian constraint for a particle in potential $V(x)$:

$$E = \frac{p^2}{2m} + V(x)$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Replace classical quantities with operators acting on a generic wave function $\Psi(x,t)$:

$$i\hbar\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial t} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{\partial^2 \Psi}{\partial x^2} + V(x)\Psi$$

This is the **Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE)**.

**Conclusion (extra):** This equation is first-order in $t$, ensuring deterministic unitary evolution, and is consistent with all known non-relativistic quantum experiments.

---

**Solution to A(b) — Free-Particle Plane Wave (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Substitute $\Psi(x,t) = Ae^{i(kx - \omega t)}$ into the free-particle TDSE (set $V = 0$):

$$i\hbar\frac{\partial \Psi}{\partial t} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{\partial^2 \Psi}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the derivatives:
- $\partial \Psi/\partial t = -i\omega\Psi$
- $\partial^2 \Psi/\partial x^2 = -k^2 \Psi$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute:
- LHS: $i\hbar(-i\omega)\Psi = \hbar\omega\Psi$
- RHS: $-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}(-k^2)\Psi = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m}\Psi$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Equate both sides:

$$\hbar\omega = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve for the angular frequency:

$$\omega = \frac{\hbar k^2}{2m}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** This is the **dispersion relation** for a free quantum particle. It is quadratic in $k$, unlike the linear relation $\omega = ck$ for photons (relativistic).
