# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Transverse Wave | Longitudinal Wave |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Particle vibration direction | Perpendicular to wave propagation | Parallel to wave propagation |
| Regions formed | Crests and troughs | Compressions and rarefactions |
| Example | Waves on a stretched string / Light | Sound waves in air / Seismic P-waves |

A wave in which particle vibration is **perpendicular** to the direction of wave propagation is called a **transverse wave** (e.g., a wave on a string). A wave in which particle vibration is **parallel** to the direction of wave propagation is called a **longitudinal wave** (e.g., sound in air). **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define wavelength, frequency, and wave velocity. Write the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Wavelength ($\lambda$):** The shortest distance between two points on a wave that are in phase, or the distance travelled by the wave in one time period. **Unit: metre (m)**.
- **Frequency ($f$):** The number of complete oscillations produced by the wave per second. **Unit: hertz (Hz)**.
- **Wave velocity ($v$):** The distance travelled by the wave per unit time. **Unit: m/s**.

**Relationship:** The wave travels a distance $\lambda$ in time $T = 1/f$, so:

$$v = f \lambda$$

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Answer **any one** full question from each module. Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal sub-parts.

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the general progressive wave equation $y(x, t) = A \sin(\omega t - kx + \phi)$ starting from first principles. Show that the wave speed is $v = \omega / k$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stretched string has linear mass density $\mu = 0.5$ g/cm and is under tension $T = 100$ N. Calculate the speed of transverse waves on this string. If a wave of frequency $f = 200$ Hz is set up, find the wavelength. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Derivation of the Wave Equation

**Step 1 — Set up the problem:** Consider a transverse wave moving in the positive $x$-direction. Let the displacement of a particle at position $x$ at time $t$ be $y(x, t)$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Motion at the origin:** At $x = 0$, the particle executes simple harmonic motion:

$$y(0, t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

where $A$ is amplitude, $\omega$ angular frequency, and $\phi$ initial phase. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Time delay for propagation:** The wave reaches position $x$ after a time delay of $\frac{x}{v}$. So the particle at $x$ is doing what the particle at $0$ was doing at the **earlier** time $t - \frac{x}{v}$:

$$y(x, t) = A \sin\left[\omega\left(t - \frac{x}{v}\right) + \phi\right] = A \sin(\omega t - \frac{\omega x}{v} + \phi)$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Identify the wave number:** Using $k = \frac{\omega}{v} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$:

$$y(x, t) = A \sin(\omega t - kx + \phi)$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Confirm wave speed:** For a constant phase point, $\omega t - kx + \phi = \text{const}$:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{\omega}{k} = v \quad \checkmark$$ **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.5$ g/cm $= 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg / $10^{-2}$ m $= 0.05$ kg/m
- $T = 100$ N
- $f = 200$ Hz

**Step 1 — Wave speed on the string:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{0.05}} = \sqrt{2000} \text{ m/s} \approx 44.72 \text{ m/s}$$ **(4 marks)**

**Step 2 — Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{44.72}{200} = 0.2236 \text{ m} \approx 22.36 \text{ cm}$$ **(3 marks)**

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** What is a longitudinal wave? Show that the speed of sound in a gas is $v = \sqrt{\gamma P / \rho}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sound wave of frequency $f = 512$ Hz travels through air at $v = 340$ m/s. Find its wavelength and the time period. What is the phase difference between two points on the wave separated by 0.5 m? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a) — Longitudinal Wave & Speed of Sound

**Definition:** A longitudinal wave is a wave in which the particles of the medium vibrate **parallel** to the direction of wave propagation, producing alternating **compressions** and **rarefactions**. Examples: sound waves, seismic P-waves. **(2 marks)**

**Derivation of $v = \sqrt{\gamma P / \rho}$:**

**Step 1 — Newton's second law for a fluid slab:** Consider a thin slab of gas of area $A$ and thickness $\Delta x$. A pressure difference $-\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \Delta x$ across it causes longitudinal acceleration:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} A \Delta x = \rho A \Delta x \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2}$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Adiabatic compressibility:** Sound propagates adiabatically. The bulk modulus is $B = -V \frac{dP}{dV} = \gamma P$. For a small displacement $\xi$:

$$\Delta P = -B \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} = -\gamma P \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Combine and identify $v$:** Substituting and simplifying:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\rho}{\gamma P} \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2} = \frac{1}{v^2} \frac{\partial^2 \xi}{\partial t^2}$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$$ **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:** $f = 512$ Hz, $v = 340$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{340}{512} = 0.664 \text{ m}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Time period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{512} = 1.953 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 1.95 \text{ ms}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Phase difference for $\Delta x = 0.5$ m:**

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta x = \frac{2\pi}{0.664} \times 0.5 = \pi \times \frac{0.5}{0.332} = 4.73 \text{ rad}$$ **(3 marks)**

Equivalently, in degrees: $\Delta \phi \approx 4.73 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} \approx 271°$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap:** Linear mass density $\mu$ is often given in g/cm. KTU students frequently forget to convert to SI units (kg/m) before applying $v = \sqrt{T/\mu}$. **Loss of 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Phase difference range:** A phase difference must always be reported in **radians** (or sometimes in multiples of $\pi$). Reporting it as a decimal number without the unit loses marks. The result should be in the range $[0, 2\pi]$; if you compute $> 2\pi$, take the modulo.
> 3. **Sign of phase:** $\omega t - kx$ (wave moving in $+x$ direction) and $\omega t + kx$ (wave moving in $-x$ direction). A sign error in the wave equation loses 1 mark in 14-mark derivations.
> 4. **Forgetting $\gamma$:** When asked for the speed of sound in **gas**, students often write $v = \sqrt{P/\rho}$ (Newton's formula, isothermal). The **correct** one with the adiabatic bulk modulus is $v = \sqrt{\gamma P / \rho}$. Always mention "adiabatic" and "$\gamma$".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

- [x] A wave transports **energy and momentum**, **not matter**.
- [x] In a **transverse** wave, particle vibration is **⊥** to wave direction. Examples: light, waves on a string, ripples on water.
- [x] In a **longitudinal** wave, particle vibration is **∥** to wave direction. Examples: sound, seismic P-waves, slinky push-pull.
- [x] **Compressions** and **rarefactions** are the defining features of longitudinal waves.
- [x] **Crests** and **troughs** are the defining features of transverse waves.
- [x] **Wavelength $\lambda$** = distance between two consecutive identical phases (crest-to-crest, compression-to-compression).
- [x] **Frequency $f$** = number of oscillations per second, $f = 1/T$. **Unit: Hz**.
- [x] **Master equation:** $v = f \lambda$ (works for ALL wave types).
- [x] **General wave equation:** $y(x, t) = A \sin(\omega t - kx + \phi)$, where $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $\omega = 2\pi f$.
- [x] **Speed of transverse wave on string:** $v = \sqrt{T/\mu}$ — $T$ = tension (N), $\mu$ = linear mass density (kg/m).
- [x] **Speed of sound in gas:** $v = \sqrt{\gamma P / \rho}$ — adiabatic process.
- [x] **Speed of sound in air at 0 °C:** ~331 m/s; at 20 °C: ~343 m/s.
- [x] **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- [x] **Phase difference from path difference:** $\Delta \phi = (2\pi/\lambda) \cdot \Delta x$.
- [x] **Only transverse waves can be polarized.** Longitudinal waves cannot.
- [x] **All electromagnetic waves are transverse** and can travel through vacuum.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes KTU Students Make in This Topic:**
> 1. Confusing **time period $T$** with **string tension $T$** — always write units!
> 2. Using $v = \sqrt{P/\rho}$ (isothermal, wrong) instead of $v = \sqrt{\gamma P/\rho}$ (adiabatic, correct) for sound in gas.
> 3. Forgetting to convert $\mu$ from g/cm to kg/m in numerical problems.
> 4. Saying *"longitudinal waves cannot travel through solids"* — **wrong!** Sound (longitudinal) and seismic P-waves travel through solids just fine.
> 5. Saying *"transverse waves need no medium"* — **wrong!** Mechanical transverse waves (e.g., string) need a medium. Only **electromagnetic** transverse waves don't.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Frequency** is a fundamental physical quantity that quantifies the rate at which a periodic phenomenon repeats itself per unit time. In the framework of the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, frequency is rigorously defined as:

> **Frequency (f or ν):** The number of complete cycles, oscillations, or repetitions of a periodic event that occur in one unit of time, measured in **Hertz (Hz)** where **1 Hz = 1 s⁻¹** = one cycle per second.

Mathematically, the instantaneous frequency is the reciprocal of the **time period (T)** of the periodic event:

$$f = \frac{1}{T}$$

where **T** is the minimum time interval after which the motion or signal repeats itself identically (i.e., it satisfies the periodicity condition $y(t+T) = y(t)$ for all $t$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In wave mechanics, frequency is an *intrinsic property* of the source. It does **not** change when a wave moves from one medium to another — only the wavelength $\lambda$ and the speed $v$ change such that the fundamental relation $v = f\lambda$ remains valid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are sitting in a park watching a **clock pendulum** swing back and forth. Each complete "swing there and back" counts as **one cycle**. If the pendulum completes exactly 2 full swings in one second, then its frequency is **2 Hz**. The faster the pendulum swings, the higher its frequency; the slower it swings, the lower the frequency.

**Another real-world analogy — a beating heart:** A healthy human heart beats about 72 times per minute at rest. The cardiac frequency is therefore:

$$f_{heart} = \frac{72 \text{ beats}}{60 \text{ s}} = 1.2 \text{ Hz}$$

This shows frequency is not limited to physics — it appears in **biology, acoustics, communication engineering, signal processing, optics, and quantum mechanics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Note:** The KTU 2024 Module 4 of GZPHT121 introduces frequency as the cornerstone of oscillatory and wave phenomena, laying the foundation for resonance, wave propagation, and the wave-particle duality later in the course.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following constants and units are essential when working with frequency in KTU problems:

- **Unit of frequency (SI):** Hertz (Hz) = **s⁻¹**
- **Larger units:** kHz (10³ Hz), MHz (10⁶ Hz), GHz (10⁹ Hz), THz (10¹² Hz)
- **Angular (angular) frequency $\omega$:** measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**
- **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.8$ **m/s²** (used in pendulum problems)
- **Standard speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s** (for photon frequency $E = hf$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** Never confuse *ordinary frequency $f$* (in Hz) with *angular frequency $\omega$* (in rad/s). They differ by a factor of $2\pi$: $\omega = 2\pi f$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Frequency

The concept of frequency emerges from the need to **quantify repetition** in nature. Here is the structured logic behind it:

- **Step 1 — Periodicity Detection:** When we observe a system (a pendulum, a wave, an alternating current) and notice that it returns to its initial state after a fixed interval, we call that interval the **time period $T$**.

- **Step 2 — Rate of Repetition:** A small $T$ means the event repeats quickly (high frequency), while a large $T$ means slow repetition (low frequency). This inverse relationship is captured by $f = 1/T$.

- **Step 3 — Angular Representation:** Because cycles are continuous, we often want to track the *phase* of motion. The **angular frequency $\omega$** measures how fast the phase changes in radians, providing a cleaner representation in differential equations such as $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \omega^2 x = 0$.

- **Step 4 — Spectral Characterisation:** In modern physics, frequency is the *preferred variable* for spectral analysis (Fourier transforms, spectrograms) because it remains invariant under propagation — wavelengths stretch, but the frequency of a wave is preserved unless the source itself changes.

- **Step 5 — Connection to Energy:** Through Planck's relation $E = hf$, frequency directly links the wave description to **quantum energy**, making it arguably the most important variable bridging classical and modern physics.

## 2.2 Types of Frequency (KTU High-Yield Classification)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises several categories of frequency, each with its own physical meaning:

- **Natural (or Resonant) Frequency ($f_0$):** The frequency at which a system oscillates freely without external forcing or damping. Example: a tuning fork vibrating at 440 Hz.
- **Driving (or Forced) Frequency ($f_d$):** The frequency of the external periodic force applied to a system.
- **Fundamental Frequency ($f_1$):** The lowest natural frequency of a vibrating system. All higher modes are integer multiples: $f_n = n f_1$.
- **Harmonic Frequency ($f_n$):** Integer multiples of the fundamental — $f_2, f_3, f_4, \ldots$ — that together form the harmonic series.
- **Beat Frequency ($f_{beat}$):** $|f_1 - f_2|$, arising from the superposition of two waves of slightly different frequencies.
- **Cut-off Frequency ($f_c$):** The threshold frequency below (or above) which a filter or a medium blocks a wave — critical in electronics and optics.
- **Angular Frequency ($\omega$):** $\omega = 2\pi f$, appearing everywhere rotational and oscillatory mathematics is used.
- **Threshold Frequency ($f_0$):** The minimum frequency of light required to liberate electrons in the photoelectric effect.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every essential formula for the "Concept of Frequency" required for KTU 2024 board examinations. Mastery of these is non-negotiable.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **SI Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ordinary frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | $T$ = time period | Hz (s⁻¹) |
| Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | $T$ = time period | rad/s |
| Wave frequency | $v = f\lambda$ | $v$ = wave speed, $\lambda$ = wavelength | m/s |
| Wave number | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\omega}{v}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength | rad/m |
| SHM (spring-mass) | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ = spring constant, $m$ = mass | Hz |
| Simple pendulum | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{L}}$ | $g$ = gravity, $L$ = length | Hz |
| Torsional pendulum | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\kappa}{I}}$ | $\kappa$ = torsion const., $I$ = moment of inertia | Hz |
| LC circuit | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | $L$ = inductance, $C$ = capacitance | Hz |
| Beat frequency | $f_{beat} = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$ | $f_1, f_2$ = superposed frequencies | Hz |
| Doppler (source moving) | $f' = f \left(\dfrac{v}{v \mp v_s}\right)$ | $v_s$ = source speed | Hz |
| Planck's relation | $E = hf$ | $E$ = photon energy, $h$ = Planck's const. | J |
| Photoelectric threshold | $f_0 = \dfrac{\Phi}{h}$ | $\Phi$ = work function | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for SHM:** All simple harmonic motion systems have the form $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{restoring coefficient}}{\text{inertia coefficient}}}$. The numerator contains whatever produces the restoring torque/force; the denominator contains whatever resists acceleration.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The concept of frequency is the lifeblood of numerous engineering domains:

- **Electrical Engineering:** AC mains operate at **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz** (USA). Power grids must maintain this frequency within tight tolerances (±0.5 Hz).
- **Telecommunications:** FM radio broadcasts in the **88–108 MHz** band; mobile networks use frequencies up to several **GHz** (4G/5G).
- **Medical Imaging:** MRI machines use radio frequencies around **42 MHz/Tesla**; ultrasound imaging uses **2–18 MHz**.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Resonance analysis of bridges, buildings, and turbine blades requires precise matching of natural and driving frequencies to avoid catastrophic failure (e.g., the Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse of 1940).
- **Materials Science:** Each chemical bond vibrates at characteristic frequencies; IR spectroscopy identifies materials by their absorption frequencies.
- **Optics and Photonics:** Laser frequencies (e.g., 632.8 nm red He-Ne laser corresponds to $\sim 4.74 \times 10^{14}$ Hz) are used in metrology, barcode scanners, and interferometers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No logical jumps. This matches the rigor expected in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) full-mark answers.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Frequency of a Spring-Mass System (SHM)

Consider a mass $m$ attached to a light spring of spring constant $k$ on a frictionless horizontal surface, displaced by $x$ from equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Identify the restoring force.**
By Hooke's law, the spring exerts a force opposite to displacement:
$$F = -kx$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law.**
$$F = ma = m \frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions.**
$$m \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -kx$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the standard SHM differential equation.**
$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \frac{k}{m} x = 0$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the canonical SHM equation.**
The general form of undamped SHM is:
$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \omega^2 x = 0$$

**Step 6 — Match coefficients to identify angular frequency.**
$$\omega^2 = \frac{k}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 7 — Convert angular frequency to ordinary frequency.**
$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** The frequency of a spring-mass system increases with stiffness $k$ and decreases with mass $m$. This is why a stiffer, lighter car suspension feels "sportier" (higher $f$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency of a Simple Pendulum (Small-Angle Approximation)

Consider a simple pendulum of length $L$ and bob mass $m$ displaced by a small angle $\theta$ from the vertical.

**Step 1 — Identify the restoring torque about the pivot.**
The gravitational force component tangential to the arc is $-mg \sin\theta$, acting at distance $L$. The restoring torque is therefore:
$$\tau = -mgL \sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rotational form of Newton's second law.**
$$\tau = I \alpha = I \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$$

where $I = mL^2$ is the moment of inertia of the point mass about the pivot.

**Step 3 — Equate torque expressions.**
$$mL^2 \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -mgL \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**
$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\frac{g}{L} \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 — Apply the small-angle approximation.**
For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians). Thus:
$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \frac{g}{L} \theta = 0$$

**Step 6 — Match with canonical SHM form.**
$$\omega^2 = \frac{g}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

**Step 7 — Extract ordinary frequency.**
$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

**Step 8 — Period of pendulum (for completeness).**
$$T = \frac{1}{f} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{L}{g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The pendulum frequency is *independent of mass* and *independent of amplitude* (in the small-angle regime). This is what made pendulums so valuable for early clocks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Frequency from the General Wave Equation

Consider a one-dimensional sinusoidal wave travelling in the $+x$ direction:
$$y(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 1 — Compute the spatial period (wavelength) $\lambda$.**
We require $y(x+\lambda, t) = y(x, t)$ for all $t$. Therefore:
$$A \sin(k(x+\lambda) - \omega t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t)$$
$$k\lambda = 2\pi$$
$$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{k}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temporal period $T$.**
We require $y(x, t+T) = y(x, t)$ for all $x$. Therefore:
$$A \sin(kx - \omega(t+T)) = A \sin(kx - \omega t)$$
$$\omega T = 2\pi$$
$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

**Step 3 — Invert to find frequency.**
$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the universal wave relation.**
Wave speed equals wavelength divided by period, which also equals frequency times wavelength:
$$v = \frac{\lambda}{T} = \lambda f$$

Thus, the **master relation** governing all linear waves is confirmed:
$$v = f \lambda$$

**Step 5 — Rewriting in terms of $\omega$ and $k$.**
$$\omega = vk \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024 exams:** This derivation links $\omega$, $f$, $T$, $\lambda$, $k$, and $v$ into a single coherent framework. Most wave problems in the KTU board paper test your fluency in switching between these equivalent representations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Frequency Variation with System Parameters

This matrix summarises how the natural frequency of common KTU-level oscillators responds to changes in physical parameters:

| **System** | **Doubles $f$ when...** | **Halves $f$ when...** | **Independent of...** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spring-mass | $k$ quadruples; $m$ quarters | $k$ quarters; $m$ quadruples | Amplitude (in ideal SHM) |
| Simple pendulum | $g$ quadruples; $L$ quarters | $g$ quarters; $L$ quadruples | Mass $m$; amplitude (small angle) |
| Torsional pendulum | $\kappa$ quadruples; $I$ quarters | $\kappa$ quarters; $I$ quadruples | Amplitude |
| LC circuit | $L$ or $C$ quarters | $L$ or $C$ quadruples | Charge amplitude |
| Standing wave on string | Tension $T$ quadruples; linear density $\mu$ quarters | Tension quarters; density quadruples | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: The Frequency Ecosystem

The following Mermaid diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy of frequency — its definition, its mathematical siblings, and the systems that use it.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Periodic Motion] --> B[Time Period T]
    A --> C[Frequency f]
    A --> D[Angular Frequency omega]
    A --> E[Wave Number k]
    B --> F["f = 1 slash T"]
    D --> G["omega = 2 pi f"]
    E --> H["k = 2 pi slash lambda"]
    C --> I[Master Wave Relation]
    I --> J["v = f lambda"]
    C --> K[SHM Systems]
    K --> L["f = 1 slash 2pi sqrt k slash m"]
    K --> M["f = 1 slash 2pi sqrt g slash L"]
    K --> N["f = 1 slash 2pi sqrt kappa slash I"]
    K --> O["f = 1 slash 2 pi sqrt 1 slash LC"]
    C --> P[Wave Phenomena]
    P --> Q[Beats: f_beat = absolute f1 minus f2]
    P --> R[Doppler Shift]
    P --> S[Resonance]
    C --> T[Quantum Link]
    T --> U["E = h f"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: How Frequency Arises in a System

This block diagram traces the origin of frequency from physical setup to measurable output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Restoring Force or Torque] --> A1[Equation of Motion]
    S2[Inertia of System] --> A1
    A1 --> A2[Second Order ODE]
    A2 --> A3[SHM Form: d2x slash dt2 plus omega squared x = 0]
    A3 --> A4[Solution: x of t = A cos omega t plus phi]
    A4 --> A5[Extract omega = 2 pi f]
    A5 --> A6[Final Frequency f]
    A6 --> O1[Measured in Hz]
    A6 --> O2[Used in Wave Equation v = f lambda]
    A6 --> O3[Linked to Energy via E = h f]
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree: Types of Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Frequency f] --> T1[Natural or Resonant]
    ROOT --> T2[Driving or Forced]
    ROOT --> T3[Fundamental]
    ROOT --> T4[Harmonic]
    ROOT --> T5[Beat]
    ROOT --> T6[Cut-off or Threshold]
    T1 --> T1A[Free oscillation frequency of system]
    T2 --> T2A[Frequency of external periodic force]
    T3 --> T3A[Lowest mode: f1]
    T4 --> T4A[Integer multiples: f2, f3, etc.]
    T5 --> T5A[Absolute difference of two close frequencies]
    T6 --> T6A[Boundary frequency of filter or photoemission]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Each node uses raw uppercase alphanumeric text inside its label to ensure clean Mermaid rendering without parsing errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term *frequency* of a periodic motion. State its SI unit and write its relation with the time period.**

**Model Answer:**

Frequency of a periodic motion is the number of complete oscillations (or cycles) executed by the oscillating body in one unit of time. It is the reciprocal of the time period $T$.

**Mathematical relation:**
$$f = \frac{1}{T}$$

**SI unit:** Hertz (Hz), where $1 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ s}^{-1}$, i.e., one cycle per second.

**Larger units** include kilohertz (kHz = 10³ Hz), megahertz (MHz = 10⁶ Hz), and gigahertz (GHz = 10⁹ Hz). [3 marks]

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between *angular frequency* and *ordinary frequency*. Write the relation connecting them.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Ordinary Frequency ($f$)** | **Angular Frequency ($\omega$)** |
|---|---|
| Number of cycles per unit time | Rate of change of phase angle (radians per second) |
| SI unit: Hz (s⁻¹) | SI unit: rad/s |
| Used in wave speed relation $v = f\lambda$ | Used in SHM equation $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \omega^2 x = 0$ |

**Relation:**
$$\omega = 2\pi f \quad \text{or} \quad f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$$

Since one complete cycle corresponds to a phase change of $2\pi$ radians, angular frequency is $2\pi$ times the ordinary frequency. [3 marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive an expression for the frequency of a simple pendulum. Hence, calculate the length of the pendulum required to produce a frequency of 0.5 Hz at a location where $g = 9.8$ m/s².

**(b) [7 marks]** Define *beat frequency*. Two tuning forks when sounded together produce 4 beats per second. One tuning fork has a frequency of 256 Hz. If a small piece of wax is attached to the other fork, the beat frequency becomes 2 Hz. Find the frequency of the other tuning fork (before waxing).

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Derivation (full 5 marks):**

Consider a simple pendulum of length $L$ and bob mass $m$ displaced through a small angle $\theta$ from the mean position.

**Step 1:** The restoring torque about the pivot is:
$$\tau = -mgL \sin\theta$$

**Step 2:** Using $\tau = I\alpha$ with $I = mL^2$:
$$mL^2 \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -mgL \sin\theta$$

**Step 3:** Simplifying:
$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = -\frac{g}{L} \sin\theta$$

**Step 4:** For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$:
$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \frac{g}{L} \theta = 0$$

**Step 5:** Comparing with the standard SHM form $\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \omega^2 \theta = 0$:
$$\omega^2 = \frac{g}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

**Step 6:** Ordinary frequency:
$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{L}}$$

**Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

Given $f = 0.5$ Hz and $g = 9.8$ m/s²:
$$0.5 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9.8}{L}}$$
$$0.5 \times 2\pi = \sqrt{\frac{9.8}{L}}$$
$$\pi = \sqrt{\frac{9.8}{L}}$$
$$\pi^2 = \frac{9.8}{L}$$
$$L = \frac{9.8}{\pi^2} = \frac{9.8}{9.8696}$$
$$L \approx 0.993 \text{ m} \approx 1 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** $L \approx 0.993$ m [Total: 7 marks]

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition (2 marks):**

**Beat frequency** is the frequency of the periodic variation in loudness (intensity) heard when two sound waves of nearly equal frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ are superposed. It equals the absolute difference of the two source frequencies:
$$f_{beat} = |f_1 - f_2|$$

**Problem Solution (5 marks):**

**Given:**
- Frequency of first tuning fork: $f_1 = 256$ Hz
- Initial beat frequency: $f_{beat,1} = 4$ Hz
- After waxing the second fork, beat frequency: $f_{beat,2} = 2$ Hz

**Step 1:** Let the frequency of the second tuning fork (before waxing) be $f_2$.

From the initial condition:
$$|f_2 - 256| = 4$$
$$f_2 = 260 \text{ Hz} \quad \text{or} \quad f_2 = 252 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2:** When wax is attached, the effective mass of the second fork *increases*, which *decreases* its frequency (analogous to a loaded tuning fork or loaded spring). So $f_2$ becomes smaller.

**Step 3:** After waxing, the new frequency is $f_2' < f_2$, and the new beat frequency is 2 Hz:
$$|f_2' - 256| = 2$$
$$f_2' = 258 \text{ Hz} \quad \text{or} \quad f_2' = 254 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4:** Combining the constraints:
- If $f_2 = 260$ Hz, then $f_2' < 260$. Possible values: 258 or 254. Both are *less than 260*, so both consistent.
- If $f_2 = 252$ Hz, then $f_2' < 252$. But the new beat frequency gives $f_2' = 254$ (which is *greater* than 252) — contradicts the wax-loading rule.

**Step 5:** Therefore $f_2 = 260$ Hz is the correct original frequency.

**Answer:** Frequency of the other tuning fork (before waxing) = **260 Hz** [Total: 7 marks]

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of a mass $m$ attached to a spring of spring constant $k$. A spring of spring constant 200 N/m is attached to a mass of 0.5 kg. Calculate (i) the angular frequency, (ii) the time period, and (iii) the frequency of oscillation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Define *resonant frequency*. A string of length 1 m, mass 4 g, and tension 100 N is plucked. Calculate its fundamental frequency. (Hint: $v = \sqrt{T/\mu}$, $f_1 = v/(2L)$.)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a mass $m$ attached to a spring of constant $k$ displaced by $x$.

**Step 1:** Restoring force (Hooke's law): $F = -kx$

**Step 2:** Newton's second law: $F = m \frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$

**Step 3:** Equating:
$$m \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -kx$$
$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + \frac{k}{m} x = 0$$

**Step 4:** Comparing with SHM form:
$$\omega^2 = \frac{k}{m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 5:** Ordinary frequency:
$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: $k = 200$ N/m, $m = 0.5$ kg.

(i) **Angular frequency:**
$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{0.5}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

(ii) **Time period:**
$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi}{20} = \frac{\pi}{10} \approx 0.314 \text{ s}$$

(iii) **Frequency:**
$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{10}{\pi} \approx 3.183 \text{ Hz}$$

**Answer:** $\omega = 20$ rad/s, $T \approx 0.314$ s, $f \approx 3.18$ Hz [Total: 7 marks]

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition (2 marks):**

**Resonant frequency** is the natural frequency of a system at which the amplitude of oscillation becomes maximum for a given driving force, due to constructive energy transfer from the driving source to the oscillating system. At resonance, the driving frequency matches the system's natural frequency.

**Numerical Calculation (5 marks):**

**Given:**
- Length $L = 1$ m
- Mass of string $M = 4$ g $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ kg
- Tension $T = 100$ N

**Step 1:** Linear mass density:
$$\mu = \frac{M}{L} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg}}{1 \text{ m}} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}$$

**Step 2:** Wave speed on string:
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{4 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{25000} = 50\sqrt{10} \text{ m/s} \approx 158.11 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Fundamental frequency (first harmonic, fixed at both ends, $\lambda_1 = 2L$):
$$f_1 = \frac{v}{2L} = \frac{50\sqrt{10}}{2 \times 1} = 25\sqrt{10} \text{ Hz} \approx 79.06 \text{ Hz}$$

**Answer:** Fundamental frequency $\approx 79.06$ Hz [Total: 7 marks]

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Frequency-Based Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing angular frequency in Hz or ordinary frequency in rad/s. The examiner will deduct marks if you fail to specify units.
>
> 2. **Missing the small-angle approximation (–1 mark):** In pendulum derivations, writing $\sin\theta = \theta$ *without stating* "for small angles" loses the validity condition. Always mention the approximation explicitly.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $2\pi$ factor (–2 marks):** The most common error is writing $f = \sqrt{k/m}$ (missing the $2\pi$ in the denominator). Remember: $f = \omega/(2\pi)$, so the $2\pi$ is essential.
>
> 4. **Beat-frequency direction error (–2 marks):** When a fork is loaded with wax, its mass increases and frequency *decreases*. Many students assume it increases. Always think of the fork as a "spring-mass system" — more mass = lower frequency.
>
> 5. **Plugging numbers into the wrong formula (–1 to –3 marks):** Confusing $f_1 = v/(2L)$ (string fixed at both ends) with $f_1 = v/(4L)$ (open organ pipe). Memorise the boundary conditions carefully.
>
> 6. **In Doppler problems:** Sign convention errors. Use $f' = f \cdot v/(v - v_s)$ when source moves *towards* observer, and $f' = f \cdot v/(v + v_s)$ when source moves *away*.
>
> 7. **No units in final answer (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always quote the final numerical answer with units. A bare "0.993" without "m" is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- **Definition:** Frequency $f$ is the number of complete cycles per unit time; SI unit is **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Reciprocal relation:** $f = 1/T$ — the cornerstone relation.
- **Angular form:** $\omega = 2\pi f$ — the bridge between SHM and circular motion.
- **Wave relation:** $v = f\lambda$ — universally valid for all linear waves.
- **Wave number:** $k = 2\pi/\lambda = \omega/v$ — useful in differential form of wave equation.
- **Spring-mass SHM:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ — frequency rises with stiffness, falls with mass.
- **Simple pendulum:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{g/L}$ — independent of mass and (for small angles) amplitude.
- **Torsional pendulum:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\kappa/I}$ — rotational analogue of spring-mass.
- **LC circuit:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ — electrical analogue of spring-mass.
- **Beats:** $f_{beat} = |f_1 - f_2|$ — used to tune musical instruments.
- **Planck's link:** $E = hf$ — frequency determines quantum energy.
- **Threshold (photoelectric):** $f_0 = \Phi/h$ — minimum frequency for electron emission.
- **String harmonics:** $f_n = n f_1 = n v/(2L)$ — integer multiples of fundamental.
- **Doppler effect:** $f' = f \cdot \dfrac{v}{v \mp v_s}$ — sign depends on source direction.
- **Master mental model:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{restoring factor}}{\text{inertia factor}}}$ — applies universally to ideal SHM systems.
- **Invariance principle:** Frequency is set by the source; it does **not** change between media.
- **Resonance danger:** When driving frequency equals natural frequency, amplitudes grow catastrophically — the cause of mechanical failures.
- **Exam tip:** Always state units, always justify small-angle or small-amplitude approximations, and never skip the $2\pi$ factor when computing $f$ from $\omega$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wavelength and Time Period (No Derivation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wavelength and Time Period (No Derivation)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wavelength (λ - Lambda)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Wavelength (λ)** is the spatial period of a periodic wave — the distance over which the wave's shape repeats itself. It is the distance between two consecutive corresponding points on a wave that are in the same phase (e.g., crest-to-crest, trough-to-trough, or any identical points separated by one full cycle).

**SI Unit:** **metre (m)**
**Common Sub-units:** **nanometre (nm)** for light, **millimetre (mm)** for microwaves, **metre (m)** for radio waves.

---

### 1.2 Time Period (T)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Time Period (T)** is the temporal period of a periodic wave — the time required to complete one full oscillation or cycle. It is the duration between two consecutive corresponding instants when the wave passes through the same phase at a given point in space.

**SI Unit:** **second (s)**
**Symbol Convention:** Always written in **uppercase T** (lowercase $t$ is reserved for instantaneous time).

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a beach watching ocean waves roll in:

- **Wavelength (λ) — The "Step Length" of a Wave:**
  If you measure the **physical distance** between two consecutive wave crests hitting the shore, that distance is the wavelength. Think of it as the **"spatial footprint"** of one complete wave. For a long ocean swell rolling in from far away, this distance might be **50 metres or more**. For the ripples in a small cup of water, it could be a few **millimetres**.

- **Time Period (T) — The "Patience" of a Wave:**
  Now, instead of measuring space, start a **stopwatch** the moment one crest passes your feet, and stop it the very moment the **next crest** touches your feet. That elapsed time is the time period. It tells you **how long** the wave takes to repeat itself at one fixed location.

- **The Connection — Wave Speed (v):**
  If a wave moves a distance of one wavelength in a time of one period, then the speed of the wave is simply:
  - **Speed = How far per how long = Wavelength / Time Period**

  This intuitive relationship, $v = \lambda / T$, is the cornerstone of all wave physics.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "**Lambda travels in T seconds**" → $v = \lambda \div T$

---

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Referenced

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s** |
| Speed of sound in air (at 20 °C) | $v_{sound}$ | **$\approx 343$ m/s** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_0$ | **$1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Wave Motion

Every periodic wave (whether a sound wave in air, a light wave in space, or a wave on a string) is described by **three fundamental quantities**:

1. **Amplitude (A):** The maximum displacement from equilibrium (the "height" of a crest above the mean level).
2. **Wavelength (λ):** The "length" of one complete cycle in space.
3. **Time Period (T):** The "duration" of one complete cycle in time.

These three combine to give the **wave speed (v)**, which is the rate at which the wave pattern propagates through the medium.

### 2.2 Logical Derivation of the Wave Speed Relation

**Step 1 — Defining what speed means:**
Speed is the distance travelled per unit time. For a wave, the most natural distance to consider is the length of one full cycle, which is the wavelength $\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Identifying the corresponding time:**
The time it takes to travel that single cycle's length is the time period $T$.

**Step 3 — Forming the ratio:**

$$v = \frac{\text{distance of one cycle}}{\text{time of one cycle}} = \frac{\lambda}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Introducing frequency as the inverse of period:**
Frequency $f$ is the number of complete cycles that occur per second. By definition:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{1}{f}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting back into the speed equation:**

$$v = \lambda \cdot f$$

This is the **universal wave equation**, valid for **all** types of waves in linear media.

---

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Relationship

- **Why is $f = 1/T$?** Because if a wave completes $f$ cycles in one second, then one cycle must take $1/f$ seconds. It is a simple unit-consistency argument.
- **Why is $v = \lambda f$?** Because in the time $T$ that the wave takes to complete one cycle at a point, the wavefront itself has moved forward by exactly one wavelength $\lambda$. So in $T$ seconds, the wave travels $\lambda$ metres — hence $v = \lambda / T = \lambda f$.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master This Table — These Equations Appear in 90%+ of KTU Wave Problems.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | SI Unit of Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Period from frequency | **s** |
| 2 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Frequency from period | **Hz** |
| 3 | $v = \lambda \cdot f$ | Wave speed (universal) | **m/s** |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{\lambda}{T}$ | Wave speed via wavelength & period | **m/s** |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Wavelength from speed & frequency | **m** |
| 6 | $\lambda = v \cdot T$ | Wavelength from speed & period | **m** |
| 7 | $\omega = 2\pi f = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Angular frequency | **rad/s** |
| 8 | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | Angular wave number | **rad/m** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Warning in Markdown Tables:** When writing $\vert v \vert$ or absolute values inside KTU answer sheets or digital notes, use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent the vertical bar from breaking markdown table rendering.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of $\lambda$, $T$, $v$ |
|---|---|
| **Telecommunications** | Allocating frequency bands; designing antenna sizes as $\lambda/2$ or $\lambda/4$. |
| **Medical Ultrasound** | Choosing frequency so wavelength matches tissue features to be imaged. |
| **Optical Fibre Networks** | Selecting wavelength windows (1310 nm, 1550 nm) to minimise loss. |
| **Seismology** | Determining earthquake wave speed via travel time and epicentral distance. |
| **Musical Acoustics** | Relating pitch (frequency) to instrument size (wavelength). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Directive:** "**No Derivation**" for this specific topic means the **derivation of the wave equation itself** (i.e., $\partial^2 y / \partial t^2 = v^2 \, \partial^2 y / \partial x^2$) is **excluded**. However, **deriving the relationships between $v$, $\lambda$, $f$, and $T$ is mandatory** and is a frequent exam question. Below are the complete derivations a KTU student **must** be able to reproduce.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Wave Speed Equation $v = \lambda f$

**Statement:** Show that the speed of a wave is the product of its wavelength and frequency.

**Setup:** Consider a periodic transverse wave travelling in the $+x$ direction through a uniform medium. The wave pattern repeats itself in space every distance $\lambda$ and in time every interval $T$.

**Step-by-step proof:**

**Step 1 — Definition of wave speed:**
Wave speed is defined as the rate at which a fixed point on the wave profile (for example, a particular crest) moves through the medium:

$$v = \frac{\text{distance travelled by the wave pattern}}{\text{time taken}}$$

**Step 2 — Identify a natural "unit distance" for the wave:**
Since the wave repeats every wavelength, the most natural and complete unit of travel is one full wavelength, $\lambda$.

**Step 3 — Identify the corresponding "unit time":**
The time the wave takes to advance by exactly one wavelength is precisely one full oscillation period $T$, because the pattern is identical after every $T$ seconds.

**Step 4 — Form the ratio:**

$$v = \frac{\lambda}{T}$$

**Step 5 — Relate period to frequency:**
By the standard definition of frequency as cycles per second:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T = \frac{1}{f}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the speed expression:**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{\lambda}{T} \\
  &= \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{T} \\
  &= \lambda \cdot f
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{v = \lambda f}$$

**Physical interpretation:** In one second, the wave emits $f$ complete cycles, each of length $\lambda$. So in one second, the wavefront has moved forward a total distance of $f \times \lambda$ metres — which is exactly the wave speed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation between Wavelength, Period, and Angular Quantities

**Step 1 — Angular frequency definition:**
One full cycle corresponds to a phase change of $2\pi$ radians. Therefore, the angular frequency is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = 2\pi f \quad \text{(units: rad/s)}$$

**Step 2 — Angular wave number definition:**
Similarly, one full wavelength corresponds to a phase change of $2\pi$ radians in space. Therefore:

$$k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \quad \text{(units: rad/m)}$$

**Step 3 — Combine to obtain an alternative wave-speed formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega &= 2\pi f \\
k &= \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \\
\frac{\omega}{k} &= \frac{2\pi f}{2\pi / \lambda} = f \lambda = v
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{v = \frac{\omega}{k}}$$

This form is exceptionally useful in **quantum mechanics** and **modulated signal analysis**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A radio station broadcasts at a frequency of **98.3 MHz**. Given that electromagnetic waves travel at the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, find:
- (i) the time period of the wave,
- (ii) the wavelength of the broadcast signal.

**Solution:**

**(i) Time Period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{98.3 \times 10^6 \, \text{Hz}}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 1.017 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{s} \approx 10.17 \, \text{ns}}$$

**(ii) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}}{98.3 \times 10^6 \, \text{Hz}}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 3.052 \, \text{m}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Insight:** The FM band lies in the VHF range, and the wavelength around 3 m is why FM car antennas are typically a **quarter-wavelength (≈ 75 cm)** whip — a directly KTU-relevant application of the wave relation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: The Wave-Quantity Relationship Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Periodic Wave in a Medium"] --> B["Three Defining Quantities"]
    B --> C["Wavelength lambda = spatial period"]
    B --> D["Time Period T = temporal period"]

    C --> F["Derived Quantity: Wave Speed v"]
    D --> F
    F --> G["v equals lambda divided by T"]
    D --> H["Derived Quantity: Frequency f"]
    H --> G
    G --> I["Universal Wave Equation: v equals lambda times f"]
    C --> J["Angular Wave Number k equals 2pi over lambda"]
    D --> K["Angular Frequency omega equals 2pi over T"]
    J --> L["Alternative Speed Form: v equals omega over k"]
    K --> L
    I --> M["Engineering Applications"]
    L --> M
    M --> N["Telecom and Antenna Design"]
    M --> O["Medical Ultrasound Imaging"]
    M --> P["Optical Fibre Networks"]
    M --> Q["Seismic Wave Analysis"]
```

---

### 4.2 Textual Schematic: Visualising One Wavelength and One Period

```
         y (displacement)
         │
    +A ──┤      ●←——— Crest 1          ●←——— Crest 2
         │    ╱   ╲                  ╱   ╲
         │  ╱       ╲              ╱       ╲
   ──────┼●─────────●────────────●─────────●─────── x (distance)
         │            ╲        ╱              ╲
         │              ╲    ╱                  ╲
    -A ──┤                ●←—— Trough            ●
         │
         └───────────────────────────────────────────►
              |◄──────── λ ────────►|
                                    |◄──────── λ ────────►|

   SPATIAL VIEW  → Wavelength λ is crest-to-crest distance.

   TEMPORAL VIEW (at a fixed point x):
         y(t)
         │
    +A ──┤   ●         ●         ●         ●
         │ ╱   ╲     ╱   ╲     ╱   ╲     ╱   ╲
   ──────┼●─────●───●─────●───●─────●───●─────●───► t
         │        ╲ ╱       ╲ ╱       ╲ ╱
    -A ──┤         ●         ●         ●
         │
         └───────────────────────────────────────►
              |◄── T ──►|◄── T ──►|◄── T ──►|

   One full time period T is peak-to-peak time.
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Wave-Information Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Source / Oscillator"] --> S2["Wave Generator"]
    S2 --> S3["Medium of Propagation"]

    S1 -.defines.-> P2["Period T"]
    S3 -.governs.-> P3["Speed v"]
    S3 -.governs.-> P4["Wavelength lambda"]
    P1 --> R["v equals lambda times f"]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    P4 --> R
    R --> APP["Engineering Use Cases"]
    APP --> A1["Antenna Sizing"]

    APP --> A3["Communication Bandwidth"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define wavelength and time period of a wave. State their SI units.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Wavelength ($\lambda$):** It is the distance between two nearest points on a wave that are in the same phase (e.g., two consecutive crests or two consecutive troughs). **SI Unit: metre (m).**
- **Time Period ($T$):** It is the time required for a wave to complete one full oscillation or cycle at a given point. **SI Unit: second (s).**

---

#### **Q2. The frequency of a sound wave is 440 Hz and its speed in air is 352 m/s. Calculate the wavelength and the time period of the wave.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $f = 440$ Hz, $v = 352$ m/s.

**Time Period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{440} \approx 2.27 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{s} = 2.27 \, \text{ms}$$

**Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{352}{440} = 0.8 \, \text{m} = 80 \, \text{cm}$$

**Result:** $T \approx 2.27$ ms, $\lambda = 0.8$ m.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and **always** offer an internal choice. Both Question A and Question B below carry equal weight; attempt **any one** as per the official KTU 2024 template.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Speed-Frequency-Wavelength Triad**
`[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024, Q4b]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relation $v = \lambda f$ for a periodic wave. Clearly state the meaning of every symbol.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ocean wave has a time period of 8 s and a wavelength of 40 m. Find (i) the wave speed, (ii) the frequency, and (iii) the distance travelled by the wave in 2 minutes.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

**Brief recap (board-style answer):**

1. Wave speed is the distance travelled by the wave pattern per unit time.
2. In time $T$, the wave moves forward by one full wavelength $\lambda$.
3. Hence $v = \lambda / T$.
4. By definition, frequency $f = 1/T$.
5. Substituting: $v = \lambda f$, where $v$ is in m/s, $\lambda$ in m, $f$ in Hz.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $T = 8$ s, $\lambda = 40$ m.

**(i) Wave speed:**

$$v = \frac{\lambda}{T} = \frac{40 \, \text{m}}{8 \, \text{s}} = 5 \, \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \, \text{Hz}$$

**(iii) Distance travelled in 2 minutes (120 s):**

$$d = v \times t = 5 \, \text{m/s} \times 120 \, \text{s} = 600 \, \text{m}$$

**Result:** $v = 5$ m/s, $f = 0.125$ Hz, $d = 600$ m.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Electromagnetic Wave Analysis**
`[KTU University Exam - Model 2024, Internal Choice Path]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms with units: (i) Wavelength, (ii) Time period, (iii) Frequency, (iv) Wave number.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A microwave oven operates at a frequency of 2.45 GHz. Calculate the wavelength of the microwaves. State one engineering significance of this wavelength.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Quantity | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Wavelength ($\lambda$)** | Spatial period — distance over which the wave repeats. | **m** |
| **Time Period ($T$)** | Temporal period — time for one complete cycle. | **s** |
| **Frequency ($f$)** | Number of complete cycles per second; $f = 1/T$. | **Hz (s⁻¹)** |
| **Wave Number ($k$)** | Spatial frequency; $k = 2\pi/\lambda$. | **rad/m** |

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $f = 2.45$ GHz $= 2.45 \times 10^9$ Hz, $v = c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.45 \times 10^9} \approx 0.1224 \, \text{m} \approx 12.24 \, \text{cm}$$

**Engineering significance:** This wavelength is comparable to the size of water molecules, so microwave radiation at 2.45 GHz is **efficiently absorbed by water-containing food**, heating it from within — this is the operating principle of every domestic microwave oven.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $f$ and $T$:** Frequency is the *inverse* of period. A student writing $T = f$ instead of $T = 1/f$ will be marked **zero** for that sub-part.
> 2. **Unit conversions:** A frequency given in **MHz or GHz** must be converted to **Hz** before substituting. Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for unit errors.
> 3. **Wavelength vs. Amplitude:** A student confusing "wavelength" with "amplitude" is a recurring KTU first-year error. Wavelength is a **length**, not a height.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds** in distance-time problems — a classic 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Writing the wave equation derivation but not stating SI units** of every quantity — a 1-mark deduction in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Wavelength ($\lambda$):** distance of one full cycle in **space** — unit **m**.
- ✅ **Time Period ($T$):** duration of one full cycle in **time** — unit **s**.
- ✅ **Frequency ($f$):** number of cycles per second — unit **Hz** — $f = 1/T$.
- ✅ **Universal wave equation:** $v = \lambda f = \lambda / T$ — valid for **all** linear waves.
- ✅ **Angular frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi / T$ — unit **rad/s**.
- ✅ **Wave number:** $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ — unit **rad/m**.
- ✅ **Alternative speed form:** $v = \omega / k$.
- ✅ **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — use this for all EM wave questions unless told otherwise.
- ✅ **Speed of sound in air (20 °C):** $v \approx 343$ m/s.
- ✅ **Memory hook:** "**v = λ f**" → **"velocity = length × frequency"** (a length times a per-second gives a per-second-length, which is a speed).
- ✅ **Conversion discipline:** Always convert kHz → Hz (×10³), MHz → Hz (×10⁶), GHz → Hz (×10⁹) **before** substituting into formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transverse Vibrations in a Stretched String - Derivation of Velocity and Frequency - Laws of Transverse Vibration; Acoustics - Reverberation and Echo
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE AND LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transverse Vibrations in a Stretched String - Derivation of Velocity and Frequency - Laws of Transverse Vibration; Acoustics - Reverberation and Echo

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transverse Vibrations in a Stretched String

### Formal Definition
A **transverse vibration** in a stretched string is a periodic oscillatory motion in which each particle of the medium (string) moves **perpendicular** to the direction of wave propagation along the string. When a stretched string is plucked, struck, or bowed, it vibrates transversely, producing standing waves with discrete nodes and antinodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A stretched string is a one-dimensional bounded system. Only those frequencies that satisfy the boundary conditions (fixed at both ends) are permitted — these are the natural modes of vibration, governed by the fundamental equation of a transverse wave.

**Key Parameters (with SI Units):**
- Tension in string: $T$ (Newton, **N**)
- Mass per unit length (linear density): $\mu$ or $m$ (kg/m)
- Length of string: $L$ (metre, **m**)
- Velocity of transverse wave: $v$ (m/s)
- Frequency of vibration: $f$ (Hertz, **Hz**)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a long rope tied to a wall. If you flick the free end **up and down** (perpendicular to the rope's length), the disturbance travels **along the rope** towards the wall, but your hand and each point on the rope move **side-to-side**. The wave moves horizontally, the rope moves vertically — this is exactly what transverse vibration looks like. Similarly, a guitar string when plucked vibrates transversely; the sound you hear is just the air being pushed by these sideways oscillations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for engineering?** Musical instruments (guitars, violins, pianos), sonar systems, and string-based sensors all rely on transverse vibration physics. The same equations determine the pitch of a violin and the failure mode of a bridge cable.

---

## 1.2 Acoustics — Reverberation and Echo

### Reverberation — Formal Definition
**Reverberation** is the persistence of sound in an enclosed space after the original sound source has stopped, caused by multiple successive reflections of sound waves from the surfaces (walls, ceiling, floor) of the enclosure.

**Reverberation Time ($T_{60}$ or $RT_{60}$):** The time required for the sound intensity level to fall by **60 dB** (i.e., the sound pressure to drop to one-millionth of its original value, or intensity to $10^{-6}$ of original) after the source is switched off.

### Echo — Formal Definition
An **echo** is a distinct, separately recognizable repetition of a sound caused by reflection from a distant surface (such as a cliff, building, or hillside) arriving at the listener's ear **after** the direct sound has died away.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> **Reverberation** is what you experience clapping inside an empty cathedral — the sound "lingers" and blurs into a smooth tail because reflections arrive almost continuously. 
> **Echo** is what you experience shouting across a deep valley — you hear your own voice returned as a distinct, separate repetition because the reflecting surface is far enough that the gap between original and reflected sound exceeds ~0.1 second (the human ear's resolution limit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Echo Condition:** For a human listener to perceive an echo, the reflected sound must reach the ear **at least 0.1 s** after the original. Since sound travels at ~344 m/s in air at 20°C, this translates to a minimum reflecting surface distance of:
>
> $$d_{\min} = \frac{v \times t}{2} = \frac{344 \times 0.1}{2} = 17.2 \text{ m}$$
>
> Hence, any reflecting wall must be at least **17.2 m** away for a clear echo.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverberation vs. Echo — The Key Distinction:**
>
> | Phenomenon | Cause | Distinguishable? |
> |---|---|---|
> | Echo | Single (or few) strong reflection from a distant surface | **Yes** — separate repetition heard |
> | Reverberation | Many rapid reflections in an enclosed space | **No** — sound blurs into a smooth tail |

---

## 1.3 Standing Waves and Modes (Preview)

When a stretched string vibrates transversely, the fixed ends must always be **nodes** (zero displacement). The simplest standing wave has a single antinode in the middle (fundamental mode); higher modes have 2, 3, 4... antinodes. Each permitted pattern is called a **harmonic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequency Series on a Stretched String:**
> - Fundamental (1st harmonic): $f_1 = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
> - 2nd harmonic: $f_2 = 2f_1$
> - 3rd harmonic: $f_3 = 3f_1$
> - $n$-th harmonic: $f_n = n f_1 = \dfrac{n}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$

---

## 1.4 Coefficient of Absorption (Preview)

The **absorption coefficient** ($\alpha$) of a surface is defined as the ratio of sound energy absorbed by the surface to the total sound energy incident on it:

$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Sound energy absorbed by surface}}{\text{Total sound energy incident on surface}}$$

- $\alpha = 1$ (or unity): perfect absorber (open window)
- $\alpha = 0$: perfect reflector (polished marble)

> **Total absorption (in sabins):** $A = \sum \alpha_i S_i$, where $S_i$ is the area of the $i$-th surface having absorption coefficient $\alpha_i$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic — Why the Formula Has This Form

### The Core Idea Behind Velocity in a Stretched String
When a transverse impulse travels along a stretched string, the string momentarily takes the shape of a small arc (curved bend). At this instant, each mass element of the bend moves in a small circular arc — undergoing **uniform circular motion**. The required centripetal force is supplied entirely by the **tension** in the string. Balancing this centripetal force against the mass-times-centripetal-acceleration yields the wave speed.

### Step-by-Step Logic Chain
1. A transverse wave of speed $v$ travels along a string of linear density $\mu$ under tension $T$.
2. Imagine the wave passes over a small imaginary pulley of radius $r$ — the string wraps around it through a small angle $\theta$.
3. The string tension $T$ acts along both straight portions of the string; their vector sum provides a **net inward radial force** $= 2T\sin(\theta/2)$.
4. The small arc of length $ds = r\theta$ has mass $\mu \, ds = \mu r \theta$.
5. This mass executes uniform circular motion of speed $v$, requiring centripetal force $\dfrac{(\mu r\theta)v^2}{r}$.
6. Equating force from tension to required centripetal force cancels $r$ and $\theta$, leaving $v = \sqrt{T/\mu}$.

---

## 2.2 Frequency of a Vibrating String

A standing wave is formed on a string fixed at both ends. The boundary condition requires a **node at each end**, so the string length must contain an integer number of half-wavelengths:

$$L = \frac{n\lambda_n}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_n = \frac{2L}{n}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

Using $v = f\lambda$ and $v = \sqrt{T/\mu}$:

$$f_n = \frac{n}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

---

## 2.3 Laws of Transverse Vibration of Strings

These three laws govern how frequency depends on length, tension, and mass:

| **Law** | **Statement** | **Mathematical Form** |
|---|---|---|
| Law of Length | Frequency is inversely proportional to the vibrating length (at constant $T$, $\mu$) | $f \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$ |
| Law of Tension | Frequency is directly proportional to the square root of tension (at constant $L$, $\mu$) | $f \propto \sqrt{T}$ |
| Law of Mass | Frequency is inversely proportional to the square root of mass per unit length (at constant $L$, $T$) | $f \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sonometer Experiment:** These three laws are verified experimentally using a **sonometer** — a hollow wooden box with a wire stretched over it, whose tension is adjusted using hanging weights, and whose effective vibrating length is altered using movable bridges.

---

## 2.4 Reverberation — Sabine's Formula Derivation Logic

Wallace Clement Sabine (Harvard, ~1900) empirically discovered that the **reverberation time** $T$ of a hall is:

$$T = K \cdot \frac{V}{A}$$

where:
- $V$ = volume of the hall (in m³)
- $A$ = total absorption of all surfaces = $\sum \alpha_i S_i$ (in sabins or metric sabins)
- $K$ = a constant determined experimentally

He found $K \approx 0.161$ s/m when $V$ is in m³ and $A$ is in metric sabins (m² of open-window equivalent absorption). This gives the celebrated **Sabine's Formula**:

$$T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \, V}{A} = \frac{0.161 \, V}{\sum \alpha_i S_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Verification:**
> $\dfrac{\text{s} \cdot \text{m}^3}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{sabins}} = \text{s}$ (since 1 sabin = 1 m² of equivalent open-window absorption, units cancel correctly to give seconds.)

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity of transverse wave | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$ | m/s | Speed at which disturbance travels along string |
| 2 | Wavelength of $n$-th mode | $\lambda_n = \dfrac{2L}{n}$ | m | Distance for one full wave pattern |
| 3 | Frequency of $n$-th harmonic | $f_n = \dfrac{n}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$ | Hz | Allowed vibration frequencies |
| 4 | Fundamental frequency | $f_1 = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$ | Hz | Lowest possible pitch |
| 5 | Tension for given $f_1$ | $T = 4L^2 f_1^2 \mu$ | N | Design equation for string instruments |
| 6 | Time period | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{f_n}$ | s | Time for one full oscillation |
| 7 | Sabine's reverberation formula | $T_{60} = \dfrac{0.161 \, V}{A}$ | s | Time for sound to decay by 60 dB |
| 8 | Total absorption | $A = \sum \alpha_i S_i$ | sabin | Sum of absorption-area products |
| 9 | Average absorption coefficient | $\bar{\alpha} = \dfrac{\sum \alpha_i S_i}{\sum S_i}$ | unitless | Effective absorption per unit area |
| 10 | Minimum echo distance | $d_{\min} = \dfrac{v_s \cdot t_{\min}}{2}$ | m | $t_{\min} \approx 0.1$ s for human ear |
| 11 | Loudness level (Loudness in phons) | $L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | dB | Logarithmic measure of sound intensity |
| 12 | Intensity $\propto$ amplitude² | $I \propto A^2$ | — | Energy carried by wave |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Domain** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| **Musical Instrument Design** | Choosing string gauge ($\mu$), length ($L$), and tuning tension ($T$) for desired pitch |
| **Architectural Acoustics** | Designing concert halls, auditoriums, classrooms for optimal reverberation time |
| **Civil Engineering** | Predicting vibration modes in bridge cables, suspension cables, guy-wires |
| **Audio Engineering** | Echo cancellation in recording studios, designing anechoic chambers |
| **Medical Acoustics** | Ultrasound imaging probe design (transverse wave principles in piezoelectric crystals) |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Vibration analysis of rotating shafts and belts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Velocity of Transverse Wave in a Stretched String

### Setup and Assumptions
- A long, flexible, uniform string of linear mass density $\mu$ (kg/m) is stretched horizontally under tension $T$ (N).
- The string is **perfectly flexible** (no bending stiffness) and **homogeneous**.
- A transverse wave pulse travels along the string from left to right with speed $v$.

### Conceptual Trick
In the **reference frame of the pulse**, the string appears to move to the left, sliding over a small imaginary frictionless pulley of radius $r$. This converts a wave-problem into a simple **uniform circular motion problem** — easy to solve.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a small element of the string wrapping around the imaginary pulley through an angle $\theta$ (small angle, in radians). The chord length (which approximates the arc length for small $\theta$) is:

$$ds = r\theta$$

**Step 2:** The mass of this curved element is:

$$dm = \mu \, ds = \mu r \theta$$

**Step 3:** Tension $T$ acts tangentially along the string at both ends of the element. Resolve each tension into components:
- Along the radial (inward) direction: $T\sin(\theta/2)$ at each end.
- The components along the bisector (perpendicular to the radius) cancel by symmetry.

So the **net inward radial force** on the element is:

$$F_{\text{net}} = 2T \sin\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the small-angle approximation $\sin(x) \approx x$ for $x \ll 1$:

$$F_{\text{net}} = 2T \cdot \frac{\theta}{2} = T\theta$$

**Step 5:** This net radial force provides the **centripetal force** required for the element to move in a circular path of radius $r$ at speed $v$:

$$F_{\text{centripetal}} = \frac{dm \cdot v^2}{r} = \frac{(\mu r \theta) v^2}{r} = \mu \theta v^2$$

**Step 6:** Equate the net force from tension (Step 4) to the required centripetal force (Step 5):

$$T\theta = \mu \theta v^2$$

**Step 7:** Cancel $\theta$ (non-zero, since the string is curved) from both sides:

$$T = \mu v^2$$

**Step 8:** Solve for the wave speed $v$:

$$\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}\,}$$

### Analytical Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observations from the Derivation:**
> 1. The radius $r$ cancelled out — meaning the wave speed is **independent of the shape** of the disturbance.
> 2. The angle $\theta$ cancelled out — meaning the formula is valid for **any amplitude** (linear regime).
> 3. **The string is assumed perfectly flexible** — a stiff string (like a rod) has additional restoring force from bending stiffness, giving higher wave speed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Frequency of Vibration of a Stretched String

### Setup
The string is fixed at both ends $x = 0$ and $x = L$. A standing wave is established.

### Boundary Conditions
Both fixed ends must be **nodes** (zero displacement at all times):
$$y(0, t) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad y(L, t) = 0$$

### General Standing Wave Solution
The general standing wave equation on a string is:

$$y(x, t) = A \sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$$

where $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ (wave number) and $\omega = 2\pi f$ (angular frequency).

### Applying Boundary Conditions

**Condition 1:** At $x = 0$:
$$y(0, t) = A \sin(0)\cos(\omega t) = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (automatically satisfied)}$$

**Condition 2:** At $x = L$:
$$y(L, t) = A \sin(kL)\cos(\omega t) = 0 \quad \text{for all } t$$

This requires:

$$\sin(kL) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

### Extracting Allowed Wavelengths

$$k_n = \frac{n\pi}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2\pi}{\lambda_n} = \frac{n\pi}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,\lambda_n = \frac{2L}{n}\,}$$

### Extracting Allowed Frequencies
Using $v = f\lambda$ and $v = \sqrt{T/\mu}$:

$$f_n = \frac{v}{\lambda_n} = \frac{\sqrt{T/\mu}}{2L/n} = \frac{n}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_n = \frac{n}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}\,, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\,}$$

### Verification: Fundamental Mode ($n = 1$)

$$\lambda_1 = 2L \quad (\text{half a wavelength fits in the string length — matches a single antinode at the centre})$$

$$f_1 = \frac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

This is the **lowest natural frequency** — the pitch you hear when plucking a guitar string in its standard mode.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Sabine's Reverberation Formula

### Empirical Foundation
Sabine measured reverberation times in many rooms of different sizes and absorption levels, then proposed that:

1. Reverberation time **increases linearly** with the volume $V$ of the room.
2. Reverberation time **decreases inversely** with the total absorption $A$.

So:
$$T \propto \frac{V}{A}$$

### Determining the Proportionality Constant
Sabine performed hundreds of experiments and found the constant $K = 0.161$ s/m in SI units. He defined absorption in "metric sabins" — one metric sabin = 1 m² of perfectly absorbing surface ($\alpha = 1$).

### Final Sabine's Formula

$$\boxed{\,T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \, V}{\sum \alpha_i S_i}\,}$$

### Alternative Form
Since $A = \bar{\alpha} \cdot S_{\text{total}}$ where $\bar{\alpha}$ is the average absorption coefficient and $S_{\text{total}}$ is the total interior surface area:

$$T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \, V}{\bar{\alpha} \, S_{\text{total}}}$$

### Quick Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

> **Problem:** A hall has volume $V = 2000$ m³ and total surface area $S = 1500$ m² with average absorption coefficient $\bar{\alpha} = 0.2$. Find the reverberation time.

**Solution:**

$$A = \bar{\alpha} \cdot S = 0.2 \times 1500 = 300 \text{ sabins}$$

$$T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \times 2000}{300} = \frac{322}{300} = 1.073 \text{ s}$$

This is a **good reverberation time** for a concert hall (typical ideal range: 1.5–2 s for orchestral music, 0.5–1 s for speech halls).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Minimum Distance for an Echo

### Condition
Human ear can distinguish two sounds only if they are separated by at least $t_{\min} = 0.1$ s.

### Geometry
Sound travels from source to reflecting wall and back — total path = $2d$.

### Calculation

$$2d = v_s \times t \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \frac{v_s \cdot t_{\min}}{2}$$

Taking $v_s = 344$ m/s at 20°C:

$$\boxed{\,d_{\min} = \frac{344 \times 0.1}{2} = 17.2 \text{ m}\,}$$

### Temperature Correction
At temperature $T$ (in °C), the speed of sound in air is:

$$v_s = v_0 \sqrt{1 + \frac{T}{273}}$$

where $v_0 = 331$ m/s at 0°C.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Key Equations at a Glance

| **Scenario** | **Input Knowns** | **Target Output** | **Working Equation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Find wave speed | $T$, $\mu$ | $v$ | $v = \sqrt{T/\mu}$ |
| Find fundamental frequency | $L$, $T$, $\mu$ | $f_1$ | $f_1 = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$ |
| Find tension for desired pitch | $L$, $\mu$, $f_1$ | $T$ | $T = 4L^2 f_1^2 \mu$ |
| Find wavelength of $n$-th harmonic | $L$, $n$ | $\lambda_n$ | $\lambda_n = \dfrac{2L}{n}$ |
| Find reverberation time | $V$, $\alpha_i$, $S_i$ | $T_{60}$ | $T_{60} = \dfrac{0.161 V}{\sum \alpha_i S_i}$ |
| Find minimum echo distance | $v_s$, $t_{\min}$ | $d_{\min}$ | $d_{\min} = \dfrac{v_s \, t_{\min}}{2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Transverse Wave on a Stretched String

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[String Under Tension T] --> B[Transverse Pulse Generated]
    B --> C[Pulse Travels Along String]
    C --> D[String Element Bends Over Imaginary Pulley Radius r]
    D --> E[Tension T Provides Net Radial Force]
    E --> F[Net Force Equals Centripetal Force]
    F --> G[T theta Equals mu theta v squared]
    G --> H[Cancel theta Both Sides]
    H --> I[T Equals mu v squared]
    I --> J[Solve for v]
    J --> K[Wave Speed v Equals sqrt of T over mu]
    K --> L[Boundary Conditions Both Ends Fixed]
    L --> M[Standing Wave Pattern Forms]
    M --> N[Allowed Wavelengths lambda n Equals 2L over n]
    N --> O[Allowed Frequencies f n Equals n over 2L times sqrt of T over mu]
    O --> P[Harmonic Series Generated]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Flow — Reverberation Process in a Hall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Sound Source Emits Energy E0] --> B1[Direct Sound Reaches Listener]
    B1 --> C1[Sound Strikes Wall Surface]
    C1 --> D1[Part Absorbed alpha times E]
    C1 --> E1[Part Reflected 1 minus alpha times E]
    E1 --> F1[Reflected Sound Strikes Next Surface]
    F1 --> D1
    F1 --> E1
    E1 --> G1[Multiple Successive Reflections]
    G1 --> H1[Sound Intensity Decays Exponentially]
    H1 --> I1[Time for 60 dB Drop Measured]
    I1 --> J1[Reverberation Time T60]
    J1 --> K1[T60 Equals 0.161 V over Sum alpha S]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Sonometer Setup (Laws Verification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sonometer Box Hollow Wooden Resonator] --> S2[Steel Wire Stretched Over Bridges]
    S2 --> S3[Movable Bridge A Changes Effective Length L]
    S2 --> S4[Movable Bridge B Changes Effective Length L]
    S2 --> S5[Hanging Weights at Pulley Provide Tension T]
    S5 --> S6[Different Masses Produce Different T]
    S2 --> S7[Wire Plucked at Centre]
    S7 --> S8[Transverse Vibration Produced]
    S8 --> S9[Frequency f Measured by Tuning Fork Comparison]
    S9 --> S10[Law of Length f inverse L]
    S9 --> S11[Law of Tension f square root T]
    S9 --> S12[Law of Mass f inverse square root mu]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Standing Wave Modes on a String

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Mode n Equals 1 Fundamental] --> M1a[One Half Wavelength in String]
    M1a --> M1b[Nodes at Both Ends Single Antinode at Centre]
    M2[Mode n Equals 2 First Overtone] --> M2a[Two Half Wavelengths in String]
    M2a --> M2b[Nodes at Ends and Centre Two Antinodes]
    M3[Mode n Equals 3 Second Overtone] --> M3a[Three Half Wavelengths in String]
    M3a --> M3b[Nodes at Ends plus Two Internal Three Antinodes]
    M4[Mode n General] --> M4a[n Half Wavelengths in String]
    M4a --> M4b[n plus 1 Nodes n Antinodes]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**State and explain the three laws of transverse vibration of a stretched string.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three laws of transverse vibration are:

1. **Law of Length:** For a string of given mass per unit length and under constant tension, the fundamental frequency of vibration is **inversely proportional** to the vibrating length of the string.
$$f \propto \frac{1}{L} \quad \text{(at constant } T, \mu\text{)}$$

2. **Law of Tension:** For a string of given length and mass per unit length, the fundamental frequency is **directly proportional** to the square root of the tension in the string.
$$f \propto \sqrt{T} \quad \text{(at constant } L, \mu\text{)}$$

3. **Law of Mass:** For a string of given length and tension, the fundamental frequency is **inversely proportional** to the square root of the mass per unit length of the string.
$$f \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu}} \quad \text{(at constant } L, T\text{)}$$

These laws are verified using the **sonometer** experiment and form the basis of musical instrument design and acoustic engineering.

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark each law + 1 mark for statement + 1 mark for mathematical form. Award 1 mark for naming, 1 mark for proportionality, 1 mark for math expression.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define reverberation. What is reverberation time? Write Sabine's formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reverberation** is the persistence of sound in an enclosed space after the original source has ceased, due to multiple successive reflections from the walls, ceiling, and floor of the enclosure.
