| Increase temperature of liquid | Density and K change; Λ changes | v decreases for water near 4 °C, increases otherwise |
| Increase acoustic amplitude | Brightness of orders changes (Raman–Nath regime) | v unaffected (amplitude independent) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Ultrasonic Diffractometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RF OSCILLATOR\n2 to 5 MHz] -->|electrical signal| B[QUARTZ CRYSTAL\npiezoelectric transducer]
    B -->|ultrasonic waves| C[LIQUID CELL\nacoustic grating forms here]
    L1[MONOCHROMATIC LIGHT SOURCE\nsodium lamp or He-Ne laser] --> M1[COLLIMATING LENS]
    M1 --> C
    C -->|diffracted beams| M2[CONVERGING LENS]
    M2 --> SC[DIFFRACTION PATTERN\non micrometer eyepiece]
    SC --> OBS[OBSERVER MEASURES\norder positions x sub n]
    OBS --> CALC[CALCULATION\nv = f n lambda D over x sub n]
    F1[FREQUENCY COUNTER\nmonitors f] -.-> A
    T1[THERMOMETER\nrecords T] -.-> C
```

## 4.2 Optical Path Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Light Source S] --> L1a[Collimating Lens L1]
    L1a --> S2[Parallel Beam enters Cell]
    subgraph CELL[Liquid Cell with Ultrasonic Grating]
        direction TB
        Q1[Quartz Crystal] -->|emits sound waves| LIQ[Transparent Liquid\nperiodic n x]
    end
    S2 --> LIQ
    LIQ -->|diffracted orders 0, +1, +2 ...| L2a[Converging Lens L2]
    L2a --> SC1[Micrometer Scale\ndistance D from cell]
    SC1 --> EYE[Measuring Microscope\nreads x sub n]
```

## 4.3 Diffraction Pattern Visualised

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Central Order\n0th maximum brightest] --- P1[First Order\nplus or minus n = 1]
    P1 --- P2[Second Order\nplus or minus n = 2]
    P2 --- P3[Third Order\nplus or minus n = 3]
    style C0 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P2 fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P3 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Working principle in one line:** The acoustic wave makes the liquid behave as a diffraction grating of element Λ. Measuring the diffraction angles of monochromatic light gives Λ, and v = fΛ gives the ultrasonic velocity.

## 4.4 Raman–Nath vs Bragg Regimes (Important Classification)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ultrasonic Grating Regime] --> B{Acoustic Amplitude}
    B -->|small, Q less than 1| C[RAMAN NATH REGIME\nmany orders visible\nDebye Sears formula valid]
    B -->|large, Q greater than 1| D[BRAGG REGIME\nonly first order strong\nangle satisfies Bragg condition]
    style C fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1d8348
    style D fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21
```

where the **Klein–Cook parameter** is
$$Q = \frac{2\pi \lambda L}{\Lambda^2}$$
L is the acoustic beam width, λ the optical wavelength, and Λ the ultrasonic wavelength.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**State the Debye–Sears effect. How is it used in an ultrasonic diffractometer?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Debye–Sears effect** is the phenomenon in which a beam of monochromatic light, on passing through a transparent medium carrying a high-frequency ultrasonic wave, gets diffracted into several orders symmetrically placed about the central maximum. The periodic compressions and rarefactions in the medium produce a sinusoidal variation of refractive index, so that the medium acts as a **phase grating** of element equal to the ultrasonic wavelength Λ.

In the **ultrasonic diffractometer**, this effect is exploited as follows: a piezoelectric quartz crystal excited by an RF oscillator produces ultrasonic waves of known frequency *f* in the liquid. A parallel beam of monochromatic light is sent perpendicular to the sound direction. The diffraction pattern is observed with a microscope, and the angular position of the *n*-th order gives Λ = nλ/sin θ. The velocity is then calculated as **v = fΛ**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]
**Why is monochromatic light used in an ultrasonic diffractometer? What is the role of the RF oscillator?**

**Model Answer:**
**Monochromatic light is essential** because the diffraction grating equation (e sin θ = nλ) requires a single, sharply defined wavelength λ. If white light were used, every colour would diffract at a different angle, the orders would overlap into broad coloured bands, and the position *x_n* could not be measured precisely. A sodium lamp with filter (λ = 589.3 nm) or a He–Ne laser (λ = 632.8 nm) is therefore used.

The **RF oscillator** supplies a stable, high-frequency alternating voltage (1–10 MHz) to the quartz crystal. The crystal, by the **inverse piezoelectric effect**, vibrates mechanically at this same frequency, thereby generating ultrasonic waves in the liquid. The RF oscillator must be tunable so that the crystal can be driven at its **resonant frequency**, where the vibration amplitude (and hence diffraction intensity) is maximum.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working of an ultrasonic diffractometer**. Explain the role of the Debye–Sears effect. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **velocity of ultrasonic waves** in a liquid using the grating equation. A diffractometer uses a quartz crystal of frequency **2.5 MHz** and a sodium lamp (λ = 5893 Å). The first-order diffraction is observed at **x₁ = 0.65 cm** with the microscope at **D = 80 cm**. Calculate the velocity of ultrasonic waves in the liquid. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

| Component | Function | Marks |
|-----------|----------|-------|
| RF Oscillator | Generates high-frequency (MHz) electrical signal | 1 |
| Quartz Crystal | Piezoelectric transducer; converts electrical to mechanical (ultrasonic) vibrations | 1.5 |
| Liquid Cell | Holds the liquid; acoustic waves produce a periodic refractive index grating | 1 |
| Monochromatic Source | Provides collimated light of known wavelength λ | 1 |
| Optical System (lenses) | Collimates light, projects diffraction pattern on micrometer scale | 1 |
| Measuring Microscope | Measures *x_n* and *D* | 0.5 |
| Frequency Counter | Monitors the frequency *f* of the RF oscillator | 0.5 |
| Working Principle Explanation | Debye–Sears effect: compressions/rarefactions → n(x) → phase grating | 0.5 |

**Working:** The RF oscillator drives the quartz crystal at its resonant frequency. The crystal launches longitudinal ultrasonic waves into the liquid. The refractive index varies as n(x) = n₀ + Δn cos(2πx/Λ), creating an optical phase grating of element **Λ**. Monochromatic light passing perpendicularly diffracts into orders at angles satisfying **Λ sin θ = nλ**. By measuring these angles, Λ and hence *v = fΛ* are obtained.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Derivation** (already given in Section 3.1, summarising the key steps):
1. Phase grating formed with element e = Λ.
2. Grating equation: Λ sin θ_n = nλ.
3. Velocity: v = fΛ = f·nλ/sin θ_n.
4. Small-angle: sin θ_n ≈ x_n/D.
5. Final form: v = f n λ D / x_n.

**Numerical Computation:**
Given: f = 2.5 × 10⁶ Hz, λ = 5893 × 10⁻¹⁰ m = 5.893 × 10⁻⁷ m, n = 1, x₁ = 0.65 × 10⁻² m, D = 80 × 10⁻² m = 0.80 m.

$$v = \frac{2.5 \times 10^6 \times 1 \times 5.893 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.80}{0.65 \times 10^{-2}}$$

**Step-by-step simplification:**

Numerator:
$$2.5 \times 10^6 \times 5.893 \times 10^{-7} = 1.4733$$
$$1.4733 \times 0.80 = 1.1786$$

Denominator: 0.65 × 10⁻² = 6.5 × 10⁻³

$$v = \frac{1.1786}{6.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 181.3\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 181\ \text{m/s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A velocity this low indicates the medium is a **gas** rather than a liquid — useful real-world interpretation. (Mark awarded for physical reasoning: **+0.5 bonus** often seen in KTU valuation.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **Debye–Sears effect** in detail. Show that the liquid medium carrying ultrasonic waves acts as a **diffraction grating of grating element Λ** and obtain the condition for the *n*-th order maximum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the **advantages and limitations** of the ultrasonic diffractometer method? In an experiment, the *second-order* diffraction spot is obtained at an angle of **θ₂ = 2° 15′** when ultrasonic waves of frequency **f = 3 MHz** are used. If the light source is a He–Ne laser (λ = 6328 Å), find the velocity of sound in the liquid. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Debye–Sears Effect (2 marks):**
When plane-progressive ultrasonic waves propagate through a transparent liquid, the alternating compressions and rarefactions produce periodic density and pressure variations. Since the refractive index of a medium depends on its density, *n* varies sinusoidally along the wave-propagation direction with a spatial period exactly equal to the ultrasonic wavelength Λ.

**Mathematical treatment (3 marks):**
$$n(x) = n_0 + \Delta n \cos\left(\frac{2\pi x}{\Lambda}\right)$$
The medium now transmits light as if it were a **phase grating** of element
$$e = \Lambda$$

**Grating condition (2 marks):**
For a transmission phase grating with element *e*,
$$e \sin\theta_n = n\lambda \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \Lambda \sin\theta_n = n\lambda$$
which is the condition for the *n*-th order maximum.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- High accuracy (within 1 %) for sound velocity in liquids.
- Non-destructive and simple to perform.
- Direct measurement of *v* in a wide range of liquids.
- Can be extended to study temperature/pressure dependence.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- Requires a transparent liquid (cannot be used for opaque media).
- Liquid must be free of bubbles and dust (they scatter light).
- The Debye–Sears formula is valid only in the **Raman–Nath regime** (small acoustic amplitude).
- Frequency stability of the RF oscillator is critical.

**Numerical (3 marks):**
Given: n = 2, θ₂ = 2° 15′ = 2.25° = 2.25 × π/180 rad = 0.03927 rad, f = 3 × 10⁶ Hz, λ = 6328 × 10⁻¹⁰ m.

$$\sin\theta_2 = \sin(2.25°) = 0.03926$$

From grating equation:
$$\Lambda = \frac{n\lambda}{\sin\theta_2} = \frac{2 \times 6328 \times 10^{-10}}{0.03926}$$
$$\Lambda = \frac{1.2656 \times 10^{-6}}{0.03926} = 3.224 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 32.24\ \mu\text{m}$$

Velocity:
$$v = f\Lambda = 3 \times 10^6 \times 3.224 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\boxed{v \approx 96.7\ \text{m/s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result (≈ 97 m/s) is unphysical for a true liquid — it again suggests either an air-like medium or an experiment meant to illustrate the formula rather than measure a real liquid. The expected value for water should be ~1500 m/s. KTU examiners appreciate when students **comment on the physical plausibility** of the answer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks in Ultrasonic Diffractometer questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Λ and λ** — always write *Λ* (ultrasonic wavelength, mm) and *λ* (optical wavelength, nm) clearly. Mixing them costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation** — if the angle is small, use sin θ ≈ tan θ = x/D. Writing sin θ = x/D without justification loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation steps** — KTU demands the *full* derivation of v = f n λ D / x_n. Writing only the final formula = 0 marks for the derivation part (out of 5–7).
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion** — frequency in MHz must be converted to Hz, optical wavelength in nm/Å must be converted to m, and distances in cm must be converted to m. Mixing cm with Hz is the single most common arithmetic error.
> 5. **Not drawing the block diagram with labelled arrows** — a diagram without arrows (signal flow) or without labels (RF oscillator, quartz crystal, liquid cell, light source, microscope) loses 2 marks even if the text is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Debye–Sears effect** → periodic compressions/rarefactions in a liquid create a sinusoidal refractive index variation that acts as a **phase diffraction grating** of element **Λ** (the ultrasonic wavelength).
- The **grating element e = Λ = v/f**, where *v* is ultrasonic velocity and *f* is the driving frequency.
- The **grating equation** for the *n*-th order maximum is **Λ sin θ_n = nλ**, where λ is the *optical* wavelength of the light source.
- **Master formula for ultrasonic velocity:** 
  $$v = \frac{f \, n \, \lambda}{\sin\theta_n} = \frac{f \, n \, \lambda \, D}{x_n}\ \text{(small-angle form)}$$
- **Block diagram essentials:** RF oscillator → quartz crystal → liquid cell (acoustic grating) ← light source → converging lens → micrometer eyepiece.
- **Why monochromatic light?** → to obtain sharp, well-defined diffraction orders.
- **Why perpendicular illumination?** → automatic satisfaction of the Debye–Sears condition; Λ ≫ λ gives small diffraction angles and well-separated orders.
- **Raman–Nath regime** (Q < 1) is the operating regime of the standard diffractometer, where many orders are visible.
- **Bragg regime** (Q > 1) is for high amplitudes, where only the first order is strong.
- **Typical experimental values:** f ≈ 2–5 MHz, Λ ≈ 0.3 mm in water, v (water) ≈ 1500 m/s.
- **Common error to avoid:** never mix up *Λ* (acoustic, mm) with *λ* (optical, nm). Always include units.
- **Sanity check:** the calculated velocity should be in the range 800–2000 m/s for ordinary liquids; lower values (50–200 m/s) suggest gases or measurement errors.
- **Engineering utility:** pharmaceutical testing, oceanography, SONAR calibration, medical ultrasonics, food-oil purity testing, NDT.
- **Related formula (Newton–Laplace):** $v = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ — used to cross-check the experimental value.
- **Refractive index modulation:** $n(x) = n_0 + \Delta n \cos(2\pi x/\Lambda)$ — the fundamental mathematical model of the acoustic grating.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
