## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Pulse-Echo Ultrasound Scanner

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Pulser Circuit] -->|High Voltage RF Pulse| B[Transducer Probe]
    B -->|Ultrasound Beam| C[Patient Body Tissue]
    C -->|Reflected Echoes| B
    B -->|Electrical Echo Signal| D[Receiver Amplifier]
    D --> E[Time Gain Compensation TGC]
    E --> F[Signal Processor]
    F --> G[Display Unit]
    G --> H[Diagnostic Image A scan B scan M scan]
    H --> I[Clinician Interpretation]
```

**Block-by-Block Function Explanation:**

1. **Pulser Circuit:** Generates short (~1–2 μs), high-voltage (~100–300 V) electrical pulses at the desired ultrasound frequency (2–15 MHz).
2. **Transducer Probe:** Converts the electrical pulse into a mechanical ultrasound wave (and vice versa) using the **piezoelectric effect**.
3. **Patient Body Tissue:** The medium through which ultrasound propagates and from which it reflects at acoustic interfaces.
4. **Receiver Amplifier:** Amplifies the weak returning echo signals (often in the microvolt range).
5. **Time Gain Compensation (TGC):** Compensates for attenuation by applying progressively higher gain to echoes returning from greater depths.
6. **Signal Processor:** Performs demodulation, envelope detection, scan conversion, and filtering.
7. **Display Unit:** Renders the final image in A-mode, B-mode, M-mode, or Doppler format.
8. **Clinician Interpretation:** A trained radiologist/sonographer reads the displayed image for diagnosis.

---

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of an Ultrasound Transducer

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Protective Casing] --> B[Matching Layer acoustic impedance matching]
    B --> C[Piezoelectric Crystal PZT or PVDF]
    C --> D[Backing Material damping block]
    D --> E[Electrical Leads to Pulser Receiver]
    C --> F[Acoustic Lens focusing]
    A -.-> F
```

**Key Components and Their Functions:**

- **Protective Casing:** Outer housing, ergonomic grip, and electrical insulation.
- **Matching Layer:** A thin layer (often plastic or epoxy) with acoustic impedance between that of the PZT crystal and the soft tissue. It reduces the impedance mismatch at the front face, allowing more ultrasound to enter the body.
- **Piezoelectric Crystal:** The active element. PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) is most common. PVDF (a polymer) is used in some flexible arrays.
- **Backing Material (Damping Block):** A heavy, acoustically absorbing material (e.g., tungsten-epoxy composite) glued to the back of the crystal. It absorbs ultrasound radiated backward, **shortening the pulse duration** and thereby **improving axial resolution**.
- **Electrical Leads:** Carry the high-voltage pulse to the crystal and the echo signal from it.
- **Acoustic Lens:** A concave plastic lens bonded to the front face that focuses the ultrasound beam to a narrow region at a specific depth.

---

### 4.3 Three Modes of Display — A-scan, B-scan, M-scan

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MODES[Three Display Modes in Medical Ultrasound]
        A1[A MODE Amplitude Modulation] --> A2[Single Crystal]
        A2 --> A3[1D Vertical Spikes on Time Base]
        A3 --> A4[Spike Height = Echo Strength]
        A3 --> A5[Spike Position = Depth]
        
        B1[B MODE Brightness Modulation] --> B2[Linear Phased Array]
        B2 --> B3[2D Cross Sectional Image]
        B3 --> B4[Brightness of Dot = Echo Strength]
        B3 --> B5[Position of Dot = Location in Body]
        
        C1[M MODE Motion Mode] --> C2[Single Crystal Fixed Direction]
        C2 --> C3[Vertical Strip Moving Horizontally]
        C3 --> C4[Dots Brighten at Moving Interfaces]
        C3 --> C5[Time on Horizontal Axis]
    end
```

**Comparison Table of the Three Modes:**

| Feature | A-mode | B-mode | M-mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Full Name** | Amplitude Mode | Brightness Mode | Motion Mode |
| **Dimension** | 1-D (time base) | 2-D (spatial image) | 1-D + time |
| **Display** | Vertical spikes | 2-D brightness map | Moving 1-D trace |
| **Y-axis** | Echo amplitude | Depth | Depth |
| **X-axis** | Time (= depth) | Lateral position | Time |
| **Probe Type** | Single crystal | Phased / linear array | Single crystal |
| **Clinical Use** | Ophthalmology, echoencephalography | General imaging, obstetrics, abdomen | Cardiology (heart valve motion) |
| **First KTU 2024 Examined** | [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] | [KTU University Exam - May 2024] | [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] |

---

### 4.4 Detailed A-Scan Output Schematic (Text Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Transmitted Pulse Initial Spike] --> B[Gel Skin Interface Echo]
    B --> C[Fat Muscle Boundary Echo]
    C --> D[Organ Surface Echo]
    D --> E[Organ Interior Echoes]
    E --> F[Bone or Air Interface Large Echo]
    F --> G[Back Wall Echo Final]
```

**Interpretation:** Each spike corresponds to one tissue interface. The horizontal axis (time) maps directly to depth via $d = ct/2$. Spike **height** indicates **echo strength** (related to $Z$ mismatch). The **spacing** between spikes indicates the **size** of anatomical structures.

---

### 4.5 Signal Flow — From Generation to Image

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 HV Pulser Fires] --> S2[Step 2 PZT Crystal Vibrates]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Ultrasound Enters Body via Gel]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Wave Reflects at Tissue Interfaces]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Echoes Return to Same Crystal]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Crystal Generates Voltage via Reverse Piezoelectric Effect]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Signal Amplified TGC Applied]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Processed and Displayed]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q1. Define acoustic impedance. What is its SI unit? Mention the acoustic impedance of soft tissue.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Acoustic impedance $Z$ of a medium is defined as the product of the density $\rho$ of the medium and the velocity $c$ of sound in that medium:

$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$

The SI unit of acoustic impedance is **kg m⁻² s⁻¹**, also called the **Rayl** (after Lord Rayleigh). The acoustic impedance of soft tissue is approximately $Z = 1.63 \times 10^6$ Rayl.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the piezoelectric effect? Name two materials exhibiting this effect.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **piezoelectric effect** is the phenomenon in which certain crystalline materials generate an electrical voltage across their faces when subjected to mechanical pressure (direct effect), and conversely, undergo mechanical deformation when an electric voltage is applied across them (converse effect). This dual nature is exploited in ultrasound transducers — the **converse effect** is used to **generate** ultrasound, and the **direct effect** is used to **detect** the returning echoes.

Two piezoelectric materials are:
1. **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** — a synthetic ceramic, most widely used in medical transducers.
2. **Quartz (SiO₂)** — a natural crystal, used in early ultrasonic instruments.

(Other acceptable answers: PVDF polymer, Rochelle salt, tourmaline, lithium niobate.)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) Explain the principle of operation of a piezoelectric transducer used in medical ultrasound scanning. With a neat diagram, describe its construction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Operation:**

A piezoelectric transducer works on the principle of the **piezoelectric effect**, discovered by **Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880**. The transducer contains a **piezoelectric crystal** (commonly PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate) that exhibits two complementary effects:

1. **Converse Piezoelectric Effect (Used for Transmission):** When a high-frequency alternating voltage (typically 100–300 V at 2–15 MHz) is applied across the two faces of the crystal, the crystal undergoes rapid mechanical vibrations at the **same frequency** as the applied voltage. These vibrations launch an **ultrasound pulse** into the body via an acoustic coupling gel.

2. **Direct Piezoelectric Effect (Used for Reception):** When the returning echo strikes the crystal, the mechanical pressure deforms the crystal, generating a small alternating voltage across its faces. This voltage is amplified and processed to form the diagnostic image.

The same crystal thus acts as **both transmitter and receiver**, switched rapidly by the scanner (typical pulse repetition frequency ≈ 1–10 kHz).

**Construction (refer to Mermaid Section 4.2):**

The transducer consists of the following components (from back to front):

- **Backing Material (Damping Block):** Made of a dense, acoustically absorptive material (e.g., tungsten-epoxy composite). It absorbs the backward-radiated ultrasound, shortens the pulse length, and improves **axial resolution**.
- **Piezoelectric Crystal:** The active element, typically a disc of PZT of diameter 5–30 mm and thickness ≈ λ/2 at the operating frequency.
- **Matching Layer:** A thin (λ/4) layer of plastic or epoxy with acoustic impedance between that of PZT (~30 MRayl) and soft tissue (~1.6 MRayl). It minimizes the front-face reflection and maximizes ultrasound transmission into the body.
- **Protective Casing:** Outer plastic or metal housing.
- **Electrical Leads:** Coaxial cable connecting to the pulser-receiver.
- **Acoustic Lens:** A concave plastic lens bonded to the front of the matching layer to focus the beam at a specific depth.

**Key Design Equation:** The thickness of the crystal determines its resonant frequency:

$$f = \frac{c_{\text{PZT}}}{2 \cdot t_{\text{crystal}}}$$

For PZT, $c_{\text{PZT}} \approx 4000$ m/s. For a 5 MHz transducer, the required thickness is:

$$t = \frac{4000}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6} = 0.4 \text{ mm}$$

**(b) Explain the A-scan and B-scan modes of ultrasound display with neat diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**A-Scan (Amplitude Mode):**

In A-scan, a **single stationary crystal** is used. The output is a **one-dimensional** display on a cathode-ray oscilloscope (CRO). The **horizontal axis** represents **time** (which is directly proportional to depth using $d = ct/2$). The **vertical axis** represents the **amplitude (strength) of the reflected echo**.

The transmitted pulse appears as a large initial spike at $t = 0$. Each subsequent spike represents the echo from a tissue interface. The **height** of the spike is proportional to the **echo intensity** (and hence to the acoustic impedance mismatch at that interface). The **position** of the spike along the time base gives the **depth** of the interface.

*Clinical use:* Historically used in **ophthalmology** (measuring eye dimensions) and **echoencephalography** (detecting midline shift in the brain). Largely replaced by B-scan today.

**B-Scan (Brightness Mode):**

In B-scan, a **linear or phased array of crystals** is used, and the beam is **electronically swept** across a 2-D plane in the body. Each crystal (or sub-group) sends a pulse and receives the echo. The processed data is displayed as a **two-dimensional brightness-modulated image** on a monitor.

The **brightness** of each pixel is proportional to the **echo intensity** (i.e., the amplitude of the reflected signal). The **position** of each pixel corresponds to the **anatomical location** of the reflecting interface in the scanned plane. Thus, a 2-D cross-sectional map of the body is produced in **real time**.

*Clinical use:* The **most widely used** mode — obstetric imaging, abdominal scanning, cardiac, vascular, and musculoskeletal studies.

**Key Differences:**

| Parameter | A-scan | B-scan |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output | 1-D spikes | 2-D brightness map |
| Probe | Single crystal | Multi-element array |
| Information | Depth + amplitude | Spatial + amplitude |
| Modern usage | Limited (ophthalmology) | Routine clinical practice |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 1]**

**(a) Explain the various medical applications of ultrasound scanning. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Ultrasound scanning is one of the most versatile diagnostic tools in modern medicine. Its major applications are:

**1. Obstetrics and Gynecology (Most Common Application):**
- Monitoring **fetal growth and development** throughout pregnancy.
- Determining **fetal position, gender, and gestational age**.
- Detecting **ectopic pregnancy, placenta previa, and multiple pregnancies**.
- **3D/4D ultrasound** allows detailed fetal facial features to be visualized.
- Critically, it uses **no ionizing radiation**, making it safe for the fetus.

**2. Cardiology (Echocardiography):**
- Visualizing **heart valve motion** and **chamber size**.
- Measuring **ejection fraction** (a key indicator of heart function).
- Detecting **congenital heart defects**, pericardial effusion, and cardiomyopathies.
- **Doppler ultrasound** measures **blood flow velocity** through the heart and major vessels.

**3. Abdominal Imaging:**
- Imaging of the **liver** (cirrhosis, tumors, cysts), **gallbladder** (gallstones), **kidneys** (stones, hydronephrosis), **pancreas**, and **spleen**.
- Detection of **abdominal aortic aneurysms**.

**4. Vascular Studies:**
- **Doppler ultrasound** detects **blood clots (DVT)**, **arterial stenosis**, and **varicose veins**.
- **Carotid Doppler** screens for stroke risk.

**5. Musculoskeletal Imaging:**
- Detection of **tendon tears** (e.g., rotator cuff), **muscle strains**, **ligament injuries**, and **joint effusions**.

**6. Ophthalmology:**
- A-scan is used to measure the **axial length of the eye** before cataract surgery.
- B-scan detects **retinal detachment, vitreous hemorrhage, and intraocular tumors**.

**7. Emergency and Critical Care:**
- **FAST scan** (Focused Assessment with Sonography for Trauma) rapidly detects **internal bleeding** in trauma patients.
- Bedside ultrasound for **pneumothorax, pleural effusion, and cardiac tamponade**.

**8. Guided Procedures:**
- **Real-time ultrasound guidance** for needle biopsy, amniocentesis, and central venous catheter placement — greatly improving accuracy and safety.

**9. Therapeutic Applications:**
- **Physiotherapy:** Deep tissue heating for pain relief and muscle relaxation (1–3 MHz).
- **Lithotripsy:** Focused high-intensity ultrasound **breaks kidney stones** without surgery.
- **HIFU (High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound):** Used for **non-invasive tumor ablation** in the uterus (fibroids) and prostate.

**(b) Discuss the safety considerations, limitations, and biological effects of diagnostic ultrasound. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Safety Considerations and Biological Effects:**

Although diagnostic ultrasound is generally considered **safe** (no ionizing radiation), it does deposit **mechanical energy** in tissue, which can produce two main bioeffects:

**1. Thermal Effects (Heating):**
- Ultrasound energy is absorbed by tissue and converted to **heat**.
- The **Thermal Index (TI)** is displayed on the scanner to estimate the temperature rise: $TI = \dfrac{W_{\text{deg}}}{W_{\text{deg,1°C}}}$, where $W_{\text{deg}}$ is the acoustic power and $W_{\text{deg,1°C}}$ is the power required to raise tissue temperature by 1°C.
- A TI > 1 requires caution, especially in obstetric and fetal imaging.
- The **Mechanical Index (MI)** estimates the risk of non-thermal bioeffects (cavitation).

**2. Mechanical Effects (Cavitation):**
- High-intensity ultrasound can cause **microbubbles** in tissue/fluid to **oscillate and collapse** violently — a process called **cavitation**.
- This can produce **localized shock waves** and **free radicals** that may damage cells.
- The **Mechanical Index** $MI = \dfrac{P_{\text{neg}}}{\sqrt{f}}$ (where $P_{\text{neg}}$ is the peak negative pressure in MPa and $f$ is the frequency in MHz) estimates cavitation risk. MI > 0.5 raises concern for gas-containing tissues (lung, bowel).

**3. The ALARA Principle:**
The FDA and WHO recommend the **ALARA** principle — **"As Low As Reasonably Achievable"** — meaning the lowest acoustic output that produces a clinically useful image should be used.

**Limitations of Ultrasound:**

| Limitation | Reason |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Cannot image through bone or air** | Huge acoustic impedance mismatch — total reflection. |
| **Operator-dependent** | Image quality varies with skill and experience. |
| **Limited penetration at high frequency** | High-frequency waves attenuate rapidly. |
| **Obesity reduces image quality** | Excess fat scatters and attenuates the beam. |
| **Bowel gas creates artifacts** | Gas reflects ultrasound chaotically. |
| **Cannot image the lungs effectively** | Air-tissue interface is too reflective. |
| **Lower resolution than CT/MRI** | Wavelength limitation (~0.1–0.5 mm). |

**Contraindications and Cautions:**
- Avoid prolonged exposure of the **fetus** in the first trimester (unless medically indicated).
- Avoid direct application over the **eye** (risk of thermal damage to the lens).
- Use caution in patients with **contrast agents** (microbubbles) due to cavitation risk.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the depth formula.** The most common error is writing $d = c \cdot t$ instead of $d = ct/2$. Remember: the pulse travels DOWN and BACK, so the total path length is $2d$. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 2. **Confusing acoustic impedance (Z) with electrical impedance.** Acoustic impedance is $Z = \rho c$ (in Rayl), not $Z = V/I$ (in Ohms). **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Writing "ultrasound has no biological effects."** This is **wrong** for KTU 2024 standards. Ultrasound has **thermal** and **mechanical (cavitation)** bioeffects, and the ALARA principle must be mentioned. **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Not explaining WHY a coupling gel is needed.** Simply stating "gel is used" is insufficient. You must explain that it overcomes the **impedance mismatch between air and skin** (Z difference: 0.0004 vs 1.63 MRayl). **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Confusing A-scan, B-scan, and M-scan axes.** In A-scan and M-scan, the **Y-axis is depth** and the **X-axis is time** (in M-mode) or time-base. In B-scan, both X and Y axes are **spatial**. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Not stating the speed of ultrasound in soft tissue as 1540 m/s** when solving numerical problems. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention the role of the backing material** in a transducer — it is **not just structural**; it specifically **damps the crystal to shorten pulse duration and improve axial resolution**. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 8. **In B-scan diagrams, not showing a multi-element array** (linear or phased) — drawing a single crystal loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Ultrasound Scanning (Qualitative)**

- ⭐ **Ultrasound frequency range:** Diagnostic 1–15 MHz; Therapeutic 1–3 MHz; Surgical/HIFU higher.
- ⭐ **Speed in soft tissue:** $c = 1540$ m/s (assumed in all clinical calculations).
- ⭐ **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$, in Rayl (kg m⁻² s⁻¹).
- ⭐ **Depth formula:** $d = ct/2$ (NEVER forget the factor of 2).
- ⭐ **Reflection coefficient:** $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ (intensity-based).
- ⭐ **Transmission coefficient:** $T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ and $R + T = 1$.
- ⭐ **Attenuation:** Exponential decay $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$; rule of thumb: 1 dB/cm/MHz.
- ⭐ **Axial resolution:** $\Delta x = c/(2f)$ — better at higher frequency.
- ⭐ **Frequency vs penetration trade-off:** Higher $f$ → better resolution, less penetration.
- ⭐ **Piezoelectric effect:** Discovered by the Curie brothers (1880).
- ⭐ **Common piezoelectric materials:** PZT (most common), PVDF, Quartz, Rochelle salt, Tourmaline.
- ⭐ **Transducer construction (in order, back to front):** Backing → Crystal → Matching Layer → Lens.
- ⭐ **Backing material:** Damping block; **shortens pulse** → improves axial resolution.
- ⭐ **Matching layer:** λ/4 thick; **reduces front-face reflection** → improves transmission.
- ⭐ **Coupling gel:** Overcomes air-skin impedance mismatch; Z ≈ 1.5 MRayl.
- ⭐ **A-mode:** 1-D spikes; amplitude vs time; used in ophthalmology.
- ⭐ **B-mode:** 2-D brightness image; the standard clinical mode.
- ⭐ **M-mode:** Motion mode; 1-D trace vs time; used in echocardiography.
- ⭐ **Bioeffects:** Thermal (heating) and Mechanical (cavitation).
- ⭐ **ALARA principle:** As Low As Reasonably Achievable.
- ⭐ **Thermal Index (TI) and Mechanical Index (MI):** Safety indicators displayed on scanner.
- ⭐ **Major applications:** Obstetrics, Cardiology, Abdominal, Vascular, Musculoskeletal, Emergency (FAST scan), Guided procedures, Lithotripsy, HIFU.
- ⭐ **Limitations:** Cannot image through bone/air; operator-dependent; obesity reduces quality; bowel gas artifacts.
- ⭐ **Doppler ultrasound:** Measures blood flow velocity using frequency shift of reflected echoes.
- ⭐ **Curie temperature:** Above this temperature, piezoelectric materials lose their piezoelectric property (depolarization).
